This platform approach enables rapid development and scalability across diseases.

Lecture 12: Case — Moderna.q: Moderna's IPO raised approximately:
options: $600 million
$7.5 billion — one of the largest biotech IPOs in history
$100 million
$2 billion
Lecture 12: Case — Moderna.explanation: Moderna's IPO raised approximately $7.5 billion, making it one of the largest biotech IPOs in history. Combined with over $5 billion in prior funding and $955M from BARDA, Moderna secured massive capital to fund its mRNA platform development.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: Nokia dismissed touchscreens, Kodak invented the digital camera but didn't commercialize it, and AT&T invented VOIP compression but was slow to adopt it. What do all three cases have in common?
options: All three firms lacked the technical capability to develop the new technology
All three firms were victims of competence traps — their existing success made them unable or unwilling to shift to the new paradigm
All three firms were destroyed by patent trolls
All three firms were outspent on R&D by new entrants
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: None of these firms lacked technical skill. They were trapped by existing incentives, sunk investments, organizational politics, and the rational logic of serving current customers. This is the core pattern of incumbent failure.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: A startup develops a new battery technology. It works, but the energy density is currently lower than lithium-ion. However, it improves 40% per year vs lithium-ion's 8%. An incumbent battery maker surveys its customers, who say they prefer lithium-ion. What concept best predicts what happens next?
options: The Bass diffusion model predicts the startup will fail
Christensen's disruptive innovation — the startup will serve niche markets, improve, and eventually take the mainstream
Henderson and Clark's architectural innovation — the components will stay the same
Barras's reverse product cycle — services will adopt it first
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Classic Christensen disruption: the new tech starts inferior on current dimensions, the incumbent listens to mainstream customers who reject it, the entrant enters niche markets, improves faster, and moves upmarket.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: EMI invented the CAT scanner but GE captured the profits. Moderna invented the mRNA vaccine and captured massive profits. What key difference explains why Moderna succeeded where EMI failed?
options: Moderna had better scientists
Moderna owned specialized complementary assets (manufacturing, digital operations, vertical integration) while EMI lacked them
GE was a larger company than any of Moderna's competitors
Moderna had stronger patents than EMI
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: In Teece's framework: EMI had weak appropriability AND lacked complementary assets (sales, service, hospital relationships) that GE had. Moderna invested in vertical integration ($125M manufacturing plant in 2018) and 'born digital' operations, giving it both the innovation AND the complementary assets.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: A VC fund invests $100M across 20 startups. 13 return less than 1x. 6 return 1-5x. 1 returns 50x. This pattern is called:
options: Adverse selection
Moral hazard
Skewness in outcomes
The hurdle rate problem
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Skewness = proportionally large ROI from a small part of the portfolio. Most VC investments fail, but the rare massive winner (50x) drives overall fund returns. This is the defining characteristic of VC.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: An entrepreneur knows her technology has a 70% chance of failing but presents only positive data to investors. The investors, lacking technical expertise, fund the project. This is an example of:
options: Moral hazard
Adverse selection
Skewness in outcomes
The crossing the chasm problem
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Adverse selection = the entrepreneur knows more about the innovation than the investor (information asymmetry). The investor cannot accurately assess the true risk. Moral hazard would be if the entrepreneur took excessive risk AFTER receiving the funding.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: After receiving VC funding, an entrepreneur uses the money to hire friends, buy expensive office furniture, and pursue a risky pivot without consulting the board. This is an example of:
options: Adverse selection
Moral hazard
Skewness in outcomes
Creative destruction
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Moral hazard = taking excessive risk without bearing the consequences. The entrepreneur is spending other people's money recklessly. Adverse selection is about pre-investment information asymmetry; moral hazard is about post-investment behavior.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: Coca-Cola's formula has been protected for over 130 years. If Coca-Cola had patented it instead, protection would have expired after:
options: 10 years
20 years
50 years
Life of the inventor + 70 years
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Patents last 20 years (utility). By choosing trade secrecy instead, Coca-Cola gets potentially indefinite protection — as long as the secret isn't leaked or independently discovered. This is why secrecy is sometimes preferred.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: A pharma company develops a blockbuster drug. Which IP protection mechanism would be MOST effective, according to industry data?
options: Trade secrecy — because drugs are hard to reverse-engineer
Copyright — because the chemical formula is creative work
Patents — drug/chemical industries rate patent effectiveness highest (6.5/7)
Trademarks — because brand recognition drives drug sales
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Patents are most effective in drugs (6.5/7) and organic chemicals (6.1/7). In contrast, patents are least effective in computers (3.4/7). Drugs are hard to develop but easy to copy once the formula is known, so patent protection is critical.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: A technology has reached the flat part of its S-curve. A manager considers switching to a new technology but customers resist because the new product requires them to change infrastructure. This illustrates a limitation of S-curves as a management tool because:
options: S-curves cannot be plotted accurately
S-curves tell you change may be needed but don't account for customer switching costs, complementary technologies, or organizational capabilities
S-curves only work for manufacturing industries
The manager should ignore customer feedback entirely
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Shane identifies several S-curve limitations: they don't tell you WHEN to switch, don't incorporate customer readiness, complementary technologies, firm capabilities, or expected diffusion rates. The decision is more complex than just reading the curve.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: Google's '20% time' policy (which produced Gmail) is an example of:
options: External corporate venturing
Organizational slack — giving employees time to explore non-core activities
Disruptive innovation from a new entrant
Crossing the chasm strategy
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Organizational slack = providing employees time and resources to explore beyond their assigned work. Gmail was famously created during Google's policy of allowing engineers to spend 20% of their time on side projects.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: Apple's functional structure works for innovation because:
options: Each product division can optimize its own P&L independently
Deep technical expertise is concentrated by discipline, enabling cross-functional collaboration on integrated products
It minimizes the number of managers needed
It was copied from GM's multi-divisional structure
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Apple groups experts by function (design, engineering, marketing) not by product. This concentrates deep expertise and enables the tight cross-functional collaboration needed for integrated products like the iPhone. The GM/DuPont multi-divisional model optimizes for accountability, not innovation.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: In the Selden vs Ford case, what is the key strategic lesson?
options: Patents always protect innovators effectively
Broad patents can be used to control an entire industry, but process innovation can circumvent them
Trade secrets are always better than patents
The first person to file a patent always wins
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Selden held a broad patent on the gasoline automobile and used it to sue competitors. Ford fought it and won on appeal (he used a different engine type). Lesson: patents can be used strategically/abusively, and process innovation can be a way around them.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: A copyright protects creative works for the author's life plus 70 years. Which of the following CANNOT be copyrighted?
options: A novel about technology strategy
A musical composition about innovation
An idea for a new type of smartphone
Computer software source code
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Copyright protects tangible creative expressions, not ideas. You cannot copyright an idea, a method, a principle, or a title. You CAN copyright the specific code, text, or artistic work that expresses the idea.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: Moderna had spent a decade on mRNA R&D and built a $125M manufacturing plant before COVID-19 hit. This pre-pandemic investment is best understood as:
options: A sunk cost that should have been written off
Building specialized complementary assets and real options that enabled rapid response when opportunity arose
An example of moral hazard with investor money
A failed strategy since no vaccines had reached market
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Moderna's decade of R&D and manufacturing investment created both specialized complementary assets (Teece) and real options (flexibility to act quickly). When COVID hit, these pre-built capabilities allowed Moderna to design a vaccine in 2 days and produce it in 25 days.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: Nokia had technological leadership, global reach, strong marketing, and operational excellence — yet failed. Which of the following best explains why strong capabilities can become a liability?
options: Strong capabilities always lead to complacency
Capabilities become core rigidities when the competitive landscape shifts and the firm's strengths become irrelevant or actively harmful to adaptation
Nokia's capabilities were never actually strong
Only startups can innovate; large firms always fail
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Core competencies become core rigidities when the environment changes. Nokia's hardware expertise, Symbian OS, and matrix organization — all former strengths — became obstacles when the market shifted to software ecosystems and touchscreen smartphones.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: An incumbent is considering a new technology. It is radical, builds on fundamentally different knowledge, but the firm's existing sales force, distribution network, and brand are still valuable. According to Tushman, this technology is:
options: Competence-destroying because the knowledge base changes
Competence-enhancing because existing complementary assets remain useful
Both — the technology is competence-destroying on the knowledge dimension but the firm may still succeed if it can acquire new technical skills while leveraging existing assets
Architectural innovation because only the linkages change
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: This is a nuanced case. The technology itself is competence-destroying (new knowledge needed), but the firm has valuable complementary assets. Success depends on whether the firm can build new technical capabilities while leveraging existing market assets — exactly what GE did with medical imaging transitions.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: The adoption S-curve shows slow early growth. A manager concludes the product will fail. What mistake is the manager making?
options: No mistake — slow early growth always predicts failure
Confusing the fluid phase with the specific phase
Failing to recognize that S-curves naturally start slowly — the product may still be in the innovator/early adopter phase before crossing the chasm
Using the wrong type of S-curve (should use technology S-curve instead)
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: S-curves are S-shaped precisely because early adoption is slow (only innovators and early adopters). The acceleration comes when the early majority enters. Judging a product based on slow early growth ignores the fundamental shape of the adoption curve.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: A firm's patent expires and revenue drops from $13B to $2B. This most closely matches which real example from the lectures?
options: Coca-Cola losing trade secret protection
Nokia losing market share to Apple
Pfizer's Lipitor after patent expiry
Ford vs Selden patent dispute
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Pfizer's Lipitor revenue dropped dramatically from ~$13B to ~$2B after patent expiry as generic competitors entered. This illustrates the 'patent cliff' and why appropriability matters — without legal protection, competitors capture value.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: According to the lecture's dictionary, what distinguishes 'adoption' from 'diffusion'?
options: Adoption is aggregate; diffusion is individual
Adoption is the individual customer decision to buy; diffusion is the aggregate rate of adoption across the market
Adoption applies to products; diffusion applies to services
They are interchangeable terms
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Adoption = the individual customer decision to buy. Diffusion = the rate of adoption of a new technology across the market. One is micro (individual), the other is macro (market-level).

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: A firm wants indefinite protection for its manufacturing process, which is extremely difficult to reverse-engineer and involves tacit knowledge held by a small team. What protection mechanism is best?
options: Patent — because it gives the strongest legal monopoly
Trade secrecy — because the process is hard to reverse-engineer, involves tacit knowledge, and secrecy has no expiration date
Copyright — because the process is a creative work
Trademark — because it protects the firm's brand identity
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Trade secrecy is preferred when: few sources of information, product complexity, poorly understood process, presence of tacit knowledge. A patent would require disclosure and expire after 20 years. Secrecy can last indefinitely.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: P&G's Connect & Develop reduced R&D spending as a percentage of sales from 4.8% to 3.4%, while Moderna spent $4 billion on R&D in 2022 (estimated). Both are considered highly innovative. What concept explains how opposite R&D spending strategies can both succeed?
options: Higher R&D spending always produces more innovation

P&G leveraged external innovation sources (open innovation) while Moderna built deep internal capabilities (vertical integration) — the optimal strategy depends on the firm's innovation model and industry
Both firms were simply cutting costs to maximize short-term profits
Government subsidies made the spending levels irrelevant
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: P&G's open innovation model (Connect & Develop) taps external ideas and develops them internally, reducing the need for internal R&D spending. Moderna's 'born digital' model requires massive internal R&D because mRNA is a radical platform technology requiring deep proprietary expertise. Both approaches are valid — the right strategy depends on the nature of the innovation and the industry.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: GlaxoSmithKline decentralized its R&D into 6 centers of excellence and gave scientists stock options tied to drug targets. Google gave engineers 20% time for side projects. Both produced more innovation. These cases share which underlying principle?
options: Both are examples of external corporate venturing (ECV)
Both illustrate that aligning incentives and providing organizational slack encourages exploration, regardless of whether the structure is centralized or decentralized
Both firms adopted Apple's functional structure
Both firms reduced total R&D spending to become more efficient
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: GSK changed structure (decentralization) AND incentives (stock options). Google provided slack (20% time). The common thread: both gave employees autonomy, resources, and motivation to explore beyond immediate duties. This connects Shane's organizational slack concept with the exploration-exploitation balance.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: Eli Lilly invested $1 billion in Humulin (100% pure insulin) but could not sustain a 25% price premium. Moderna invested over $5 billion before COVID-19 with no products on the market, then earned $17.6 billion in 2021. What distinguishes a costly technological bet that fails commercially from one that succeeds?
options: Lilly's technology was inferior to Moderna's

Lilly overshot market needs on a dimension customers no longer valued (purity), while Moderna built a platform that addressed an urgent unmet need when it arose — commercial success requires alignment between technological capability and market demand
Moderna simply had better marketing than Lilly
Lilly failed because it did not patent Humulin
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Lilly's purity trajectory overshot market needs (Christensen's overshoot concept) — customers were satisfied with existing purity levels. Moderna's mRNA platform created a real option that became enormously valuable when COVID-19 created unprecedented demand. The lesson: technology must align with what the market actually needs, and timing matters enormously.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: Apple uses a centralized functional structure and maintains a single companywide P&L. Shane Ch 16 lists 'encourages radical innovation' as a benefit of centralization. Yet Apple also exhibits characteristics Shane associates with organic structures. This combination suggests:
options: Shane's framework is incorrect and should be discarded

Apple benefits from centralized decision-making for large-scale integration while using organic elements (cross-functional debate, collaborative culture, immersion in details) to foster creativity — firms can combine structural dimensions strategically
Apple is actually a decentralized company despite having a functional structure
Centralization and organic structure are always found together
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: Shane's three dimensions (centralization, organicity, slack) are independent — a firm can be centralized AND organic. Apple centralizes strategic decision-making (one P&L, functional structure) while maintaining organic elements: cross-functional debate, non-hierarchical collaboration, 'good mess' culture. This hybrid approach enables both radical innovation and tight product integration.

Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.q: A firm acquires a startup (ECV), but the startup's innovative culture clashes with the parent company's mechanistic structure, and key employees leave. This outcome connects to which combination of concepts from the course?
options: Adverse selection and moral hazard in VC financing
The tension between organic and mechanistic structures (Shane Ch 16) combined with the risk that corporate venturing can interfere with or be undermined by ongoing operations
The Bass diffusion model and crossing the chasm
Patent trolling and non-practicing entity behavior
Hard Mode: Tricky Connections.explanation: This scenario combines multiple course concepts: (1) organic vs. mechanistic structures — startups are typically organic, acquirers often mechanistic; (2) corporate venturing risks — Shane notes that CV can cause conflict with ongoing operations; (3) the exploration-exploitation tension — the acquiring firm's exploitation-oriented structure can kill the startup's exploration culture. This is why many acquisitions fail to deliver expected innovation.

Mock Exam Style.q: The discovery of penicillin by Alexander Fleming is best classified as:
options: A disruptive innovation
An accidental technological innovation
A business model innovation
A planned radical innovation
Mock Exam Style.explanation: Penicillin was discovered accidentally when Fleming noticed mold killing bacteria. It is a technological innovation (a new product/process based on scientific knowledge), not a disruptive innovation (which refers to Christensen's specific market dynamic).

Mock Exam Style.q: Corporate venturing can interfere with a firm's ongoing operations.
options: True
False
Mock Exam Style.explanation: True. Corporate venturing (internal startups, new business units) can compete for resources, management attention, and talent with the firm's core business, potentially interfering with ongoing operations.

Mock Exam Style.q: There exists a common international copyright law that protects creative works uniformly across all countries.
options: True
False
Mock Exam Style.explanation: False. There is no single international copyright law. While treaties like the Berne Convention establish minimum standards, each country has its own copyright legislation. Protection must be enforced country by country.

Mock Exam Style.q: A patent's 'working requirement' means:
options: The inventor must demonstrate the patent works in a laboratory
The patent holder must manufacture the patented product in the country where the patent is granted
The patent must be renewed annually to remain in force
The inventor must be currently employed to hold the patent
Mock Exam Style.explanation: The working requirement obliges the patent holder to manufacture or use the patented invention in the country where the patent was granted. Failure to 'work' the patent can result in compulsory licensing or revocation in some jurisdictions.

Mock Exam Style.q: R&D intensity (R&D spending as a percentage of sales) is generally higher for small firms than for large firms.
options: True
False
Mock Exam Style.explanation: True. Small firms tend to have higher R&D intensity because they spend a larger proportion of their revenue on R&D, even though large firms spend more in absolute terms. Small firms often depend on innovation for competitive survival.

Mock Exam Style.q: Locking into particular strategies early is a benefit of investing heavily in R&D.
options: True
False
Mock Exam Style.explanation: False. Locking into strategies is a COST of R&D investment, not a benefit. Heavy R&D commitments can reduce strategic flexibility by creating path dependencies, sunk costs, and organizational inertia around chosen technological directions.

Mock Exam Style.q: In marketing strategy, a 'horizontal' strategy refers to:
options: Selling a single product line within one industry
Marketing the same technology or product across multiple industries
Targeting only the bottom of the market
Focusing exclusively on B2B customers
Mock Exam Style.explanation: A horizontal marketing strategy involves selling the same product or technology across multiple industries (broad application). A vertical strategy, by contrast, focuses on serving a single industry deeply with specialized solutions.

Mock Exam Style.q: A key advantage of decentralization in organizational structure is that it:
options: Reduces the total number of employees needed
Ensures consistent decision-making across all units
Facilitates innovation in noncore areas of the business
Eliminates the need for middle management
Mock Exam Style.explanation: Decentralization pushes decision-making authority to lower levels and business units. This enables experimentation and innovation in noncore areas, as local managers can pursue opportunities without waiting for central approval.

Mock Exam Style.q: Moderna's COVID-19 vaccine delivery relied on lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) as a key component of its technology platform.
options: True
False
Mock Exam Style.explanation: True. Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) are an essential part of Moderna's mRNA delivery system. They protect the fragile mRNA molecules and help deliver them into human cells. LNPs are a core component of Moderna's technology model.

Mock Exam Style.q: The demand for spin-off companies has been increasing primarily because of:
options: Government subsidies for all new businesses
Declining interest in venture capital
Growth in high-tech startups and entrepreneurial ecosystems
A reduction in patent filing costs
Mock Exam Style.explanation: The growth in high-tech startups, entrepreneurial ecosystems, and the increasing importance of specialized technology firms have driven rising demand for spin-offs as a way to commercialize innovations outside the parent organization.

Mock Exam Style.q: Which of the following would NOT be considered a source of innovation?
options: Universities and government-funded research
Lead users who modify products for their own needs
A firm's existing accounting procedures
Private, nonprofit organizations conducting basic research
Mock Exam Style.explanation: Sources of innovation include universities, government labs, private nonprofits, lead users, and firms' R&D departments. Routine accounting procedures are operational activities, not sources of innovation.

Mock Exam Style.q: An organic organizational structure is characterized by:
options: Rigid hierarchy, formal rules, and narrow spans of control
Flat hierarchy, flexible roles, and decentralized decision-making
A single CEO making all strategic decisions
Strict separation of functional departments with no cross-communication
Mock Exam Style.explanation: Organic structures feature flat hierarchies, flexible and broadly defined roles, lateral communication, and decentralized authority. They are suited for dynamic environments requiring innovation. Mechanistic structures are the opposite: rigid, hierarchical, and formalized.

Mock Exam Style.q: A mechanistic organizational structure is most appropriate when:
options: The environment is highly dynamic and uncertain
The firm needs to maximize radical innovation output
The environment is stable and tasks are routine and well-understood
The firm is a small startup with fewer than 10 employees
Mock Exam Style.explanation: Mechanistic structures work best in stable, predictable environments where efficiency and consistency are priorities. Their rigid hierarchies and formalized procedures are well-suited for routine tasks but poorly suited for innovation.

Mock Exam Style.q: In a vertical marketing strategy, a firm:
options: Sells the same product to customers in many different industries
Focuses on serving one specific industry with tailored products and services
Only targets consumers in the top income bracket
Distributes products through horizontal channel partners
Mock Exam Style.explanation: A vertical marketing strategy means focusing deeply on one industry, providing specialized solutions tailored to that industry's needs. This contrasts with horizontal strategies that cut across multiple industries.

Mock Exam Style.q: According to the lecture material, which of the following is true about patent effectiveness across industries?
options: Patents are equally effective in all industries
Patents are most effective in the computer and electronics industries
Patents are most effective in pharmaceuticals and chemicals
Patents have become obsolete due to open-source movements
Mock Exam Style.explanation: Survey data shows patents are most effective in drugs (6.5/7) and organic chemicals (6.1/7), because products are easy to copy but hard to develop. Patents are least effective in computers (3.4/7) where products involve many components and workarounds exist.

Mock Exam Style.q: A centralized organizational structure is best suited for:
options: Encouraging autonomous innovation across business units
Maintaining tight control and consistency in core operations
Allowing rapid local adaptation to market changes
Fostering experimentation in noncore areas
Mock Exam Style.explanation: Centralized structures concentrate decision-making at the top, which ensures consistency and control over core operations. However, this comes at the cost of slower response to local conditions and less innovation in noncore areas.

Mock Exam Style.q: The Bass diffusion model's coefficient 'p' represents:
options: The rate at which imitators adopt based on word-of-mouth
The coefficient of innovation — the rate of adoption independent of prior adopters
The total market potential for a product
The price elasticity of demand
Mock Exam Style.explanation: In the Bass model, 'p' is the coefficient of innovation (external influence), representing the probability of adoption by someone not yet influenced by existing adopters. 'q' is the coefficient of imitation (internal/word-of-mouth influence).

Mock Exam Style.q: Henderson and Clark's framework defines 'architectural innovation' as:
options: A breakthrough in core component technology
A change in how components are linked together, while individual components remain largely unchanged
An innovation that creates an entirely new market category
Incremental improvements to existing products
Mock Exam Style.explanation: Architectural innovation reconfigures the linkages between components (the architecture) while the core component knowledge stays the same. This is dangerous for incumbents because it makes their organizational knowledge of how components fit together obsolete.

Mock Exam Style.q: A firm that protects its innovation primarily through lead time advantage and learning curve effects is relying on:
options: Formal IP protection mechanisms
Informal (strategic) appropriability mechanisms
Government regulation
Franchise agreements
Mock Exam Style.explanation: Lead time, learning curves, and complementary assets are informal or strategic appropriability mechanisms. Formal mechanisms include patents, copyrights, and trademarks. Research shows managers in most industries rate informal mechanisms as more effective than formal ones.

Mock Exam Style.q: Schumpeter's concept of 'creative destruction' describes:
options: The legal process of dissolving a bankrupt firm
The process by which new innovations render existing products, firms, and industries obsolete
A deliberate corporate strategy to destroy competitor products
The physical destruction of outdated manufacturing equipment
Mock Exam Style.explanation: Creative destruction is Schumpeter's concept that innovation drives economic progress by continuously destroying old products, methods, and firms while creating new ones. It is the fundamental engine of capitalism in Schumpeter's view.

Mock Exam Style.q: Moderna designed its COVID-19 vaccine candidate in just 2 days. This was possible primarily because:
options: The virus was simple to analyze
Moderna had a decade of prior mRNA R&D and a modular platform technology
The government provided the vaccine design
Moderna copied the design from a competitor
Mock Exam Style.explanation: Moderna's ability to design the vaccine in 2 days was the result of over a decade of foundational mRNA research and a platform technology that allowed rapid adaptation to new targets. The prior investment in R&D and specialized assets was the critical enabler.

Mock Exam Style.q: Which of the following is NOT an advantage of the multi-divisional (M-form) organizational structure?
options: Each division can be evaluated as a separate profit center
Divisions can tailor strategies to their specific markets
It maximizes deep functional expertise by grouping specialists together
It reduces information overload at headquarters
Mock Exam Style.explanation: Maximizing deep functional expertise by grouping specialists together is an advantage of the functional (U-form) structure, not the multi-divisional (M-form). The M-form groups by product or market, which can duplicate functional roles across divisions.

Mock Exam Style.q: The 'Valley of Death' in innovation financing refers to:
options: The period when a startup's stock price crashes
The funding gap between early-stage research and commercial revenue, where many innovations fail
The decline phase of a product's lifecycle
The point at which venture capitalists exit their investment
Mock Exam Style.explanation: The Valley of Death is the critical gap between initial research/development funding and the generation of commercial revenue. Many innovations die here because they need significant capital to scale but have not yet proven market viability.

Mock Exam Style.q: A copyright protects the expression of ideas, not the ideas themselves.
options: True
False
Mock Exam Style.explanation: True. Copyright protects the specific tangible expression of an idea (the actual text, code, music, artwork) but does not protect the underlying idea, concept, method, or principle itself.

Mock Exam Style.q: According to the course material, which factor is most important for crossing Geoffrey Moore's 'chasm' between early adopters and the early majority?
options: Lowering the price as much as possible
Targeting a specific niche beachhead market and delivering a whole product solution
Spending heavily on mass-market advertising
Expanding to as many market segments as possible simultaneously
Mock Exam Style.explanation: Moore argues that crossing the chasm requires focusing on a specific niche segment (beachhead) and delivering a complete, turnkey solution (whole product) that meets the pragmatic needs of the early majority, who require proven, reliable solutions.

Exam Simulator.q: According to the HBR article on Apple, what is the PRIMARY reason Apple uses a functional (U-form) structure instead of a multi-divisional (M-form) structure?
options: It reduces the total number of senior executives needed to run the company
It ensures that people with the deepest expertise in a domain make the key decisions in that domain
It allows each product line to operate as an independent profit center
It eliminates conflicts between hardware and software engineering teams
Exam Simulator.explanation: The Apple HBR article emphasizes that Apple's functional structure aligns decision-making authority with deep domain expertise. Experts — not general managers — make key decisions. The M-form gives product-line GMs P&L responsibility, which Apple deliberately avoids because GMs may prioritize short-term profits over long-term innovation.

Exam Simulator.q: According to the course material on venture capital, what percentage of VC investments is responsible for approximately 97% of all returns across the entire VC asset class?
Exam Simulator.explanation: The lecture states that 0.4% of VC investments account for 97% of returns for the entire asset class. This extreme skewness is a defining characteristic of VC. 4.3% is the share of cost going to >10x investments (which generate 61.7% of value). 2% is the management fee. 10% is a distractor.

Exam Simulator.q: In the Eli Lilly insulin case, what was the main strategic mistake Lilly's marketers made?
options: They failed to invest in recombinant DNA technology
They assumed customers would pay more for purer insulin when most patients were satisfied with existing purity levels
They refused to enter the European market
They chose trade secrecy over patents for their insulin formulation
Exam Simulator.explanation: Lilly followed a technological trajectory that 'surpassed' market needs. Their marketers assumed patients would value and pay a premium for increasingly pure (human) insulin, but most diabetics were well-served by existing animal-based insulin. This is a classic case of overshooting — pushing technical performance beyond what customers actually need.

Exam Simulator.q: Which of the following is NOT a lesson from the Selden vs. Ford patent case?
options: Patents can be used strategically and even abusively to extract rents
Process innovation can help circumvent existing patents
A patent holder must always be the original inventor of the underlying technology
Delaying patent issuance can be used as a strategic tool
Exam Simulator.explanation: Selden was a patent attorney, not the original inventor of the combustion engine (Brayton, Daimler, Benz, and Otto preceded him). He filed a broad patent and delayed issuance until the automobile industry grew. The case shows patents can be held by non-inventors and used abusively. Ford won on appeal because he used a different engine type — demonstrating process innovation circumventing patents.

Exam Simulator.q: In Rogers' adopter categories, the Late Majority is best characterized by:
options: They adopt because of economic necessity or social pressure, and are skeptical of new technologies
They are visionaries who see strategic advantage in being first
They are comfortable with risk and enjoy experimenting with unproven technologies
They actively resist all new technology and never adopt voluntarily
Exam Simulator.explanation: The Late Majority (one standard deviation after the mean) adopts out of economic necessity or peer pressure, not enthusiasm. They are skeptical and cautious. Early Adopters are the visionaries (B). Innovators enjoy experimenting (C). Laggards resist most strongly but may eventually adopt (D overstates by saying 'never').

Exam Simulator.q: Knightian uncertainty, as discussed in the course, differs from risk because:
options: Risk can be insured against, but uncertainty involves unknown probabilities that cannot be quantified
Uncertainty is always lower than risk in innovation projects
Risk applies only to financial markets while uncertainty applies only to technology
Knightian uncertainty can be eliminated through real options analysis
Exam Simulator.explanation: Frank Knight distinguished risk (measurable, insurable probability) from uncertainty (immeasurable, unknown probabilities). Innovation projects often face Knightian uncertainty because outcomes cannot be predicted with known probability distributions. Real options help MANAGE uncertainty but cannot eliminate it.

Exam Simulator.q: Which of the following is NOT a stated advantage of P&G's Connect and Develop model in the HBR article?
options: It reduces internal R&D costs by leveraging external innovation
It accelerates time-to-market by tapping into ideas that are already partially developed
It guarantees higher quality innovations than purely internal R&D
It expands the pool of potential innovations beyond the firm's own researchers
Exam Simulator.explanation: Connect and Develop offers access to more ideas (D), lower costs (A), and faster development (B). However, the article does NOT claim it guarantees higher quality — in fact, a con is that external innovations may require significant adaptation and quality control. The model supplements internal R&D rather than guaranteeing superiority.

Exam Simulator.q: Maria discovers that mixing two common household chemicals creates a powerful new cleaning agent. She wants to patent it. Under patent law, which category would this invention fall into?
options: A design patent for an original ornamental design
A utility patent for a new composition of matter
It cannot be patented because it uses existing natural substances
A plant patent for a new organic compound
Exam Simulator.explanation: A new chemical mixture (composition of matter / 'recipe') is patentable as a utility patent, provided it is useful, novel, and non-obvious. Design patents cover ornamental designs only. Plant patents cover asexually reproduced plants. The 'natural substances' exception applies to substances as found in nature, not to novel combinations created by humans.

Exam Simulator.q: An 'ambidextrous' organization is one that:
options: Has eliminated all mechanistic elements from its structure
Simultaneously pursues exploitation of existing capabilities and exploration of new opportunities
Outsources all R&D activities to external partners
Focuses exclusively on radical innovation at the expense of incremental improvement
Exam Simulator.explanation: Ambidexterity means balancing exploitation (refining and leveraging existing competencies for efficiency) with exploration (searching for new knowledge and opportunities). Neither pure exploration nor pure exploitation is optimal — firms must do both simultaneously, which is organizationally difficult.

Exam Simulator.q: A startup develops a working prototype of a flying taxi that can carry two passengers across a city. Test flights succeed, but the cost per ride is 50x that of a regular taxi and noise regulations limit where it can operate. This situation best illustrates:
options: Disruptive innovation entering from the low end of the market
Technical feasibility without commercial viability
A failure of the technology trajectory to advance
Network externalities preventing adoption
Exam Simulator.explanation: The flying taxi works (technically feasible) but is commercially unviable due to extreme costs and regulatory constraints. This parallels the Concorde and metaverse examples from the course: being able to build something does not mean the market will support it. Disruptive innovation (A) enters from the low end — this enters from the high end.

Exam Simulator.q: When Moderna is described as a 'born digital' company, this means:
options: Moderna only sells its products through digital channels
Moderna was founded as a software company before pivoting to biotech
Digital technologies like AI, cloud computing, and automation were integrated into Moderna's operations from the very beginning — not added later
Moderna's mRNA vaccines are delivered digitally to patients
Exam Simulator.explanation: Born digital means Moderna was built from day one with digital infrastructure — cloud computing, AI/ML, automation, and seamless data sharing — embedded throughout its operations. This contrasts with traditional pharma companies that digitize legacy processes. Moderna did not sell digitally (A), was not a software company (B), and vaccines are physical products (D).

Exam Simulator.q: A pharmaceutical company's lead scientist retires, and despite thorough documentation, the company struggles to replicate her most successful experimental techniques. This difficulty is best explained by:
options: Adverse selection in the labor market
The presence of tacit knowledge that cannot be fully codified
Weak patent protection in the pharmaceutical industry
The company's failure to file for copyright on the techniques
Exam Simulator.explanation: Tacit knowledge is knowledge that is difficult or impossible to transfer through written documentation — it is learned through experience and practice. This is a key source of appropriability because competitors (or successors) cannot easily replicate it. Patent protection in pharma is actually strong (C is wrong). Copyright protects expression, not techniques (D).

Exam Simulator.q: Nokia's failure to successfully transition to smartphones is MOST accurately attributed to:
options: A lack of financial resources to invest in smartphone R&D
Organizational inertia and a culture that could not shift from hardware-centric thinking to a software-ecosystem strategy
Being a new entrant that lacked brand recognition in mobile phones
Apple holding key patents that prevented Nokia from making touchscreen devices
Exam Simulator.explanation: Nokia was the dominant incumbent (not a new entrant — C is wrong) with ample resources (A is wrong). Nokia did not fail due to patents (D). The core issue was organizational inertia: Nokia's culture, processes, and leadership were optimized for hardware-focused feature phones and could not adapt to the software-ecosystem paradigm that Apple and Android introduced.

Exam Simulator.q: Schumpeterian rents are economic profits that arise from:
options: Government subsidies granted to innovative firms
Temporary monopoly power gained through innovation, before competitors imitate
Long-term cost advantages from economies of scale
Charging higher prices due to strong brand recognition
Exam Simulator.explanation: Schumpeterian rents are the above-normal profits an innovator earns during the period between introducing an innovation and the point when competitors successfully imitate. They are temporary by nature — driven by the innovator's head start, not by government support (A), scale (C), or branding (D).

Exam Simulator.q: A university spin-off has promising lab results for a new battery technology. Government grants funded the research, but the company now needs EUR 5 million to build a pilot production facility. No bank will lend to them and they are too early for most VCs. This gap is called:
options: The chasm between early adopters and early majority
Adverse selection
The Valley of Death
Creative destruction
Exam Simulator.explanation: The Valley of Death is the funding gap between early research (often grant-funded) and the point where a venture generates commercial revenue. Banks cannot lend (no collateral, uncertain cash flows) and VCs may consider it too early-stage. The chasm (A) refers to market adoption, not financing. Adverse selection (B) is information asymmetry. Creative destruction (D) is Schumpeter's concept of new innovations replacing old ones.

Exam Simulator.q: An organic organizational structure is characterized by centralized decision-making and rigid hierarchies that enable rapid innovation.
options: True
False
Exam Simulator.explanation: False. Organic structures are characterized by DECENTRALIZED decision-making and FLAT (not rigid) hierarchies. The statement swaps in traits of mechanistic structures. Organic structures enable innovation through flexibility, broad role definitions, and lateral communication — the opposite of centralized, rigid control.

Exam Simulator.q: Venture capital firms typically charge a management fee of approximately 2% per year and retain a carry of 20-30% of profits.
options: True
False
Exam Simulator.explanation: True. The standard VC fee structure is approximately 2% annual management fee plus 20-30% carry (the share of profits the VC firm keeps). LPs (limited partners) receive the remaining 70-80% of profits after the carry.

Exam Simulator.q: Copyright protection must be formally applied for through a national patent office before it takes effect.
options: True
False
Exam Simulator.explanation: False. Copyright is granted AUTOMATICALLY when a work is put into tangible form — no application is needed. It is PATENTS that require a formal application through a national patent office. This is a key distinction between the two forms of IP protection.

Exam Simulator.q: Diffusion refers to an individual customer's decision to purchase a new technology.
options: True
False
Exam Simulator.explanation: False. ADOPTION is the individual customer's decision to buy. DIFFUSION is the aggregate rate of adoption across a market. The statement swaps the two definitions — a common exam trap.

Exam Simulator.q: Unlike patents, trade secrecy can provide a potentially non-expiring monopoly, as long as the secret is maintained.
options: True
False
Exam Simulator.explanation: True. Patents expire after 20 years (utility), but trade secrecy has no fixed expiration. As long as the information remains secret, it continues to provide competitive advantage. Coca-Cola's formula has been a trade secret for over 130 years.

Exam Simulator.q: In Moderna's mRNA platform analogy, the mRNA sequence itself is compared to the 'hardware' of a smartphone.
options: True
False
Exam Simulator.explanation: False. In Moderna's platform analogy: lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) = hardware, mRNA = operating system (OS), and the specific vaccine (e.g., COVID-19) = the app. The mRNA is the OS, not the hardware.

Exam Simulator.q: Trademarks last for 20 years and cannot be renewed after expiry.
options: True
False
Exam Simulator.explanation: False. Trademarks last for 10 years (not 20) and CAN be renewed indefinitely as long as the mark remains in use. The 20-year figure is the duration of a utility patent — a common source of confusion.

Exam Simulator.q: The term 'metaverse' originated in the 2011 novel Ready Player One by Ernest Cline.
options: True
False
Exam Simulator.explanation: False. The term 'metaverse' originated in the 1992 novel Snow Crash by Neal Stephenson, not Ready Player One (2011). Ready Player One popularized VR themes but did not coin the term. Getting the year and source right matters for the exam.

Exam Simulator.q: The Connect and Develop model is designed to replace a firm's internal R&D entirely with external sourcing of innovation.
options: True
False
Exam Simulator.explanation: False. Connect and Develop SUPPLEMENTS internal R&D — it does not replace it. P&G still maintains strong internal R&D capabilities. The model works by connecting internal needs with external innovations, not by eliminating internal research. The article explicitly discusses this as open innovation, not outsourcing.

Exam Simulator.q: Socially complex knowledge — such as a firm's unique culture and team dynamics — is easy for competitors to replicate through reverse engineering.
options: True
False
Exam Simulator.explanation: False. Socially complex knowledge (organizational culture, interpersonal relationships, team dynamics) is inherently difficult to replicate because it is embedded in human interactions and institutional history. Unlike a product, it cannot be reverse-engineered. This is why socially complex knowledge is a source of sustained competitive advantage and high appropriability.

Hard Exam.q: According to Christensen, Raynor & McDonald (2015), why is Uber NOT considered a disruptive innovation?
options: Because Uber uses existing technology (smartphones) rather than inventing new technology
Because Uber launched in a well-served mainstream market with a superior product, the opposite direction of disruption
Because Uber did not achieve profitability within its first five years of operation
Because Uber's pricing model is identical to traditional taxi companies
Hard Exam.explanation: Christensen argues Uber is NOT disruptive because it launched in San Francisco (a well-served taxi market), targeted existing taxi users with a BETTER service, and built its position in the mainstream first — the exact OPPOSITE of disruption. Disruption requires starting in a low-end or new-market foothold and moving upmarket.

Hard Exam.q: What was the critical error in Eli Lilly's market research that led them to overinvest in Humulin?
options: They relied on focus groups of diabetic patients who exaggerated their willingness to pay
They weighted the opinions of endocrinologists most heavily, who represented the most demanding — not mainstream — customers
They failed to conduct any market research and relied solely on internal R&D assessments
They surveyed general practitioners who underestimated the clinical significance of purity improvements
Hard Exam.explanation: Lilly's marketers surveyed physicians and put the GREATEST WEIGHT on endocrinologists — the leading specialists. These were the most demanding, high-end customers who emphasized insulin resistance concerns. But the mainstream market (GPs, patients) was already satisfied with animal insulin at 10 ppm purity. Classic Christensen: listening to the most demanding customers leads to overshooting.

Hard Exam.q: In the VC outcome distribution, what percentage of total portfolio cost is allocated to investments returning greater than 10x, and what share of total value do they generate?
options: 10.1% of cost, generating 45.3% of value
4.3% of cost, generating 61.7% of value
6.8% of cost, generating 97.0% of value
0.4% of cost, generating 61.7% of value
Hard Exam.explanation: 4.3% of portfolio cost goes to >10x investments, but they generate 61.7% of total value. The 0.4% figure refers to VC investments responsible for 97% of returns for the ENTIRE asset class (not just one fund). The 63.2% figure is the cost going to <1x returns (failures).

Hard Exam.q: A company develops a product that functions identically to a patented device but uses aluminum instead of steel in its construction. Which statement is MOST accurate?
options: This qualifies for a new utility patent because aluminum has different material properties
This would likely NOT be patentable as mere substitution of materials is generally not considered non-obvious
This constitutes a design patent since the external appearance changed
This is automatically protected as a trade secret since it involves a manufacturing choice
Hard Exam.explanation: Simply substituting one known material for another (e.g., steel to aluminum) without producing unexpected results is generally considered obvious to a person skilled in the art and fails the non-obviousness requirement. Similarly, merely changing size or shape of known elements typically does not meet the patentability threshold.

Hard Exam.q: Under which condition does a real options approach generate substantially MORE value than a standard NPV analysis?
options: When future cash flows are highly predictable and the investment is irreversible
When the magnitude of potential outcomes is large relative to the initial investment and uncertainty is high
When the project has a short time horizon and low technical risk
When the hurdle rate is set below the internal rate of return
Hard Exam.explanation: Real options generate larger value than DCF/NPV when uncertainty is high AND the magnitude of potential outcomes is large relative to the investment. The option to wait, expand, or abandon has more value when there is significant upside potential with limited downside. When cash flows are predictable, NPV works fine and the option premium adds little.

Hard Exam.q: After implementing Connect & Develop, P&G's R&D investment as a percentage of sales changed from:
options: 6.2% to 4.8%, a reduction enabled by eliminating internal R&D teams
4.8% to 3.4%, while R&D productivity increased by nearly 60%
3.4% to 1.8%, because most innovation was outsourced to supplier networks
5.5% to 4.8%, with innovation success rate remaining constant at 35%
Hard Exam.explanation: P&G's R&D as % of sales dropped from 4.8% (2000) to 3.4% (2006), while R&D productivity increased nearly 60% and innovation success rate more than doubled. The old success rate was about 35%. This was NOT outsourcing — P&G maintained strong internal R&D.

Hard Exam.q: In the Nature (2023) study on scientific disruption, what does the CD index measure about a paper?
options: The citation count of a paper relative to other papers in the same journal and year
Whether subsequent papers that cite the paper also cite its predecessors (consolidating) or replace them (disruptive)
The time lag between a paper's publication and its first citation by another researcher
The ratio of a paper's self-citations to external citations as a proxy for influence
Hard Exam.explanation: The CD index measures whether a paper is disruptive or consolidating. If subsequent work citing the paper is LESS likely to also cite its predecessors, the paper is DISRUPTIVE (it replaces older work). If subsequent work citing it is MORE likely to also cite predecessors, it is CONSOLIDATING (it builds on existing work).

Hard Exam.q: Which of the following is NOT identified as a factor in Nokia's failure to respond to the smartphone revolution?
options: Treating software as secondary to hardware capabilities
An atmosphere of fear within the organization that stifled honest internal communication
A matrix organizational structure that created competing priorities and diffused accountability
Excessive investment in open-source operating systems that fragmented their developer ecosystem
Hard Exam.explanation: Nokia's failures included treating software as secondary, organizational fear that prevented honest communication, and a matrix structure that diffused accountability. Nokia did NOT fail due to excessive investment in open-source OS — in fact, their problem was failing to build a competitive software ecosystem at all, unlike Apple's App Store.

Hard Exam.q: An inventor creates a breakthrough battery technology that makes existing lithium-ion batteries obsolete. The profits earned before competitors catch up are BEST described as:
options: Ricardian rents from scarce natural resources
Schumpeterian rents from creative destruction
Monopoly rents from government-granted market protection
Quasi-rents from sunk cost advantages
Hard Exam.explanation: Schumpeterian rents are profits earned by innovators who create temporary monopolies through creative destruction — making existing technologies obsolete. These rents persist until competitors imitate or leapfrog. Ricardian rents come from scarce resources, monopoly rents from legal protection, and quasi-rents from asset specificity.

Hard Exam.q: Barras's reverse product cycle theory suggests that innovation in service industries follows an opposite pattern to manufacturing. What is the correct sequence for services?
options: Product innovation first, then process innovation, then organizational innovation
Process innovation first (improving efficiency), then product innovation (new services)
Radical innovation first, then incremental innovation, then innovation cascades
Market innovation first, then supply chain innovation, then technology innovation
Hard Exam.explanation: Barras's reverse product cycle argues that services follow the OPPOSITE sequence to manufacturing. In manufacturing, product innovation comes first, followed by process innovation. In services, process innovation comes first (e.g., computerizing back-office operations for efficiency), and only later does this enable genuinely new service products (product innovation).

Hard Exam.q: According to Christensen's framework, which is NOT a major reason why successful incumbent firms fail to respond to disruptive innovation?
options: Resource allocation processes systematically favor sustaining innovations that serve existing customers
Disruptive technologies initially address small, low-margin markets that are unattractive to incumbents
Incumbent firms lack the technical expertise to develop the disruptive technology
Incumbents overshoot mainstream needs by focusing on their most demanding and profitable customers
Hard Exam.explanation: Christensen explicitly argues that incumbents usually DO have the technical capability to develop disruptive technologies. The problem is organizational and strategic: their resource allocation favors existing customers, disruptive markets start too small to matter, and they overshoot mainstream needs. It is NOT about lacking technical expertise.

Hard Exam.q: A software company allows engineers to spend 15% of their working hours on personal projects unrelated to their assigned teams. One engineer's side project becomes the company's most popular product. This is an example of:
options: Corporate venture capital (CVC)
External corporate venturing (ECV)
Organizational slack enabling exploration
Mechanistic organizational structure
Hard Exam.explanation: This mirrors Google's '20% time' policy that produced Gmail. Organizational slack provides employees time and resources to explore non-core activities, enabling serendipitous innovation. This is an INTERNAL mechanism (not external venturing or CVC) and reflects an organic (not mechanistic) approach to fostering innovation.

Hard Exam.q: A startup develops fully functional jetpacks that meet all aviation safety standards. Despite successful test flights, they cannot sell units profitably because manufacturing costs exceed $800,000 per unit. This situation BEST illustrates:
options: A crossing-the-chasm problem between early adopters and early majority
Technical feasibility without commercial viability
A disruptive innovation in the low-end foothold stage
The innovator's dilemma where incumbents ignore the new technology
Hard Exam.explanation: This is a textbook case of feasibility ≠ viability, like Concorde. The technology WORKS (technically feasible, meets safety standards) but cannot be sold profitably (not commercially viable). This is distinct from crossing-the-chasm problems (which involve market adoption, not fundamental cost issues).

Hard Exam.q: Which of the following is NOT typically a component of a VC term sheet?
options: Cap table specifying ownership percentages
Employee stock option pool
Board structure with company, investor, and neutral seats
Non-disclosure agreements between portfolio companies
Hard Exam.explanation: The four key term sheet components are: cap table (ownership %), employee pool (stock for employees), board structure (company vs. investor vs. neutral seats), and preferred payout (order of investment payouts). NDAs between portfolio companies are NOT a standard term sheet component.

Hard Exam.q: A pharmaceutical company allocates 90% of its R&D budget to improving existing drugs and 10% to investigating entirely new therapeutic approaches. This firm is MOST at risk of:
options: Organizational slack leading to wasted resources on unfocused projects
A competency trap where over-exploitation crowds out the exploration needed for long-term survival
Excessive diversification diluting core competencies across too many domains
Diseconomies of scale from operating too many parallel R&D programs
Hard Exam.explanation: This describes a classic exploitation-heavy imbalance. While exploitation (improving existing products) generates short-term returns, insufficient exploration (investigating new approaches) leaves the firm vulnerable to radical innovations from competitors. This is a 'competency trap' — the firm becomes so good at incremental improvement that it fails to develop next-generation capabilities.

Hard Exam.q: Moderna designed its COVID-19 mRNA vaccine in 25 days after the SARS-CoV-2 genetic sequence was posted online.
options: True
False
Hard Exam.explanation: False. Moderna designed the vaccine in TWO DAYS after the genetic sequence was posted (January 11, 2020). The 25 days refers to producing the FIRST BATCH of the vaccine (February 7). These are two distinct milestones that are commonly confused.

Hard Exam.q: According to Christensen (2015), Tesla is a disruptive innovator because it is a small entrant that successfully challenged established automakers.
options: True
False
Hard Exam.explanation: False. Christensen explicitly argues Tesla is NOT disruptive. Tesla's foothold is in the HIGH END of the auto market ($70,000+ cars) — a segment that IS interesting to incumbents and has attracted significant competitive response. Being a successful new entrant does NOT automatically make a company disruptive. Disruption requires a low-end or new-market foothold.

Hard Exam.q: P&G's Connect & Develop goal was to source 35% of innovations from outside the company by 2010.
options: True
False
Hard Exam.explanation: False. CEO A.G. Lafley set the goal at 50% external innovations, not 35%. The 35% figure is the RESULT achieved — more than 35% of new products had elements from outside P&G. The 35% was the innovation success rate that had stagnated BEFORE Connect & Develop was implemented.

Hard Exam.q: An organic organizational structure is characterized by rigid hierarchies, formal communication channels, and clearly defined job responsibilities.
options: True
False
Hard Exam.explanation: False. This describes a MECHANISTIC structure. An ORGANIC structure is characterized by flexible hierarchies, informal communication, loosely defined roles, and lateral collaboration. Organic structures are better suited for innovation and exploration, while mechanistic structures suit stable, efficiency-focused environments.

Hard Exam.q: In a typical VC fund structure, the management fee is approximately 20-30% of profits and the carry is approximately 2% per year of assets under management.
options: True
False
Hard Exam.explanation: False. The numbers are REVERSED. The management fee is ~2% per year, and the carry (share of profits retained by the VC) is ~20-30%. This is a common confusion — the small recurring fee is the management fee, while the larger percentage applies to profits.

Hard Exam.q: Patents are most effective at protecting innovations in the computer and electronics industries, where products are complex and multi-component.
options: True
False
Hard Exam.explanation: False. Patents are LEAST effective in computers (3.4/7) and most effective in drugs (6.5/7) and organic chemicals (6.1/7). The biological/chemical industries benefit most from patents because products are more easily defined by composition. Software and electronics patents are especially difficult to enforce.

Hard Exam.q: Moderna was founded in 2015 and had successfully brought three mRNA vaccines to market before the COVID-19 pandemic.
options: True
False
Hard Exam.explanation: False — TWO errors. Moderna was founded in 2010 (not 2015), and it had developed 9 vaccines before COVID-19 but had NEVER brought ANY to market. The COVID-19 vaccine was Moderna's first commercially available product.

Hard Exam.q: The Nature (2023) study found that both the proportion AND the absolute number of disruptive scientific papers have declined since the mid-20th century.
options: True
False
Hard Exam.explanation: False. The study found that the PROPORTION of disruptive papers declined, but the ABSOLUTE NUMBER stayed broadly the same. The proportion dropped because the total number of papers and patents exploded. This distinction is crucial to the debate — the 'not worried' side argues a stable absolute count is fine.

Hard Exam.q: Unlike patents and trademarks, copyright protection is granted automatically when a work is put into tangible form, without requiring a formal application.
options: True
False
Hard Exam.explanation: True. Copyright is granted automatically when a work is put into tangible form — no application is needed. This is a key distinction from patents (which require formal application, examination, and approval) and trademarks (which can be obtained by use OR registration, but registration provides stronger protection).

Hard Exam.q: According to Christensen (2015), the iPhone was a disruptive innovation in the smartphone market when it launched in 2007.
options: True
False
Hard Exam.explanation: False. Christensen explicitly states that the iPhone launched as a SUSTAINING innovation in the smartphone market — it targeted the same customers as incumbents and succeeded on product superiority. However, its subsequent growth was disruptive — not of smartphones, but of the LAPTOP as the primary internet access point, enabled by the App Store ecosystem.

Weak Spots Drill.q: Eli Lilly invested nearly $1 billion developing Humulin, but could not sustain its 25% price premium. Meanwhile, Novo Nordisk commanded a 30% premium per unit. What did Novo Nordisk compete on?
options: Higher purity levels than Humulin
Delivery method (insulin pens offering convenience)
Lower production costs through genetic engineering
Exclusive licensing agreements with endocrinologists
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: Novo Nordisk won by shifting the basis of competition from purity to convenience. Their insulin pen reduced injection time from 1–2 minutes to under 10 seconds, and customers willingly paid the 30% premium — while Lilly’s purity-based premium could not be sustained.

Weak Spots Drill.q: Lilly’s marketers made a critical error when assessing demand for Humulin. What was the miscalculation?
options: They surveyed patients directly instead of physicians
They ignored FDA purity requirements and overproduced
They weighted the opinions of endocrinologists most heavily, even though GPs wrote 80% of insulin prescriptions
They assumed genetically engineered insulin would be cheaper than animal insulin
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: Lilly put the greatest weight on endocrinologists’ opinions — the most demanding specialists — but these doctors wrote only about 20% of prescriptions. The mainstream market (GPs, 80% of prescriptions) was already satisfied with animal insulin purity. This is Christensen’s classic point about listening to the wrong customers.

Weak Spots Drill.q: The Eli Lilly insulin case illustrates which of Christensen’s core concepts?
options: Crossing the chasm between early adopters and early majority
Network externalities creating winner-take-all dynamics
New-market disruption from a low-end foothold
Overshooting — following a performance trajectory that exceeds market needs
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: Lilly relentlessly improved insulin purity (from 50,000 ppm in 1925 to 0 ppm with Humulin), but the market was already satisfied with pork insulin at ~10 ppm. This is textbook overshooting: the performance trajectory exceeded the trajectory of market need, and the basis of competition shifted to convenience.

Weak Spots Drill.q: Eli Lilly’s difficulty responding to Novo Nordisk’s insulin pen was primarily caused by lack of R&D funding.
options: True
False
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: False. Lilly had invested nearly $1 billion in Humulin alone — funding was not the problem. The issue was path dependency and core rigidity: Lilly’s entire corporate identity was built around pharmaceutical chemistry and purity improvement. They were a drug company, not a medical device company.

Weak Spots Drill.q: According to Christensen, Raynor & McDonald (HBR 2015), why is Uber NOT considered a disruptive innovation?
options: Because Uber did not use new technology
Because Uber was not profitable in its early years
Because Uber started in a well-served mainstream market rather than a low-end or new-market foothold
Because Uber did not replace the taxi industry entirely
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: Christensen argues Uber is not disruptive because it launched in San Francisco — a well-served taxi market — targeting existing taxi users with a better service. Disruption requires starting in a niche/low-end or new-market foothold and moving upmarket. Uber did the opposite.

Weak Spots Drill.q: In Christensen’s disk drive study, what percentage of sustaining entrants succeeded against incumbents?
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: Only 6% of sustaining entrants succeeded against established incumbents in Christensen’s disk drive study. This statistic underscores why disruption theory matters: attacking incumbents head-on with sustaining innovations almost never works.

Weak Spots Drill.q: According to Christensen (HBR 2015), Tesla is a disruptive innovation because it introduced electric vehicles that incumbents initially ignored.
options: True
False
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: False. Tesla is NOT disruptive according to Christensen. Tesla’s foothold is in the high end of the auto market ($70,000+ cars) — a segment that is very interesting to incumbents and has attracted significant competitive investment. Disruption requires starting in a low-end or new-market foothold.

Weak Spots Drill.q: The iPhone is classified by Christensen as disruptive to laptops as internet-access devices, but as a sustaining innovation in the smartphone market.
options: True
False
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: True. The iPhone launched as a sustaining innovation in smartphones (targeting same customers with a superior product). But its subsequent growth was disruptive — not of other smartphones, but of the LAPTOP as the primary internet access point, achieved through the App Store business model.

Weak Spots Drill.q: A ‘patent thicket’ refers to:
options: A government program that fast-tracks patent applications in emerging industries
A portfolio of patents held by a single firm to block competitors from entering a market
A dense web of overlapping patents that makes it difficult for firms to commercialize technology without infringing
The process of filing patents in multiple countries simultaneously via the PCT
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: A patent thicket is a dense web of overlapping patents held by multiple parties, making it difficult for any single firm to commercialize new technology without potentially infringing on others’ patents. This is different from a single firm’s patent portfolio or strategic blocking.

Weak Spots Drill.q: In the VHS vs. Betamax standards battle, what strategy helped VHS win despite Betamax having arguably superior technical quality?
options: VHS used a wholly proprietary strategy to maintain quality control
VHS was first to market by several years
VHS secured exclusive government contracts for educational use
VHS pursued liberal licensing, building a larger installed base and more complementary content
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: VHS won through liberal licensing — allowing many manufacturers to produce VHS players, which built a larger installed base. This attracted more content providers (complementary goods), creating a virtuous cycle. In markets with network effects, diffusing the technology broadly can be more important than maintaining tight control.

Weak Spots Drill.q: On Schilling’s spectrum from wholly proprietary to wholly open, Microsoft Windows is an example of moderate licensing because it controls the platform architecture while allowing third-party software development.
options: True
False
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: True. Microsoft Windows sits at moderate licensing on the open-proprietary spectrum. Microsoft maintains architectural control over the OS while allowing broad third-party software development. This is between limited licensing (e.g., Xbox game developers) and liberal licensing (e.g., Sun’s Java).

Weak Spots Drill.q: According to Shane Ch 16, which of the following is a benefit of CENTRALIZED R&D?
options: It improves access to information through more external contact points
It makes R&D more market-oriented by placing teams closer to customers
It reduces financial risk by isolating failed efforts within individual units
It encourages radical innovation because decentralized managers tend to focus on incremental improvements
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: Centralization encourages radical innovation because decentralized managers typically focus on incremental, unit-specific improvements. Other centralization benefits include: reducing coordination costs, freeing technical personnel from operational activities, enabling large-scale innovation, and facilitating corporate strategy. The other options are benefits of decentralization.

Weak Spots Drill.q: The large-to-small firm innovation ratio varies dramatically by industry. In which industry are SMALL firms most innovative relative to large firms?
options: Aircraft (ratio 31.0)
Pharmaceuticals (ratio 9.2)
Measuring and controlling devices (ratio 0.067)
Automobiles
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: Measuring and controlling devices has a ratio of 0.067, meaning small firms vastly out-innovate large firms. Compare with aircraft at 31.0, where large firms dominate innovation. Small firms are more innovative in younger, less capital-intensive industries with simpler products.

Weak Spots Drill.q: Microsoft under CEO Steve Ballmer divided the company into seven separate divisions, illustrating how decentralization can hinder radical, cross-unit innovation.
options: True
False
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: True. Ballmer’s restructuring of Microsoft into 7 divisions is cited as an example of how decentralization can create challenges for evaluating and pursuing radical innovation that spans multiple business units. Decentralized managers tend to focus on incremental improvements within their own units.

Weak Spots Drill.q: Organizational ‘slack’ — giving employees time and resources for non-core activities — is considered wasteful and detrimental to innovation performance.
options: True
False
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: False. Slack resources enable innovation. Google’s 20% time policy (which led to Gmail and Google News) is the classic example. Innovation requires time, money, and resources for exploration beyond current operational needs. Highly innovative companies resist cutting R&D slack even during financial difficulties.

Weak Spots Drill.q: How many days elapsed from when the COVID-19 genome sequence was posted online to when Moderna shipped its vaccine candidate to the NIH?
options: 14 days
90 days
42 days
25 days
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: 42 days elapsed from the genome sequence being posted (January 11, 2020) to the vaccine being shipped to NIH (February 24, 2020). This represented a 90% reduction from the previous fastest record of 20 months for SARS. The vaccine sequence itself was designed in just 2 days.

Weak Spots Drill.q: Moderna had $60M in revenue in 2019, raised $5.1B before its IPO, received $955M from BARDA for COVID vaccine development, and saw revenue grow to $17.6B by 2021.
options: True
False
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: True. All figures are correct: $60.2M revenue (2019), $5.1B raised pre-IPO, $955M total from BARDA ($483M + $472M), and revenue of $17.6B in 2021. The IPO in December 2018 valued Moderna at $7.5B — the biggest biotech IPO at that time.

Weak Spots Drill.q: Moderna’s ‘AI factory’ operating model is best described as:
options: Outsourcing drug design to AI startups while focusing on clinical trials
Using AI exclusively for manufacturing quality control at the Norwood plant
A born-digital, vertically integrated platform where data flows freely across all teams, enabling AI-driven drug design and cross-drug learning
A partnership model where academic AI labs design mRNA sequences for Moderna
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: Moderna built a ‘born digital’ operating model from day one: cloud infrastructure, IoT-connected lab instruments, automation, and AI algorithms — all feeding a virtuous cycle where more data leads to better algorithms and better mRNA medicines. By spring 2020 they had ~20 AI algorithms in production.

Weak Spots Drill.q: The strongest argument against spinning off Moderna’s COVID-19 business is that it would create the very organizational silos that Moderna designed its integrated platform to avoid.
options: True
False
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: True. Moderna’s competitive advantage comes from shared data and an integrated platform where learnings from one drug spill into all others. Spinning off the COVID business would break this virtuous cycle by creating silos — exactly the kind of structure Moderna had seen fail in traditional big pharma.

Weak Spots Drill.q: In Apple’s discretionary leadership model (Podolny & Hansen, HBR 2020), how do VPs allocate their time across the own/learn/teach/delegate framework?
options: Mostly in the ‘delegate’ quadrant, like general managers at other companies
Equally across all four quadrants to maintain balance
Mostly in the ‘owning’ and ‘learning’ quadrants, staying deeply involved in areas of high expertise and actively building expertise in new areas
Mostly in the ‘teaching’ quadrant, mentoring junior employees
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: Apple VPs spend most time in the ‘owning’ quadrant (~40%) and ‘learning’ quadrant (~30%). This contrasts sharply with general managers at other companies who spend most time in the ‘delegating’ box. As activities mature, leaders move them from ‘owning’ to ‘teaching.’

Weak Spots Drill.q: Why does Apple’s functional structure work better for innovation than a multidivisional structure, according to the HBR 2020 article?
options: It reduces the total number of employees needed per product line
It allows each product division to optimize its own profit margin independently
