It aligns expertise with decision-making authority and insulates product decisions from short-term P&L pressure
It eliminates the need for cross-functional collaboration on complex products
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: Apple’s functional structure ensures that ‘experts lead experts’ and that R&D executives’ bonuses are based on companywide performance, not individual product P&L. This insulates technology bets from short-term financial pressures. The multidivisional structure (which failed at Apple in the 80s-90s) aligns accountability and control but can lead to short-term thinking.

Weak Spots Drill.q: Despite Apple’s revenue growing nearly 40x (from $7B to $260B) between 1997 and 2019, the number of VPs only approximately doubled (from ~50 to ~96).
options: True
False
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: True. Apple was disciplined about limiting senior positions: employees grew 8x (17,000 to 137,000) and revenue grew nearly 40x, but VPs only doubled. To manage scale, Apple split the hardware function, added AI/ML as a new function, and developed the own/learn/teach/delegate leadership model.

Weak Spots Drill.q: According to VC outcome data presented in the course, what percentage of total VC investments is responsible for 97% of returns for the entire asset class?
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: Only 0.4% of VC investments generate 97% of all returns for the entire asset class. This extreme skewness is a defining feature of venture capital: 63.2% of portfolio cost goes to investments returning less than 1x, while just 4.3% of cost goes to >10x investments that generate 61.7% of value.

Weak Spots Drill.q: Of approximately 4,000 firms seeking venture capital, how many typically raise money and how many reach NOK 1 billion in revenue?
options: 1,000 raise money; 100 reach NOK 1B
500 raise money; 50 reach NOK 1B revenue
200 raise money; 15 reach NOK 1B revenue
400 raise money; 40 reach NOK 1B revenue
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: Of 4,000 firms seeking VC: only 200 will raise money (5%), and of those, only 15 will reach NOK 1 billion in revenue. This illustrates the extreme selectivity and skewness of VC outcomes.

Weak Spots Drill.q: A limited partner (LP) investing in a VC fund typically expects a 2-3x return over 15 years.
options: True
False
Weak Spots Drill.explanation: False. LPs expect a 5-10x return over 7 years (not 2-3x over 15 years). VC is one of the riskiest asset classes, and the high expected return compensates for illiquidity and risk. The VC firm charges a management fee of ~2% per year and carry of 20-30% of profits.

Fresh Challenge.q: Nokia's market capitalization fell from approximately $250 billion in 2000 to approximately $43 billion in 2011. This decline BEST illustrates:
options: The patent cliff effect seen when key patents expire and generic competitors enter
The consequences of an incumbent failing to shift to a new technology S-curve despite having the resources to do so
Disruptive innovation from a low-end foothold, as defined by Christensen
The Valley of Death funding gap between research and commercial revenue
Fresh Challenge.explanation: Nokia's dramatic market cap decline from $250B to $43B is a textbook case of incumbent failure to shift S-curves. Nokia had the resources, R&D budget, and market position, but organizational inertia, competence traps, and failure to embrace software ecosystems prevented the shift. This is NOT a patent cliff (no patent expiry involved), NOT low-end disruption (iPhone entered at the high end), and NOT a Valley of Death (Nokia was highly profitable).

Fresh Challenge.q: Nokia launched the 7710 touchscreen phone in 2004, but it was a commercial failure. Three years later, the Nokia N95 sold over 10 million units while Apple's iPhone sold 13 million in its first year. What does this sequence illustrate about technology adoption?
options: Touchscreen technology was inherently inferior to keypad technology in all cases
Being first with a technology feature does not guarantee success — timing, ecosystem, and user experience determine adoption
The N95 failed because it lacked a touchscreen interface
Apple succeeded solely because it had superior hardware specifications
Fresh Challenge.explanation: Nokia had touchscreen technology before Apple (7710 in 2004) but it flopped. The iPhone succeeded not because of hardware specs (it had a 2MP camera vs N95's 5MP, no 3G, no GPS, no MMS) but because of superior UX, ecosystem vision, and timing. Being first with a feature is not enough — the whole product experience and market readiness matter.

Fresh Challenge.q: When the iPhone launched in 2007, it had a 2MP camera (vs Nokia N95's 5MP), no 3G, no GPS, and no MMS. Despite these inferior specifications, it outsold the N95. Which concept BEST explains why?
options: Network externalities from the App Store created indirect network effects that made hardware specs irrelevant
The iPhone was a low-end disruptive innovation that targeted non-consumers
Apple had stronger patent protection that prevented Nokia from competing
The Bass model predicted faster adoption due to a higher coefficient of imitation
Fresh Challenge.explanation: The iPhone won through its touchscreen UX and — crucially — the App Store ecosystem that created powerful indirect network effects. More apps attracted more users, which attracted more developers. Hardware specs alone could not compete with this ecosystem advantage. Note: at launch the iPhone had NO third-party apps and NO App Store, but Apple's ecosystem vision proved decisive. The iPhone was NOT low-end (it entered at the premium end), and patents did not prevent Nokia from competing.

Fresh Challenge.q: When Stephen Elop became Nokia's CEO in 2010, he faced three strategic options: (1) double down on Symbian, (2) adopt Android like other manufacturers, or (3) partner with Microsoft. Which option did he choose, and what concept does this decision illustrate?
options: He chose Android, illustrating the benefits of joining a dominant design's ecosystem
He doubled down on Symbian, illustrating the competence trap of over-exploitation
He partnered with Microsoft, illustrating the risks of choosing a platform partner that lacks a strong app ecosystem
He pursued all three simultaneously, illustrating ambidextrous organizational strategy
Fresh Challenge.explanation: Elop chose Microsoft's Windows Phone platform, abandoning both Symbian and Android. This decision proved disastrous because Windows Phone lacked the app ecosystem (indirect network effects) that iOS and Android had built. Nokia's app store 'Ovi' had already failed to attract developers, and switching to another weak platform repeated the same ecosystem problem. Nokia was eventually acquired by Microsoft in 2013.

Fresh Challenge.q: The Bayh-Dole Act (1980) fundamentally changed university innovation in the United States by:
options: Requiring all university research to be published as open-access within one year
Allowing universities to retain ownership of inventions developed with federal funding and license them commercially
Prohibiting universities from conducting applied research and restricting them to basic research only
Mandating that all university patents be transferred to the funding government agency
Fresh Challenge.explanation: Before Bayh-Dole, inventions developed with federal funding were owned by the government and rarely commercialized. The Act allowed universities to retain IP ownership and license inventions, creating a massive incentive for university-industry technology transfer. This led to a surge in university patents, spin-offs, and technology licensing offices.

Fresh Challenge.q: In the Bass diffusion model, a product has a high coefficient of innovation (p) but a low coefficient of imitation (q). What adoption pattern would you expect?
options: Very slow initial adoption followed by explosive word-of-mouth-driven growth
Strong early adoption driven by external influences (e.g., advertising), but limited acceleration from social contagion
No adoption at all, since both coefficients must be high for diffusion to occur
A J-shaped curve with adoption accelerating indefinitely without reaching a ceiling
Fresh Challenge.explanation: A high p (innovation coefficient) means many adopters are driven by external factors like advertising or independent evaluation, producing strong early adoption. A low q (imitation coefficient) means less word-of-mouth amplification, so the adoption curve rises quickly but lacks the explosive social-contagion acceleration that creates the steep middle section of a typical S-curve.

Fresh Challenge.q: Stokes' model classifies research into quadrants. Which pairing of researcher and quadrant is CORRECT?
options: Bohr quadrant (pure basic research driven by curiosity), Edison quadrant (pure applied research), Pasteur quadrant (use-inspired basic research)
Bohr quadrant (applied research for industry), Edison quadrant (curiosity-driven discovery), Pasteur quadrant (government-funded military research)
Bohr quadrant (use-inspired basic research), Edison quadrant (pure basic research), Pasteur quadrant (pure applied research)
All three quadrants represent the same type of research but differ only in funding source
Fresh Challenge.explanation: Bohr represents pure basic research motivated by fundamental understanding (e.g., atomic structure). Edison represents pure applied research driven by practical problem-solving without seeking fundamental understanding. Pasteur represents use-inspired basic research — seeking fundamental understanding motivated by practical application (e.g., microbiology driven by public health needs).

Fresh Challenge.q: P&G's Connect and Develop model relied on a network of 70 'technology entrepreneurs' stationed around the world. Their primary role was to:
options: Develop new products independently and ship them to P&G headquarters for review
Scout external innovations by mining scientific literature, patents, and databases to match solutions with P&G's defined technology needs
Manage P&G's patent portfolio and file infringement lawsuits against competitors
Recruit top scientists from universities to join P&G's internal R&D labs
Fresh Challenge.explanation: P&G's 70 technology entrepreneurs were scouts — their job was to find external solutions to P&G's internally defined technology problems by searching literature, patent databases, and networks. They did NOT develop products independently or manage patents. P&G also had 7,500 internal researchers and access to suppliers with a combined R&D staff of 50,000.

Fresh Challenge.q: A trademark can be lost if it becomes generic — meaning the brand name becomes the common term for the product category. Which of the following are real examples of trademarks lost to genericization?
options: Coca-Cola and Nike — both lost trademark protection due to widespread brand recognition
Aspirin and escalator — both were originally trademarked brand names that became generic terms
iPhone and Google — both lost trademark protection because consumers used them as general nouns
Microsoft Windows and Intel — both became generic due to market dominance
Fresh Challenge.explanation: Aspirin (originally a Bayer trademark) and escalator (originally an Otis trademark) are classic examples of trademarks lost through genericization. The brand names became so commonly used as generic product names that they lost legal trademark protection. This is why companies like Xerox and Google actively fight to prevent their names from becoming generic verbs.

Fresh Challenge.q: 3M's '15% rule' — allowing employees to spend 15% of their time on projects of their own choosing — led to the invention of Post-it Notes. This practice is an example of:
options: External corporate venturing (ECV) where 3M invested in outside startups
Organizational slack combined with decentralized innovation, enabling serendipitous discovery
A mechanistic organizational structure with formal innovation processes
The Valley of Death, where innovations struggle to find funding between research and commercialization
Fresh Challenge.explanation: 3M's 15% rule is a textbook example of organizational slack — providing employees dedicated time and resources to explore non-core activities. Combined with 3M's decentralized innovation culture, this enabled serendipitous discoveries like Post-it Notes. Google's similar '20% time' produced Gmail. Both illustrate how slack fosters exploration without central planning.

Fresh Challenge.q: Sildenafil (Viagra) was originally developed by Pfizer as a treatment for angina (chest pain). During clinical trials, an unexpected side effect was discovered, and the drug was repurposed for erectile dysfunction. In Niosi's framework, this is an example of:
options: Incremental innovation along an existing technological trajectory
Exaptation — a technology repurposed for a function other than what it was originally designed for
A patent thicket that prevented the original application from reaching market
Creative destruction where the new application destroyed the cardiovascular drug market
Fresh Challenge.explanation: Exaptation (from evolutionary biology) refers to using a feature or technology for a purpose different from what it was originally developed for. Viagra's journey from angina treatment to blockbuster erectile dysfunction drug is a classic example. Niosi uses this concept to explain how innovation cascades can branch in unexpected directions when technologies find unintended applications.

Fresh Challenge.q: Moderna's revenue trajectory from 2019 to 2021 was approximately:
Fresh Challenge.explanation: Moderna's revenue went from $60M (2019) to $803M (2020) to $17,675M ($17.6B) in 2021. The $108M figure is from 2016, not 2019. This extraordinary growth was driven entirely by COVID-19 vaccine sales. By comparison, Moderna had raised over $5 billion in funding before the pandemic and had never brought a product to market.

Fresh Challenge.q: Both copyright and patent protection require a formal application to a government office before they take effect.
options: True
False
Fresh Challenge.explanation: False. Copyright is granted AUTOMATICALLY when a work is put into tangible form — no application is needed. Patents, however, DO require a formal application, examination, and approval through a patent office (e.g., USPTO, EPO, Patentstyret). This is a fundamental difference between the two forms of IP protection.

Fresh Challenge.q: Under the 'done for hire' (work for hire) exception in copyright law, the employer — not the individual creator — owns the copyright to works created by employees within the scope of their employment.
options: True
False
Fresh Challenge.explanation: True. The 'done for hire' exception means that when an employee creates a work as part of their job duties, the copyright belongs to the employer (the commissioning entity), not the employee. This is why companies own the code their programmers write and the designs their artists create during employment.

Fresh Challenge.q: One of Nokia's strategic mistakes was refusing to customize phones for US carriers, which limited its distribution in the world's most profitable mobile market.
options: True
False
Fresh Challenge.explanation: True. Nokia refused to work closely with US carriers (like AT&T, Verizon) on customized handsets, while competitors like Motorola and later Apple were willing to tailor products for carrier distribution. This weakened Nokia's position in the US market, which was critical for smartphone adoption and where Apple launched the iPhone exclusively with AT&T.

Fresh Challenge.q: The first iPhone (2007) launched with full third-party app support and a functioning App Store from day one.
options: True
False
Fresh Challenge.explanation: False. The first iPhone launched in 2007 with NO third-party apps and NO App Store. Steve Jobs initially wanted all third-party functionality delivered through web apps. The App Store was not launched until July 2008 (one year later). Despite this limitation, the iPhone still outsold the Nokia N95 because of its revolutionary touchscreen UX and ecosystem vision.

Fresh Challenge.q: Pfizer's Lipitor experienced a 'patent cliff' — revenue dropped from approximately $13 billion to approximately $2 billion after its patent expired and generic competitors entered the market.
options: True
False
Fresh Challenge.explanation: True. Lipitor is the classic example of a patent cliff. While the patent was active, Lipitor was the best-selling drug in history. Once patent protection expired, generic manufacturers could legally produce identical versions at much lower prices, causing Pfizer's revenue to plummet. This illustrates why appropriability (via patents) is critical in the pharmaceutical industry, where drugs are easy to copy but expensive to develop.

Fresh Challenge.q: Nokia's app store 'Ovi' successfully attracted a large developer community, but Nokia failed because its hardware was inferior to Apple's.
options: True
False
Fresh Challenge.explanation: False. Nokia's hardware was NOT inferior — the N95 had a 5MP camera, GPS, 3G, and MMS when the iPhone had none of these. The problem was the OPPOSITE: Ovi was unattractive to developers because of Symbian's fragmented development environment, lack of a unified SDK, and poor developer tools. Apple's App Store succeeded because it made development easy and profitable for third-party developers, creating powerful indirect network effects.

Fresh Challenge.q: According to Niosi, innovation cascades were just as frequent and long-lasting before the Industrial Revolution as they are today.
options: True
False
Fresh Challenge.explanation: False. Niosi argues that pre-Industrial Revolution innovation cascades were SHORT-LIVED because the institutional environment did not support sustained innovation — there were no research universities, no public research labs, no systematic R&D, few innovation centers, and communication between them was slow and costly. Post-Industrial Revolution cascades are longer and more frequent due to rising science-based industries, more universities, faster communication, and policy incentives.

Fresh Challenge.q: According to Vaclav Smil (WSJ 2023), the exponential growth pattern seen in computing (Moore's Law) is representative of technological progress across all major economic sectors including agriculture, energy, and transportation.
options: True
False
Fresh Challenge.explanation: False. Smil's central argument is that Moore's Law is the EXCEPTION, not the rule. Fundamental economic activities show only slow, linear progress: agriculture at roughly 1% per year, battery energy density at about 2% per year, and steam turbine efficiency at approximately 1.5% per year. The belief that all technology follows exponential curves is a dangerous myth promoted by tech optimists like Kurzweil and Harari.

Fresh Challenge.q: Apple's iPhone 7 Plus portrait mode (2016) required collaboration among 40+ specialist teams. The project was championed by Paul Hubel, who conceived the idea in 2009 but waited years for enabling technologies. In innovation terminology, Hubel's early concept functions most like:
options: A real option — the right but not the obligation to invest further when conditions improved
A sunk cost that should have been abandoned after the first year
An example of adverse selection between Hubel and Apple management
A patent troll strategy to block competitors from developing similar features
Fresh Challenge.explanation: Hubel's early concept for computational bokeh was a real option: Apple had the right, but not the obligation, to invest further when enabling technology matured. When dual-lens cameras and sufficient processing power became available, Apple exercised the option. This is how real options work in R&D — early exploration creates optionality for future investment.

Fresh Challenge.q: The CD index (used in the Nature 2023 study on disruptiveness) measures whether a paper is disruptive or consolidating by examining:
options: How many citations the paper receives in its first year of publication
Whether subsequent papers that cite it also cite its predecessors (consolidating) or stop citing them (disruptive)
The ratio of government funding to private funding in the research
How many co-authors contributed to the paper
Fresh Challenge.explanation: The CD index works by looking at citation patterns: if papers that cite a work also cite its predecessors, the work is consolidating (building on existing knowledge). If they stop citing predecessors, the work is disruptive (replacing them). Watson & Crick's DNA paper is a classic disruptive example — it created a new field.

Fresh Challenge.q: In Apple's functional structure, R&D executives' bonuses are based on companywide performance rather than individual product P&L. The primary purpose of this compensation design is to:
options: Reduce total compensation costs across the organization
Insulate product decisions from short-term financial pressures so that technical experts can make long-term technology bets
Punish underperforming product lines by linking their teams to overall company performance
Comply with SEC regulations requiring uniform bonus structures
Fresh Challenge.explanation: By tying bonuses to companywide (not product-level) performance, Apple ensures that engineers and designers focus on building the best possible products rather than maximizing short-term revenue for their specific product. This removes the pressure that divisional P&L creates and enables longer-term, riskier innovation bets.

Fresh Challenge.q: Shane Ch 16 identifies three key dimensions of organizational structure that affect innovation: centralization, organicity, and slack. A firm that is highly centralized, mechanistic, and has zero slack would be optimized for:
options: Radical innovation and exploration of new technologies
Efficiency and incremental process improvement, but poorly suited for breakthrough innovation
Both radical and incremental innovation equally
External corporate venturing and startup acquisitions
Fresh Challenge.explanation: A centralized-mechanistic-no-slack firm is optimized for efficiency: tight control, standardized procedures, and maximum resource utilization. However, it would struggle with breakthrough innovation because mechanistic structures inhibit creativity, lack of slack eliminates exploration time, and while centralization can help radical innovation, it needs organic elements and slack to actually produce breakthroughs.

Fresh Challenge.q: Moderna achieved vertical integration in 2018 by building a $125 million manufacturing plant in Norwood, MA, even though it had zero products on the market at that time.
options: True
False
Fresh Challenge.explanation: True. Moderna invested $125M in a manufacturing plant in Norwood, MA in 2018 — two years before COVID-19 — despite never having brought a vaccine to market. This was a bold bet on vertical integration that gave Moderna the specialized complementary assets (in Teece's framework) needed to rapidly produce vaccines when the pandemic created demand. Without this pre-built capacity, Moderna could not have moved from vaccine design to production in 25 days.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: The TECHNOLOGY S-curve differs from the ADOPTION S-curve in that the technology S-curve plots:
options: Cumulative market adoption (y-axis) against time (x-axis)
Performance (y-axis) against effort or investment (x-axis)
Revenue (y-axis) against number of competitors (x-axis)
Market share (y-axis) against price (x-axis)
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: The technology S-curve plots PERFORMANCE against EFFORT/investment. The adoption S-curve plots cumulative market adoption against TIME. Same S-shape, completely different meaning. Option A describes the adoption S-curve, not the technology S-curve.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: A 'General Purpose Technology' (GPT), as described by Shane, is characterized by:
options: A technology that serves only one specific industry but dominates it completely
A technology that is pervasive, improves over time, and spawns complementary innovations across many sectors
A patented technology that generates licensing revenue from all industries simultaneously
A government-funded technology that is mandated for public use
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: Shane describes GPTs (e.g., electricity, the internet, steam engine) as technologies that are (1) pervasive across many sectors, (2) improve over time, and (3) spawn complementary innovations and co-inventions. They are not limited to one industry or dependent on patents or mandates.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: According to Shane, only 11% of new products in the US depend on academic research. This statistic suggests that:
options: Universities are irrelevant to innovation and should reduce research funding
Most innovation originates in firms, but universities play a critical supporting role for science-based industries
Academic research is the primary driver of all product innovation worldwide
Government-funded basic research has no connection to commercial products
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: The 11% figure shows that while most innovation originates in firms, academic research still provides essential foundational knowledge, particularly in science-based industries like pharmaceuticals and biotechnology. It does not mean universities are irrelevant — their role is complementary.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: The Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) is a project selection tool that is most useful when:
options: A firm needs to calculate the exact net present value of a single well-defined project
A firm must compare multiple projects across several criteria that are difficult to quantify in monetary terms
A firm wants to determine the internal rate of return for a portfolio of investments
A firm needs to value the flexibility to abandon a project if uncertainty resolves unfavorably
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: AHP helps managers rank innovation projects by breaking the decision into multiple criteria (technical feasibility, market potential, strategic fit, etc.) and making pairwise comparisons. NPV handles single-project valuation (A), IRR measures return rates (C), and real options value flexibility (D).

Final Prep Exam 1.q: According to Shane Ch 16, which of the following is a DISADVANTAGE of decentralized R&D?
options: It reduces access to external information because fewer people interact with stakeholders
It leads decentralized managers to focus on incremental, unit-specific improvements rather than radical innovation
It makes it harder to get government aid for R&D across geographic boundaries
It prevents employees from generating diverse ideas
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: Shane identifies several disadvantages of decentralization: it leads to avoidance of radical innovation (managers focus on incremental, unit-specific benefits), makes coordination more difficult, causes conflict with ongoing operations, hinders strategic management of innovation, and results in underinvestment in large-scale innovation. The other options are actually BENEFITS of decentralization.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: On Schilling's spectrum from wholly proprietary to wholly open, the Xbox console is classified as:
options: Wholly open, because anyone can develop games for it
Liberal licensing, similar to Sun's Java
Wholly proprietary, because Microsoft controls the hardware platform entirely
Moderate licensing, similar to Microsoft Windows
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: The Xbox console is wholly proprietary — Microsoft controls the hardware platform entirely. Xbox game developers operate under limited licensing (a separate category). Windows is moderate licensing. Java was liberal licensing. Glyphosate (post-2000) is wholly open.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: Which statement about copyright protection is CORRECT?
options: Copyright lasts for 20 years from the date of registration and must be applied for through a patent office
Copyright is granted automatically when a work is put into tangible form, lasts for the author's life plus 70 years, and covers expression — not ideas
Copyright protects ideas, methods, and principles as long as they are documented in writing
Copyright requires formal registration, lasts for 10 years, and is renewable indefinitely
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: Copyright is automatic (no application needed), lasts life + 70 years, and protects the EXPRESSION of ideas, not the ideas themselves. 20 years is patent duration. 10 years renewable is trademarks. Ideas/methods/principles cannot be copyrighted.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: Which pairing of product and position on Schilling's open-proprietary spectrum is INCORRECT?
options: Xbox console — wholly proprietary
Microsoft Windows — moderate licensing
Sun's Java — wholly proprietary
Glyphosate (post-2000) — wholly open
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: Sun's Java was classified as LIBERAL licensing, not wholly proprietary. Java's source code was widely available and developers could use it freely. The other pairings are correct: Xbox = wholly proprietary, Windows = moderate licensing, Glyphosate = wholly open.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: Louis Pasteur's research on microbiology is classified in Stokes' model as 'use-inspired basic research' because:
options: Pasteur conducted pure curiosity-driven research with no practical application in mind
Pasteur sought fundamental scientific understanding but was motivated by practical problems like food spoilage and disease
Pasteur focused exclusively on solving immediate industrial problems without seeking deeper understanding
Pasteur's work was funded entirely by the French government for military applications
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: The Pasteur quadrant represents research that seeks fundamental understanding (like Bohr) but is motivated by practical use (like Edison). Pasteur investigated microbiology to solve real problems (food preservation, disease prevention). Bohr = pure curiosity. Edison = pure practical problem-solving.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: A biotech startup tells investors its drug has a 60% chance of FDA approval, but internal data shows the probability is closer to 20%. This is an example of:
options: Moral hazard — the startup is taking excessive risk with investor money
Adverse selection — the startup has private information that investors cannot verify
Skewness in outcomes — the drug will either succeed massively or fail completely
The Valley of Death — the startup cannot bridge the gap between research and revenue
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: This is adverse selection: information asymmetry where the entrepreneur knows more about the true probability of success than investors. Moral hazard occurs AFTER investment (when the entrepreneur takes excessive risk with investor money). Skewness describes return distributions, and the Valley of Death is a funding gap.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: In the Selden patent case, Selden was able to control the early US automobile industry despite not being the original inventor of the combustion engine. His primary strategy was:
options: Developing a superior engine design that outperformed European alternatives
Filing a broad US patent in 1879 and strategically delaying its issuance until the automobile industry grew in value
Acquiring exclusive manufacturing rights from Daimler and Benz in Germany
Partnering with Ford to create a joint licensing agreement for all US manufacturers
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: Selden was a patent attorney who filed a broad automobile patent in 1879 and delayed its issuance until 1895, when the industry had grown. Investors then bought the patent rights and sued auto startups for infringement. Ford challenged this, won on appeal in 1911 by using a different engine type — showing that process innovation can circumvent broad patents.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: Nokia's market capitalization dropped from $250 billion to $43 billion between 2000 and 2011. Which factor was NOT a contributing cause?
options: Treating software as secondary to hardware in the smartphone transition
An organizational culture of fear that prevented honest internal communication
The complete absence of any mobile operating system, leaving Nokia unable to compete with iOS and Android
Refusing to customize phones for US carriers, limiting distribution in a critical market
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: Nokia DID have a mobile operating system — Symbian. The problem was not the absence of an OS but that Symbian was inferior as a smartphone platform compared to iOS and Android. Nokia also had MeeGo in development. The statement 'complete absence of any mobile operating system' is factually wrong.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: Moderna shipped its COVID-19 vaccine candidate to the NIH in 42 days. The PREVIOUS fastest timeline for developing a vaccine candidate was:
options: 6 months for the H1N1 influenza vaccine
20 months for SARS — making Moderna's timeline a 90% reduction
12 months for the Ebola vaccine
3 months for the MERS vaccine, which Moderna had also developed
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: The previous fastest record was 20 months for SARS. Moderna's 42-day timeline represented a 90% reduction. This was possible because of Moderna's mRNA platform, prior MERS work with NIH, digital infrastructure, and own manufacturing at Norwood. The COVID vaccine was Moderna's 10th vaccine candidate, not its first.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: According to Shane Ch 16, an organic organizational structure is BETTER than a mechanistic structure for innovation because:
options: It provides clear hierarchical authority that speeds up decision-making on innovation investments
Its standardized procedures reduce the uncertainty inherent in innovation projects
Its flexible roles, lateral communication, and few rules allow creative people to experiment without bureaucratic constraints
It uses specialized job descriptions that ensure each employee focuses on a narrow technical domain
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: Organic structures feature loosely defined jobs, informal procedures, cross-functional linkages, and mobilization by employee commitment. Research clearly shows organic structures outperform mechanistic ones for innovation. The other options describe MECHANISTIC traits: rigid hierarchy, standardized procedures, and specialized jobs.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: According to Shane (Ch 16, p.372), external equity financing for innovative firms involves:
options: Borrowing money from banks secured against the firm's physical assets
Selling ownership stakes in the firm to outside investors such as venture capitalists and angel investors
Receiving government grants that do not require repayment or equity dilution
Licensing the firm's patents to generate royalty income
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: External equity means selling ownership stakes (equity) to outside investors — VCs, angels, or through IPO. This contrasts with debt financing (borrowing from banks), which is generally not available to innovative firms due to uncertain cash flows, lack of collateral, and high failure rates.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: Adoption refers to the aggregate rate at which a new technology spreads across a market.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: False. DIFFUSION is the aggregate rate of adoption across a market. ADOPTION is the individual customer's decision to buy. The statement swaps the two definitions — adoption is micro (individual), diffusion is macro (market-level).

Final Prep Exam 1.q: In the specific phase of the Abernathy-Utterback model, product innovation dominates over process innovation.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: False. In the SPECIFIC phase, PROCESS innovation dominates (firms focus on efficiency, cost reduction, and optimizing the dominant design). PRODUCT innovation dominates in the FLUID phase, when many competing designs exist and there is no dominant design yet.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: In P&G's Connect and Develop model, the 'Develop' part refers to the external innovation sourced from outside partners.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: False. 'CONNECT' is the external part — finding and connecting with outside innovations. 'DEVELOP' is the internal part — using P&G's own capabilities (manufacturing, marketing, R&D) to develop those external ideas into market-ready products. P&G's internal capabilities are essential for development.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: Moderna designed its COVID-19 vaccine candidate in 42 days after the SARS-CoV-2 genetic sequence was posted online.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: False. Moderna designed the vaccine in TWO DAYS (January 11-13, 2020). The 42 days refers to the time from genome posting to SHIPPING the vaccine to NIH (February 24). The first clinical batch was manufactured by February 7 (25 days). These three milestones are commonly confused.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: According to Christensen (2015), Uber is a sustaining innovation relative to taxis because it launched in a well-served mainstream market with a superior service.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: True. Christensen explicitly classifies Uber as a sustaining innovation, not a disruptive one. Uber launched in San Francisco (well-served taxi market), targeted existing taxi users, and offered a service described as BETTER than taxis. Disruption requires starting in a low-end or new-market foothold — Uber did the opposite.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: According to Shane Ch 16, one of the four policies for linking R&D to development is using financial incentives to encourage researchers to consider commercial applications of their work.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: True. Shane identifies four policies for linking R&D to development: (1) financial incentives for researchers, (2) having researchers use the same equipment as development, (3) exposing researchers to development activities, and (4) ensuring R&D fits with overall corporate strategy.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: Nokia failed in the smartphone market primarily because it never developed a mobile operating system.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: False. Nokia HAD a mobile operating system — Symbian. It was the world's most widely used smartphone OS before iOS and Android. Nokia's failure was not the absence of an OS but the inability to evolve Symbian into a competitive smartphone platform with a strong app ecosystem. Nokia also had MeeGo in development.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: The large-to-small firm innovation ratio is approximately the same across all industries, with large firms consistently outperforming small firms in innovation output.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: False. The ratio varies dramatically by industry. In aircraft it is 31.0 (large firms dominate), in pharmaceuticals 9.2, but in measuring/controlling devices only 0.067 (small firms vastly outperform). Small firms are more innovative in younger, less capital-intensive industries with simpler products.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: During an industry shakeout, on average approximately 52% of firms exit the industry, though the range varies from as low as 4% (shampoo) to as high as 87% (DDT).
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: True. Shane cites data showing an average of 52% firm exit during shakeout. The range is wide: shampoo saw only 4% exit, while DDT saw 87%. Shakeouts occur after a dominant design emerges and the industry transitions from the fluid to the specific phase.

Final Prep Exam 1.q: A competence-enhancing innovation builds on existing knowledge and skills, while a competence-destroying innovation renders existing expertise obsolete — such as the shift from vacuum tubes to integrated circuits.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 1.explanation: True. Competence-enhancing innovations build on what firms already know (e.g., incremental chip improvements). Competence-destroying innovations require fundamentally different knowledge, making existing expertise obsolete. The vacuum tube to integrated circuit shift is the textbook example: glass-blowing and gas physics became irrelevant when semiconductor physics took over.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: According to Shane, which is NOT one of the six reasons incumbents are reluctant to shift to a new technology S-curve?
options: The new technology cannibalizes existing product sales
Managers fail to recognize the new technology as a competitive threat
Incumbents lack the financial resources to invest in the new technology
The firm's organizational structure and politics resist reorientation
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: Shane's six reasons include: (1) managers don't see the threat, (2) sunk costs in old tech, (3) cannibalization of existing sales, (4) incumbents can still improve old tech, (5) organizational politics resist change, (6) internal incentives favor existing products. Financial RESOURCES are typically NOT the problem — incumbents usually have plenty of money. Kodak invented the digital camera; the issue was willingness, not ability.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: The relationship between the adopter category bell curve (normal distribution) and the adoption S-curve is:
options: They represent completely different phenomena with no mathematical connection
The S-curve is the cumulative distribution function of the bell curve — each point on the S-curve represents the total proportion of adopters up to that time
The bell curve is derived by taking the derivative of the S-curve and then squaring it
The bell curve shows total adoption while the S-curve shows the rate of new adoption per period
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: The adoption S-curve is the cumulative form of the adopter category bell curve. If you plot new adopters per period, you get the bell-shaped normal distribution (innovators through laggards). If you plot TOTAL adopters over time (cumulative), you get the S-curve. The S-curve is the integral of the bell curve.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: When crossing the chasm, Geoffrey Moore recommends a 'whole product' strategy. This means:
options: Selling the core technology at a premium price to maximize revenue from early adopters before the chasm
Delivering a complete, turnkey solution — core product plus all necessary complementary products, services, and support — to satisfy pragmatic early majority buyers
Fragmenting the product into modular components that can be sold separately to different market segments
Focusing exclusively on reducing the product's price to make it affordable for mainstream consumers
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: The early majority (pragmatists) wants a complete solution that works out of the box, not a bare technology that requires assembly. The 'whole product' includes the core product plus installation, training, support, complementary products, and standards — everything needed for a pragmatic buyer to achieve their desired outcome.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: According to Shane (Ch 4), which of the following is NOT one of the four policies firms use to link R&D to development?
options: Providing financial incentives to encourage researchers to consider commercial applications
Having researchers use the same equipment as the development team
Hiring external consultants to bridge the gap between research and development teams
Exposing researchers to development activities to increase their awareness of commercial needs
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: Shane's four policies are: (1) financial incentives, (2) same equipment for R&D and development, (3) exposing researchers to development, (4) ensuring R&D fits with corporate strategy. Hiring external consultants is NOT listed. The policies focus on internal integration, not external bridging.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: A pharmaceutical company invests $10M in Phase 1 trials for a new drug. If Phase 1 succeeds, it can invest $100M in Phase 2. If Phase 1 fails, it walks away losing only $10M. This staged investment structure is BEST described as:
options: Net present value analysis with a 10% discount rate
A real option — the right but not the obligation to make the larger Phase 2 investment
The internal rate of return calculation for the combined Phase 1 and Phase 2 investment
An analytic hierarchy process comparing Phase 1 and Phase 2 as competing projects
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: This is a classic real option. The $10M Phase 1 investment buys the RIGHT (not obligation) to invest $100M in Phase 2. If Phase 1 reveals unfavorable data, the firm abandons the project, limiting losses. Real options are most valuable when uncertainty is high, potential outcomes are large, and investments can be staged.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: According to Shane (Ch 5), which of the following is a COST — not a benefit — of investing in innovation?
options: Creating a knowledge base that enables future innovations
Locking into particular strategies early, reducing strategic flexibility
Developing absorptive capacity to recognize and integrate external ideas
Building competitive advantage through first-mover effects
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: Shane identifies 'locking into particular strategies' as a COST of heavy R&D investment. Once a firm commits resources to a specific technology path, it becomes difficult and expensive to switch. This creates path dependency and reduces strategic flexibility. The other options are BENEFITS of innovation investment.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: A utility patent lasts 20 years, a trademark lasts 10 years and is renewable, and a copyright lasts for the author's life plus 70 years. Which statement about these durations is TRUE?
options: A trademark provides the strongest long-term protection because it can be renewed indefinitely as long as the mark remains in use
A patent provides the longest protection because 20 years exceeds the typical product lifecycle in most industries
Copyright provides the weakest protection because it is granted automatically without government examination
All three forms of IP expire after a fixed period with no possibility of extension
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: Trademarks can be renewed indefinitely (every 10 years) as long as the mark is in active use, making them potentially the longest-lasting IP protection. Patents are fixed at 20 years. Copyright is long (life + 70) but does eventually expire. Automatic granting (copyright) is about ease of acquisition, not strength.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: A researcher discovers that a naturally occurring enzyme in papaya can dissolve blood clots. She wants to patent the enzyme itself. Under patent law, this application would MOST LIKELY be:
options: Granted, because the enzyme has a clearly useful medical application
Denied, because natural substances cannot be patented — though a novel method of extracting or using the enzyme might be patentable
Granted as a plant patent, since the enzyme comes from a plant
Granted as a design patent, because the enzyme has a unique molecular structure
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: Natural substances as found in nature cannot be patented. However, a novel process for extracting, purifying, or using the enzyme could be patentable if it meets the requirements (useful, novel, non-obvious). Plant patents cover asexually reproduced plant varieties, not enzymes. Design patents cover ornamental designs, not molecular structures.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: Dell Computers revolutionized the PC industry not through technological innovation but through:
options: Developing a proprietary microprocessor that outperformed Intel chips
Building a direct-to-consumer, build-to-order model where customers paid in advance — a business model innovation
Creating the first graphical user interface for personal computers
Acquiring IBM's PC division and leveraging its existing distribution channels
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: Dell's innovation was in its business model: direct sales (eliminating retailer markup), build-to-order (reducing inventory), and customer prepayment (improving cash flow). Dell sold the same technology (Intel chips, Windows OS) but changed HOW it was sold. This is a non-technological/business model innovation.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: After receiving $5M in Series A funding, a startup founder hires her spouse as CFO at an inflated salary and uses company funds to lease a luxury car. This BEST illustrates:
options: Adverse selection — the founder misrepresented her qualifications before investment
Moral hazard — the founder takes excessive risk and extracts personal benefits because she bears limited consequences
The Valley of Death — the startup is running out of money between funding rounds
Skewness in outcomes — the VC fund expected some investments to behave this way
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: Moral hazard occurs AFTER investment when the entrepreneur takes actions not in the investor's interest because they are spending other people's money. The founder bears limited personal financial consequence. Adverse selection is PRE-investment information asymmetry. VCs protect against moral hazard through board seats, staged funding, and covenants.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: Nokia's market capitalization peaked at approximately $250 billion in 2000. By what year had it fallen to approximately $43 billion, and what was the PRIMARY cause?
options: By 2005, due to the rise of Chinese smartphone manufacturers offering cheaper alternatives
By 2011, due to failure to build a competitive software ecosystem and app platform to rival Apple's App Store
By 2008, due to the global financial crisis that affected all technology stocks equally
By 2013, due to patent litigation losses against Samsung and Apple
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: Nokia's market cap fell from $250B (2000) to $43B by around 2011. The primary cause was not hardware deficiency or financial crisis, but the failure to build a software ecosystem. Nokia's Ovi store failed to attract developers, while Apple's App Store created powerful indirect network effects. Nokia was eventually acquired by Microsoft in 2013.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: Moderna's IPO in December 2018 was notable because:
options: It valued Moderna at $2.5 billion and was the first mRNA company to go public
It raised $5.1 billion in a single offering, the largest biotech IPO in history
It valued Moderna at $7.5 billion, making it the biggest biotech IPO at the time, despite having ZERO products on market
It was cancelled at the last minute due to concerns about mRNA technology safety
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: Moderna's December 2018 IPO valued the company at $7.5 billion — the largest biotech IPO in history at that time. Remarkably, Moderna had never brought a single product to market. The $5.1B figure is total funding raised (not just the IPO). This illustrates extreme investor confidence in the mRNA platform's potential.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: Moderna's partnership with Lonza for COVID-19 vaccine manufacturing was remarkable because technology transfer was completed in under 3 months. Under normal circumstances, such a transfer typically takes:
options: 6-9 months
2-3 years
5-7 years
12-18 months
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: Normal pharmaceutical technology transfer takes 2-3 years. Moderna and Lonza completed it in under 3 months. This was possible because both companies were 'highly digital' — Moderna's paperless factory and digital manufacturing systems enabled rapid knowledge transfer. Juan Andres highlighted Lonza's digital capabilities and scale as the two key selection criteria.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: According to Shane Ch 16, the MAIN reason employees leave established firms to create spin-off companies is:
options: Spin-off founders typically lack the skills to work within large organizations
Employers do not share the rewards of innovation with the employees who created it
Government programs specifically incentivize employees to leave large firms and start new companies
Employees prefer the lower salaries and higher uncertainty of startup life
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: Shane states the main reason for spin-offs is that employers do NOT share rewards of innovation with employees. Xerox's policy against sharing profits led Robert Metcalfe to quit and found 3Com (exploiting Ethernet). Innovation based on human capital is more likely to lead to spin-offs because employers cannot prevent people from taking knowledge in their heads.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: According to Christensen (2015), if disruption theory is correct, what fate does Tesla MOST LIKELY face?
options: Tesla will disrupt the entire automotive industry within 5 years
Tesla will either be acquired by a larger incumbent or face a years-long battle for significant market share
Tesla will dominate the luxury car market but never reach mainstream consumers
Tesla will pivot to a low-end market strategy to become truly disruptive
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: Christensen argued that because Tesla entered at the HIGH END (not a disruptive foothold), it is following a sustaining innovation path. Historically, sustaining entrants either get acquired by incumbents or face a prolonged battle — only 6% of sustaining entrants succeeded in Christensen's disk drive study.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: Innovation and invention are the same thing — both refer to the creation of something entirely new.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: False. Invention is creating something entirely new. Innovation is the practical IMPLEMENTATION of an idea that is new to the market. Most inventions never become innovations. The distinction matters: Kodak invented the digital camera (invention) but failed to innovate (bring it to market successfully).

Final Prep Exam 2.q: Eli Lilly's marketers correctly identified that endocrinologists were the best proxy for mainstream insulin demand because they wrote the majority of insulin prescriptions.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: False. Endocrinologists were the MOST DEMANDING specialists, NOT the mainstream. General practitioners (GPs) wrote approximately 80% of insulin prescriptions. Lilly's error was weighting endocrinologists' opinions most heavily — these high-end customers wanted maximum purity, but the mainstream was already satisfied with existing insulin.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: After implementing Connect and Develop, P&G's R&D investment as a percentage of sales dropped from 4.8% to 3.4%, while their innovation success rate more than doubled.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: True. P&G's R&D as % of sales fell from 4.8% (2000) to 3.4% (2006), R&D productivity increased by nearly 60%, and innovation success rate more than doubled (from about 35%). This demonstrates that open innovation can reduce costs while improving outcomes — if the firm has strong internal capabilities.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: Research clearly shows that mechanistic organizational structures are better suited for radical innovation than organic structures.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: False. The OPPOSITE is true. Research clearly shows that ORGANIC structures are better for radical innovation. Organic structures feature flexible roles, lateral communication, few rules, and mobilization by commitment — all conducive to creativity. Mechanistic structures (rigid hierarchy, formal rules, specialized jobs) are better for efficiency and incremental innovation.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: Moderna started as 'project LS18' at Flagship Pioneering in 2010 in Cambridge, Massachusetts, and its CEO Stephane Bancel was recruited from the French diagnostics company bioMerieux.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: True. All details are correct: Moderna originated as project LS18 at Flagship Pioneering in 2010 in Cambridge, MA. Co-founders included Noubar Afeyan (Flagship CEO), with Robert Langer (MIT) and Derrick Rossi (Harvard). Bancel was recruited from bioMerieux and described Moderna as 'a technology company that happens to do biology.'

Final Prep Exam 2.q: According to Shane Ch 16, centralized R&D encourages radical innovation because decentralized managers tend to focus on incremental, unit-specific improvements.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: True. This is a key benefit of centralization. Decentralized managers focus on innovations that benefit their specific unit in the short term (incremental improvements), while centralized R&D can pursue larger, riskier, cross-cutting innovations that benefit the entire organization. Centralization also enables large-scale innovation that individual units are too small to undertake.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: Novo Nordisk beat Eli Lilly by competing on superior insulin purity, offering a product that was even purer than Lilly's genetically engineered Humulin.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: False. Novo Nordisk won by competing on CONVENIENCE (insulin pens), not purity. Their pen reduced injection time from 1-2 minutes to under 10 seconds. Novo commanded a 30% price premium on convenience, while Lilly's 25% premium on purity could not be sustained. The basis of competition shifted from purity to delivery method.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: A patent's 'working requirement' means the patent holder must demonstrate the invention works in a laboratory setting before the patent is granted.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: False. The working requirement means the patent holder must MANUFACTURE or USE the patented invention in the country where the patent was granted — not demonstrate it in a lab. Failure to 'work' the patent can result in compulsory licensing or revocation. This is about commercial use, not laboratory proof.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: Apple's concept of 'accountability without control' means that leaders are accountable for making a project succeed even though they do not control all the teams involved.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: True. This is a core concept from the Apple HBR article. In Apple's functional structure, no single function owns a product entirely — the camera system requires collaboration across 40+ specialist teams. Leaders must deliver results despite depending on teams outside their direct authority. 'Good mess' = teams work with shared purpose; 'bad mess' = teams push their own agendas.

Final Prep Exam 2.q: In the Nature (2023) study, a paper with a HIGH CD index is one where subsequent work that cites it is ALSO likely to cite its predecessors, indicating the paper is consolidating rather than disruptive.
options: True
False
Final Prep Exam 2.explanation: False. A HIGH CD index indicates the paper is DISRUPTIVE — subsequent work that cites it is LESS likely to also cite its predecessors (the new paper REPLACES older work). A LOW CD index means the paper is CONSOLIDATING — subsequent citations also cite predecessors (the paper BUILDS ON existing work). The statement describes a consolidating paper, which would have a LOW CD index.

Exam Sprint 1.q: Microsoft's Xbox console is classified as 'wholly proprietary' on Schilling's open-proprietary spectrum. Which strategic CONSEQUENCE follows most directly from this positioning?
options: Microsoft must manufacture all Xbox games internally
Microsoft controls the hardware architecture and can charge licensing fees to game developers
No third-party software can run on the Xbox platform
Xbox cannot benefit from network externalities
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: Being wholly proprietary means Microsoft controls the system architecture (architectural control, Schilling p.211). Game developers must license from Microsoft. This does NOT mean no third-party software — Xbox has 'limited licensing' for game developers. The proprietary position enables licensing revenue and platform control.

Exam Sprint 1.q: Dell's build-to-order model generated competitive advantage through negative cash conversion. This means Dell:
options: Received payment from customers before paying its suppliers, effectively using customer money to finance operations
Operated at a loss for its first decade to gain market share
Used venture capital to subsidize below-cost pricing
Patented its supply chain process to prevent competitor imitation
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: Dell's business model innovation was about cash flow: customers paid upfront, components were ordered after, and suppliers were paid later. This negative cash conversion cycle meant Dell's operations were partially financed by customers — a business model innovation, not a product or process innovation in the traditional sense.

Exam Sprint 1.q: During the early phase of a technology S-curve (Shane p.22), progress is slow because:
options: Government regulations prevent rapid development
The fundamental principles are not yet well understood, so each advance requires experimentation rather than systematic application
Firms deliberately withhold innovations to maximize patent value
Consumer demand is too low to justify R&D investment
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: The early phase of the S-curve is slow because the underlying science and engineering principles are not yet well understood. Progress requires trial-and-error experimentation. Once fundamental knowledge accumulates, the rapid middle phase begins as advances can be made more systematically.

Exam Sprint 1.q: Shane (p.25) identifies six reasons incumbents are reluctant to shift to a new technology S-curve. Which combination correctly pairs TWO of these reasons?
options: Lack of awareness of the new technology AND excessive government regulation
Investments in existing technology AND customer relationships tied to current technology
Patent thickets blocking entry AND lack of engineering talent
Hostile acquisitions by new entrants AND declining market demand
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: Two key reasons from Shane's six: (1) incumbents have sunk investments in existing technology (equipment, training, expertise) and (2) their customer relationships are built around the current technology. Other reasons include organizational inertia, fear of cannibalizing existing products, and difficulty recognizing the potential of nascent technologies.

Exam Sprint 1.q: A research team at a pharmaceutical company conducts fundamental studies on how mRNA interacts with human ribosomes, specifically because they want to design better mRNA-based drugs. In Stokes' model, this research falls into:
options: Bohr's quadrant — pure basic research with no practical application in mind
Edison's quadrant — purely applied research with no interest in fundamental understanding
Pasteur's quadrant — use-inspired basic research that seeks both fundamental understanding AND practical application
The unnamed fourth quadrant — neither basic nor applied
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: Pasteur's quadrant describes research motivated by BOTH the quest for fundamental understanding AND considerations of use. The mRNA-ribosome research seeks deep scientific knowledge (basic) but is motivated by a practical goal (better drugs). Bohr's quadrant would be pure curiosity-driven research; Edison's would be pure problem-solving without caring about underlying science.

Exam Sprint 1.q: According to Shane (p.49) and Moore's chasm theory, a technology that successfully crosses the chasm enters the early majority. Which characteristic of the early majority makes them FUNDAMENTALLY different from early adopters as a marketing target?
options: They are less educated and cannot understand complex products
They require references from OTHER PRAGMATISTS, not visionaries, creating a 'catch-22' where you need pragmatist customers to get pragmatist customers
They only buy products that have been on the market for at least five years
They refuse to pay premium prices under any circumstances
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: The fundamental challenge of the chasm is that the early majority (pragmatists) want references from other pragmatists — NOT from early adopters (visionaries). But you cannot get pragmatist references without pragmatist customers first. This 'catch-22' is what makes the chasm so dangerous. The beachhead strategy solves this by dominating one small segment first.

Exam Sprint 1.q: The adopter category bell curve (normal distribution) and the adoption S-curve represent the SAME underlying data. The S-curve is the _____ of the bell curve:
options: Mirror image
Derivative
Cumulative distribution function
Logarithmic transformation
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: The adoption S-curve is the cumulative distribution function of the adopter bell curve. Each point on the S-curve represents the TOTAL percentage of adopters up to that time. When you add up (integrate) the bell curve of individual adoptions over time, you get the S-shaped cumulative curve. The steep middle section of the S-curve corresponds to the peak of the bell curve.

Exam Sprint 1.q: Shane (p.83) distinguishes applied research from basic research. A company invests in developing a more efficient manufacturing process for lipid nanoparticles, without attempting to understand the underlying physics of lipid self-assembly. This is:
options: Basic research — it advances scientific knowledge
Applied research — it solves a specific practical problem without seeking fundamental understanding
Pasteur's quadrant research — it combines basic and applied goals
Development — it is too close to commercialization to be called research
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: Applied research (Edison's quadrant) aims to solve practical problems without necessarily seeking fundamental understanding of the underlying science. The company wants a better manufacturing process (practical goal) but is not trying to understand the physics of lipid self-assembly (no basic research motivation). If they also cared about the underlying physics, it would be Pasteur's quadrant.

Exam Sprint 1.q: Shane (p.75) describes General Purpose Technologies (GPTs) as technologies that transform entire economies. Which characteristic distinguishes a GPT from a merely important technology?
options: A GPT must be patented and owned by a single firm
A GPT has pervasive use across many sectors AND continuous improvement potential AND spawns complementary innovations
A GPT must originate from government-funded research
A GPT always follows an exponential growth trajectory like Moore's Law
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: GPTs have three defining characteristics: (1) pervasive use across many sectors of the economy, (2) inherent potential for continuous technical improvement, and (3) innovational complementarities — they spawn further innovations in application sectors. Examples include electricity, the internet, and steam power. Not all important technologies qualify — they must affect MANY sectors.

Exam Sprint 1.q: Shane (p.88) identifies four policies firms use to link R&D to development. Which of the following is one of these policies?
options: Requiring all researchers to hold MBA degrees
Using financial incentives to encourage researchers to consider commercial applications
Outsourcing all applied research to university labs
Prohibiting researchers from interacting with marketing teams to prevent bias
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: One of Shane's four policies for linking R&D to development is using financial incentives (e.g., bonuses, stock options) to encourage researchers to think about the commercial applications of their work. Other policies include rotating personnel between R&D and operating units, using cross-functional teams, and locating R&D near business units.

Exam Sprint 1.q: The Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP, Shane p.103) is MOST useful when a firm faces which type of decision?
options: A binary go/no-go decision on a single project with clear financial projections
Choosing among multiple innovation projects that must be evaluated on several qualitative AND quantitative criteria simultaneously
Determining the optimal patent filing strategy across multiple jurisdictions
Calculating the exact NPV of a project with uncertain but estimable cash flows
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: AHP is most useful when comparing multiple alternatives across multiple criteria that include BOTH qualitative and quantitative dimensions. It structures complex decisions by decomposing them into a hierarchy of criteria and sub-criteria, then using pairwise comparisons. NPV handles purely financial analysis; AHP handles multi-criteria decisions where not all factors are easily quantifiable.

Exam Sprint 1.q: A biotech firm invests $5M in Phase 1 trials for a cancer drug. If Phase 1 shows promise, it will invest $50M in Phase 2. If Phase 1 fails, it abandons the project. An NPV analysis shows a negative expected value of -$3M. The firm decides to proceed anyway. This decision is BEST justified by:
options: The sunk cost fallacy — the firm has already invested too much to stop
Real options reasoning — the Phase 1 investment buys the OPTION to invest in Phase 2, and this flexibility has value not captured by NPV
Moral hazard — management is taking excessive risks with investor money
Adverse selection — the firm has private information that the drug will succeed
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: Real options thinking (Shane p.106) recognizes that staged investments create valuable flexibility. The $5M Phase 1 investment is not just an expenditure — it purchases the RIGHT (but not the obligation) to make the larger Phase 2 investment. If Phase 1 fails, the firm loses only $5M, not $55M. This asymmetric payoff structure has positive value that standard NPV fails to capture.

Exam Sprint 1.q: A venture capitalist requires convertible preferred stock, board seats, and staged funding milestones before investing. These contractual provisions primarily address which financing friction?
options: Only adverse selection — they help the VC determine the quality of the venture
Only moral hazard — they prevent the entrepreneur from misusing funds
BOTH adverse selection and moral hazard — they provide information AND align incentives
Neither — they are standard business practices unrelated to financing frictions
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: These provisions address BOTH frictions. Board seats and staged milestones help mitigate MORAL HAZARD by monitoring and controlling the entrepreneur's behavior. Due diligence and convertible preferred stock help address ADVERSE SELECTION by protecting against information asymmetry. In practice, VC contractual terms are designed to combat both frictions simultaneously.

Exam Sprint 1.q: According to Schilling (p.211), architectural control over a technology platform provides competitive advantage because the firm that controls the architecture:
options: Can prevent all competitors from entering the market permanently
Can set the standards that complementors must follow, influencing the direction of the entire technology ecosystem
Automatically receives patent protection for all components within the architecture
Is guaranteed to maintain its market position indefinitely
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: Architectural control means the firm defines the rules of the platform — what interfaces look like, what standards complementors must meet, and how components interact. This allows the firm to guide the ecosystem's evolution in ways that favor its own products and business model (e.g., Apple controlling iOS). It does NOT guarantee permanent dominance or automatic patent protection.

Exam Sprint 1.q: A software company develops a novel algorithm for detecting fraud in financial transactions. The algorithm's effectiveness depends on the specific mathematical approach, which is documented in the source code. Which IP strategy creates the STRONGEST long-term protection?
options: Patent the algorithm — patents provide 20 years of protection and cover the underlying process
Copyright the source code — copyright is automatic and lasts life + 70 years
Keep it as a trade secret — if the algorithm cannot be reverse-engineered from the product's behavior, secrecy provides potentially indefinite protection
File for a trademark on the algorithm's name — trademarks are renewable indefinitely
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: For a software algorithm that is not observable in use (users see fraud detection results, not the algorithm itself), trade secrecy can provide indefinite protection. Copyright only protects the specific expression of code, not the underlying mathematical approach. Software patents are notoriously difficult to enforce (effectiveness 3.4/7 for computers). A trademark protects only the name, not the technology. Since the algorithm is not 'observable in use,' secrecy is optimal.

Exam Sprint 1.q: According to Shane (p.98), debt financing is problematic for innovation projects primarily because loan repayment schedules require regular cash flows, which most innovative ventures cannot generate in their early stages.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: True. Debt financing requires predictable, regular cash flows to service interest and principal payments. Innovative ventures typically have negative cash flows early on, uncertain revenue timing, and high failure rates. This mismatch between debt requirements and innovation characteristics is why equity financing (angels, VC) is generally necessary for innovation projects.

Exam Sprint 1.q: The AutoStore vs Ocado patent dispute illustrates the same strategic pattern as Selden vs Ford: a firm using broad patents to restrict competitors' market entry, demonstrating that patent strategy battles remain fundamentally unchanged over a century.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: True. Both cases show the same fundamental dynamic: using patent rights strategically to block competition. Selden used a broad automobile patent to extract licensing fees from early automakers (1895-1911). AutoStore vs Ocado follows the same pattern with warehouse automation patents. The strategic use (and potential abuse) of broad patents to control market entry persists across eras.

Exam Sprint 1.q: According to Schilling (p.209), a copyright can protect a novel manufacturing method, provided the method is described in a written document that constitutes an original creative work.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: False. Copyright protects the EXPRESSION of ideas (the specific written document), NOT the underlying method or process itself. Even if a manufacturing method is described in a copyrighted document, competitors are free to use the METHOD — they just cannot copy the specific DOCUMENT. To protect a method, you need a patent (process patent) or trade secrecy.

Exam Sprint 1.q: According to Schilling (p.203), a utility patent protects new and useful processes, machines, manufactured items, or compositions of matter, and lasts for 20 years from the filing date. Mathematical formulas and natural laws, however, cannot be patented.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: True. This accurately describes patent scope. Utility patents cover processes, machines, manufactured items, and compositions of matter ('recipes') for 20 years. Natural laws, mathematical formulas, and abstract ideas are explicitly excluded from patentability. This distinction is important: you can patent a specific APPLICATION of a natural law, but not the law itself.

Exam Sprint 1.q: According to Shane (p.364), an organic organizational structure is characterized by loosely controlled, nonhierarchical communication and unspecialized jobs, and research clearly shows it is BETTER for radical innovation than a mechanistic structure.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: True. Shane clearly states that organic structures — with loose controls, informal communication, cross-functional linkages, and employee commitment rather than authority-based mobilization — are better for radical innovation. Research consistently supports this. Mechanistic structures (tight controls, hierarchy, specialized jobs, many written rules) are better for efficiency and incremental innovation.

Exam Sprint 1.q: According to Shane (p.361), a key DISADVANTAGE of decentralized R&D is that decentralized managers tend to focus on radical, high-risk innovations because they are freed from headquarters oversight.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: False. The statement reverses the actual disadvantage. Decentralized managers tend to focus on INCREMENTAL, unit-specific improvements — NOT radical innovation. They optimize for their local unit's needs and short-term performance. It is CENTRALIZED R&D that encourages radical innovation because central management can take a broader, longer-term perspective and invest in large-scale, cross-unit projects.

Exam Sprint 1.q: According to Shane (p.372), external equity financing involves selling ownership shares in the firm to outside investors, which dilutes the founders' control but avoids the cash flow burden of debt repayment schedules.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: True. External equity financing (from angels, VCs, or public markets) requires selling ownership stakes, which dilutes founders' ownership and control. However, unlike debt, equity does not require regular interest/principal payments, making it suitable for innovative ventures with uncertain and irregular cash flows. The trade-off is control dilution versus financial flexibility.

Exam Sprint 1.q: Nokia's primary strategic failure was that it never developed any smartphone technology — the company was caught completely off-guard by the smartphone revolution and had no touchscreen or app-related projects.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: False. Nokia DID develop smartphone technology — it launched the Nokia 7710 touchscreen phone in 2004, three years BEFORE the iPhone. It also had Symbian OS and the Ovi app store. The problem was not lack of technology but organizational inertia, atmosphere of fear, over-focus on hardware, and failure to execute on the software/ecosystem dimension. Nokia's failure was organizational, not technological.

Exam Sprint 1.q: Moderna's 'digital building blocks' pyramid consists of (from bottom to top): Cloud, Integration, Automation & Robotics, Analytics, and Artificial Intelligence — with approximately 85% of company-specific tools being custom-built rather than off-the-shelf.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: True. Moderna's digital technology stack follows this pyramid: Cloud (AWS since 2013) at the base, then Integration (data harmonized, IoT-connected lab instruments), Automation & Robotics, Analytics, and AI at the top. About 85% of their company-specific tools are custom-built, while 85% of undifferentiated tools (like HR software) are off-the-shelf SaaS solutions.

Exam Sprint 1.q: According to Shane (p.24), when a new technology is first introduced, it typically performs WORSE than the established technology it aims to replace, which is one reason incumbents tend to dismiss it.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 1.explanation: True. New technologies typically start at the bottom of their own S-curve, performing worse than mature technologies that are near the top of theirs. This initial performance gap gives incumbents a rational-seeming reason to dismiss the new technology. However, if the new technology's S-curve has a higher ceiling, it will eventually surpass the old one — but only after incumbents have lost their window to adapt.

Exam Sprint 2.q: In P&G's Connect & Develop model, for every 100 ideas sourced from outside the company, approximately how many end up as products in the market?
options: 10 — C&D has a 10% success rate
1 — the screening and development process is extremely selective
25 — one quarter of external ideas reach commercialization
50 — matching the company's 50% external innovation target
