Exam Sprint 2.explanation: According to the Huston & Sakkab HBR article, for every 100 ideas found outside P&G, only 1 ends up in the market. This reflects the rigorous screening process: ideas must solve a defined problem, have evidence of working (prototype or working product), and pass 'where-to-play' criteria before entering the development pipeline.

Exam Sprint 2.q: Which type of firm would be LEAST suited to adopt P&G's Connect & Develop model?
options: A large consumer goods company with strong brand equity and global distribution
An early-stage startup that has not yet defined its core technology or target market
A pharmaceutical company with extensive internal R&D and regulatory expertise
A technology company with strong patent portfolio and manufacturing capabilities
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: C&D requires clearly defined needs ('where-to-play' criteria), strong internal R&D capabilities to absorb and develop external ideas (absorptive capacity), and infrastructure to screen and manage external idea flow. A startup that has NOT yet defined what it is looking for lacks all three prerequisites. C&D is about finding solutions to defined problems, not discovering what problems to solve.

Exam Sprint 2.q: The insulin purity trajectory shows impure parts per million declining from 50,000 ppm (1925) to 10 ppm (1980) to 0 ppm with Humulin. What was the approximate purity level in 1950?
options: 100 ppm
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: The purity trajectory: 50,000 ppm (1925) -> 10,000 ppm (1950) -> 10 ppm (1980) -> 0 ppm (Humulin). The trajectory shows rapid improvement in the middle decades, but by 1980, purity at 10 ppm was already more than sufficient for the mainstream market. Lilly's push to 0 ppm represented overshooting — performance exceeding the trajectory of market need.

Exam Sprint 2.q: Lilly's marketers surveyed physicians about the need for genetically engineered human insulin. Their critical mistake was:
options: Surveying too few physicians to achieve statistical significance
Weighting endocrinologists' opinions most heavily, even though they were the MOST demanding customers and did not represent mainstream prescribing patterns
Ignoring physician feedback entirely and relying only on laboratory data
Surveying only European physicians while targeting the American market
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: Lilly put the GREATEST WEIGHT on endocrinologists — the leading specialists who saw the most severe cases of insulin resistance. But these were the most demanding, high-end customers. The mainstream market (GPs, who wrote the majority of prescriptions) was already satisfied with existing animal insulin purity. This is exactly Christensen's point: listening to your most demanding customers leads to overshooting mainstream needs.

Exam Sprint 2.q: According to Christensen, Raynor & McDonald (HBR 2015), the precise definition of disruptive innovation requires that the entrant:
options: Introduces a technologically superior product that incumbents cannot match
Targets overlooked segments with a simpler or cheaper offering, gains a foothold, then moves upmarket into the mainstream
Destroys the incumbent through aggressive pricing and marketing
Creates a product that is immediately adopted by the mass market
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: Christensen's precise definition: a PROCESS where a smaller company targets overlooked segments (low-end or new-market foothold) with a 'good enough' offering, gains a foothold while incumbents focus on their most profitable customers, then moves UPMARKET. When mainstream customers adopt the entrant's offering in volume, disruption has occurred. It is NOT about technological superiority or immediate mass-market success.

Exam Sprint 2.q: In Christensen's study of the disk drive industry, only 6% of sustaining entrants succeeded. The strategic implication of this statistic is that:
options: The disk drive industry is uniquely hostile to new entrants
New entrants should generally AVOID directly challenging incumbents on performance dimensions the incumbents already excel at
Incumbents should always acquire sustaining entrants to prevent competition
Technology markets are essentially impossible for new firms to enter
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: The 6% success rate for sustaining entrants is not unique to disk drives — it illustrates a general principle. When entrants compete head-on against incumbents in the dimensions incumbents already serve well, the incumbent's advantages (brand, distribution, customer relationships, resources) overwhelm the entrant. The strategic implication: entrants should seek disruptive paths (overlooked segments) rather than sustaining ones.

Exam Sprint 2.q: Christensen argues Uber is NOT disruptive because it moved in the OPPOSITE direction of disruption. Specifically, Uber:
options: Started with a low-quality service that gradually improved over time
Launched in an underserved rural market and expanded to cities
Built its position in the mainstream market FIRST (San Francisco), offering a BETTER service than taxis, then expanded to overlooked segments
Created an entirely new market by serving nonconsumers who had never used transportation services
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: Uber launched in San Francisco — a well-served taxi market — targeting existing taxi users with a SUPERIOR service (not inferior). It then expanded to other cities and eventually to more price-sensitive segments (UberX, UberPool). This is the OPPOSITE of disruption: disruption starts at the bottom or in new markets and moves UP. Uber started in the mainstream and expanded outward. It is a sustaining innovation.

Exam Sprint 2.q: In Apple's discretionary leadership model (Podolny & Hansen, HBR 2020), VPs allocate approximately 40% of their time to 'owning' and 30% to 'learning.' This contrasts with general managers at most companies, who spend most of their time in:
options: The 'owning' box — deeply involved in their area of highest expertise
The 'learning' box — constantly acquiring new domain knowledge
The 'teaching' box — training subordinates in established practices
The 'delegating' box — overseeing activities outside their expertise without deep involvement
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: The key contrast: Apple VPs spend most time in owning (40%) and learning (30%) — directly engaging with details in their areas of expertise or actively building new expertise. General managers at other companies spend most time in the delegating box — they manage people and budgets without deep domain expertise. Apple's model requires leaders to be experts, not just managers.

Exam Sprint 2.q: Moderna shipped its COVID-19 vaccine candidate to the NIH in 42 days after the viral genome was posted. The PREVIOUS fastest timeline for developing a vaccine candidate was approximately:
options: 6 months for the Ebola vaccine
20 months for the SARS vaccine — meaning Moderna achieved a 90% reduction in development time
12 months for the H1N1 influenza vaccine
3 months for the Zika vaccine
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: The previous record was approximately 20 months for SARS. Moderna's 42-day timeline represented approximately a 90% reduction in development time. This was enabled by the mRNA platform (vaccine designed in 2 days), prior experience with coronaviruses (MERS work with NIH), pre-existing relationships with Fauci/NIH, and their vertically integrated digital manufacturing at Norwood.

Exam Sprint 2.q: The STRONGEST argument AGAINST spinning off Moderna's COVID-19 business is:
options: Spinoffs are illegal for biotech companies under FDA regulations
A spinoff would create organizational silos, undermining the integrated platform model where learnings from one mRNA drug spill into all others
The COVID-19 vaccine was not profitable enough to sustain a standalone company
Moderna had already contractually committed to keeping all operations unified
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: Moderna's competitive advantage comes from its integrated platform — learnings from each mRNA drug improve all others. Spinning off COVID would create the very SILOS that Moderna was designed to avoid. The digital infrastructure, Norwood plant, and integrated data systems serve ALL programs. Bancel noted they had 'seen the detrimental effects of such organizational structures in big pharma.' Separating COVID would break the virtuous cross-learning cycle.

Exam Sprint 2.q: Moderna's 'born digital' operating model differs from traditional pharma's approach to digital transformation in a fundamental way. Specifically:
options: Moderna uses only open-source software while traditional pharma uses proprietary systems
Traditional pharma digitizes existing manual processes; Moderna redesigned its processes FROM THE START to be digital-native, as articulated by Damiani's principle
Moderna outsources all digital operations to cloud providers while traditional pharma keeps IT in-house
Traditional pharma uses AI extensively while Moderna relies primarily on human expertise
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: The key distinction is Damiani's principle: 'You cannot blindly translate manual processes into digital processes. Processes must be redesigned to fit a digital environment.' Traditional pharma takes existing paper-based, sequential processes and tries to digitize them. Moderna was built from day one with digital processes — the 2012 anecdote about the scientist manually entering nucleotide sequences in Excel horrified Bancel and led to creating the 'drug design studio.'

Exam Sprint 2.q: The Nature (2023) study by Park, Leahey & Funk found that the CD index for scientific papers has been declining since the mid-20th century. Which explanation does the article suggest is MOST important?
options: Scientists have become less intelligent over time
Government funding for science has decreased dramatically
The division of science into ever-narrower specialties AND publication incentives that steer researchers away from risk-taking
All the important discoveries have already been made
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: The article identifies multiple factors: (1) division of science into narrower specialties (silos), (2) publication incentives and metrics-driven evaluation that steer away from risk-taking (safer to keep the grant-publication-citation cycle going), and (3) knowledge/information overload. Importantly, the ABSOLUTE number of disruptive papers stayed roughly constant — it is the PROPORTION that declined because total output exploded.

Exam Sprint 2.q: According to Vaclav Smil (WSJ 2023), Moore's Law-style exponential growth is the EXCEPTION in technology, not the rule. Which data point BEST supports his argument?
options: Smartphone sales have plateaued since 2017
Battery energy density grew from 25 Wh/kg (1900) to ~300 Wh/kg (2022) — only 12x increase over 122 years, or about 2% per year
The number of patent filings has decreased since 2010
Internet speeds have doubled every year since 2000
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: Smil's core argument is that fundamental economic activities show only slow, linear progress — agriculture (~1%/year), energy (~1.5%/year), steel production (<2%/year). Battery energy density growing only 12x in 122 years (2%/year) starkly contrasts with microchip components increasing 10 million+ times since 1971 (~35%/year). The exponential growth of computing has NO COUNTERPART in agriculture, energy, transportation, or large engineering.

Exam Sprint 2.q: When Stephen Elop became Nokia's CEO in 2010, he faced three strategic options. He chose to partner with Microsoft, which ultimately led to:
options: Nokia becoming the world's leading smartphone manufacturer by 2015
Nokia's phone division being sold to Microsoft in 2013, essentially ending Nokia's consumer phone business
Nokia successfully competing against both Apple and Samsung through Windows Phone
Microsoft acquiring Nokia's patent portfolio while Nokia retained its phone business
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: Elop chose option 3 (partner with Microsoft), adopting Windows Phone as Nokia's primary smartphone OS. This strategy failed — Windows Phone never gained significant market share. Nokia's phone division was eventually sold to Microsoft in 2013. This decision illustrates how choosing the wrong strategic response to disruption can accelerate decline rather than reverse it.

Exam Sprint 2.q: In the Selden vs Ford case, Selden was a patent attorney who filed the first US automobile patent in 1879 but strategically delayed issuance until 1895. Ford ultimately won on appeal because:
options: Selden's patent had expired by the time the case reached appeal
Ford proved Selden had never built a working automobile
Ford used a fundamentally different engine type than what Selden's patent covered
The Supreme Court ruled that automobile patents were unconstitutional
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: Ford won the appeal in 1911 because his Model T used a different engine type (a four-cycle engine) than what Selden's patent specifically covered (which was based on the Brayton two-stroke engine). The lesson: broad patents can be circumvented through alternative technical approaches. This same dynamic plays out in modern patent disputes like AutoStore vs Ocado in warehouse automation.

Exam Sprint 2.q: In P&G's Connect & Develop model, internal R&D employees receive LOWER rewards for innovations sourced externally compared to internally developed innovations, which helps motivate internal research efforts.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: False. P&G gives the SAME rewards whether innovation comes from inside or outside. In fact, the reward system actually FAVORS external ideas because they move faster to market, and speed is rewarded. P&G deliberately designed equal rewards to prevent 'Not Invented Here' (NIH) syndrome among internal researchers.

Exam Sprint 2.q: Eli Lilly invested nearly $1 billion developing Humulin (genetically engineered human insulin via Genentech), but the product could not sustain its 25% price premium over animal insulin because the mainstream market was already satisfied with pork insulin purity levels.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: True. This is a precise summary of the Humulin case. Lilly invested ~$1B and priced Humulin at a 25% premium, but the market response was tepid. The market was 'not terribly dissatisfied with pork insulin.' Only a 'fraction of a per cent' of diabetics had immune resistance to animal insulin. Meanwhile, Novo Nordisk's insulin pen (a convenience innovation) easily sustained a 30% premium.

Exam Sprint 2.q: According to Christensen (HBR 2015), Tesla could still become a disruptive innovator if it successfully moves from the high-end market downward to mass-market vehicles, because disruption theory predicts this top-down path can succeed.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: False. Disruption theory specifically describes a BOTTOM-UP path: starting in overlooked segments and moving upmarket. A top-down path (starting premium and moving down) is the OPPOSITE of disruption — it is a sustaining innovation trajectory. Christensen predicts that if disruption theory is correct, Tesla faces either acquisition by a larger incumbent or a years-long battle for market significance. Moving downmarket does NOT make it disruptive.

Exam Sprint 2.q: The two primary frictions that make financing innovation difficult are adverse selection (entrepreneurs know more about their innovation than investors) and moral hazard (entrepreneurs may take excessive risks with investor money). VCs address BOTH through contractual mechanisms like staged funding and board seats.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: True. Adverse selection and moral hazard are the two primary financing frictions. VCs address them through multiple contractual mechanisms: convertible preferred stock and due diligence address adverse selection (information asymmetry); staged funding, board seats, covenants, and milestone requirements address moral hazard (incentive misalignment). These mechanisms work together to bridge the information gap and align interests.

Exam Sprint 2.q: According to the Apple HBR article, the PRIMARY reason Apple uses a functional structure is to avoid the high administrative costs of running multiple divisions, since a single P&L is cheaper to manage.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: False. The primary reason is NOT cost savings. The fundamental principle is to ALIGN EXPERTISE AND DECISION RIGHTS — ensuring that those with the deepest knowledge in a domain make the key decisions. In a multidivisional structure, general managers make technology decisions based on divisional P&L, which leads to short-term thinking and lack of technical depth. Apple's functional structure ensures 'experts lead experts.'

Exam Sprint 2.q: Before the COVID-19 pandemic, Moderna had raised over $5.1 billion in funding, including $750 million from strategic partners, and its December 2018 IPO was the biggest biotech IPO in history at that time, valued at $7.5 billion.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: True. All these figures are precisely correct from the Moderna case. The $5.1B in total funding, $750M from strategic partners, and the December 2018 IPO at $7.5B valuation (largest biotech IPO at the time) — all before COVID-19 demonstrated the commercial potential of the mRNA platform. This massive pre-pandemic investment reflected the scale of Moderna's ambition and the platform's potential across multiple drug programs.

Exam Sprint 2.q: The Nature (2023) study found that the absolute number of disruptive scientific papers has dramatically declined since the mid-20th century, indicating that science is fundamentally running out of breakthrough discoveries to make.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: False. The ABSOLUTE number of disruptive papers has stayed BROADLY THE SAME. What declined is the PROPORTION — the share of disruptive papers relative to the total. The share dropped because total papers, patents, and funding exploded. The article does NOT conclude that science is 'running out' of discoveries. The decline in proportion may reflect publication incentives, specialization, and information overload rather than exhaustion of possibilities.

Exam Sprint 2.q: Novo Nordisk's insulin pen reduced injection time from 1-2 minutes to under 10 seconds, and the pen commanded a 30% price premium per unit — easily sustained by the market, unlike Lilly's 25% premium for Humulin.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: True. These are exact figures from the Christensen insulin case. The traditional injection process took 1-2 minutes; Novo's pen reduced this to under 10 seconds. The pen's 30% premium was easily sustained because the market valued CONVENIENCE over additional purity. Lilly's 25% Humulin premium could NOT be sustained because purity was already 'good enough.' The basis of competition had shifted from purity to delivery method.

Exam Sprint 2.q: P&G's Connect & Develop network leveraged the company's suppliers, who had a combined R&D staff of approximately 50,000 people — more than six times P&G's own 7,500 researchers and support staff.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: True. P&G had 7,500 researchers and support staff internally. Their supplier network had an estimated combined R&D staff of 50,000 people. Additionally, P&G deployed 70 'technology entrepreneurs' around the world to mine scientific literature, patents, and databases. This vastly expanded the idea pool beyond what internal R&D alone could generate, which was the core strategic logic of C&D.

Exam Sprint 2.q: According to Christensen (HBR 2015), the iPhone was disruptive to the SMARTPHONE market when it launched in 2007, replacing incumbents like Nokia and BlackBerry through a classic low-end disruption path.
options: True
False
Exam Sprint 2.explanation: False. Christensen explicitly states the iPhone launched as a SUSTAINING innovation in the smartphone market — it targeted the same customers as Nokia and BlackBerry and won on product superiority, not through a low-end or new-market foothold. However, the iPhone WAS disruptive — not to smartphones, but to LAPTOPS as the primary internet access device, through the App Store business model. This is a nuanced but crucial distinction.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: In the Schumpeterian cycle, what role do entrepreneurs play?
options: They maintain economic equilibrium by optimizing existing processes
They break economic equilibrium by introducing radical innovations
They consolidate industries through mergers and acquisitions
They slow down innovation to protect market stability
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: Entrepreneurs break 'economic equilibrium' with radical innovation. The Schumpeterian cycle goes: evolution (incremental) → revolution (radical) → repeat. Entrepreneurs are the 'devil' side that disrupts the status quo.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: According to the Edelson Institute framework, which technologies defined the 3rd innovation wave (~1900)?
options: Water power, textiles, iron
Steam power, rail, steel
Electricity, chemicals, internal combustion engine
Petrochemicals, electronics, aviation
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: The 3rd wave (~1900, 50 years) was defined by electricity, chemicals, and the internal combustion engine. Water/textiles/iron = 1st wave. Steam/rail/steel = 2nd wave. Petrochemicals/electronics/aviation = 4th wave.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: What trend is observed in the duration of innovation waves from the 1st to the 6th?
options: They remain constant at approximately 50 years each
They alternate between long and short periods
They are getting progressively shorter, from ~60 years to ~25 years
They are getting progressively longer as technologies become more complex
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: Innovation waves are getting shorter: 1st wave ~60yr, 2nd ~55yr, 3rd ~50yr, 4th ~40yr, 5th ~30yr, 6th ~25yr. This acceleration reflects faster technology development and diffusion cycles.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: The 6th innovation wave (~2020) is characterized by which combination of technologies?
options: Digital networks, software, new media
AI & IoT, robots & drones, clean tech
Blockchain, quantum computing, space tourism
Nanotechnology, biotechnology, 3D printing
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: The 6th wave (~2020, ~25yr) is defined by AI & IoT, robots & drones, and clean tech. Digital networks/software/new media was the 5th wave (~1990). The slide conclusion states we are leaving the 'IT Age' and entering the 'AI Age.'

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: Before the mid-19th century, what percentage of patenting inventors had NO formal scientific training?
options: About 40%
About 60%
About 80%
About 95%
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: 80% of patenting inventors had no formal scientific training in the pre-mid-19th century era. Innovation was democratized with low barriers to entry and 'technical simplicity,' dominated by the 'lonely inventor.'

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: What happened in 1876 that marked a major shift in how innovation was organized?
options: The first patent law was enacted in the United States
The first industrial research laboratories were established (Edison, Bell)
Universities began granting science degrees for the first time
The first government-funded R&D program was launched
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: 1876 saw the first industrial labs — Edison at Menlo Park and Alexander Graham Bell. This began the shift from individual 'lonely inventors' to collective/institutional R&D. By 1880-1890, institutionalization spread to Europe (pharma/dye labs in Germany).

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: The S-curve (Leibniz-S) consists of three phases. What are they, in order?
options: Disruption Phase, Consolidation Phase, Decline Phase
Initial Phase (Flat Start), Growth Phase (Steep Rise), Maturity Phase (Plateau)
Discovery Phase, Patent Phase, Market Phase
Concept Phase, Development Phase, Diffusion Phase
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: The S-curve (also called Leibniz-S) has three named phases: Initial Phase (Flat Start), Growth Phase (Steep Rise), and Maturity Phase (Plateau). Incumbents tend to stay on the current S-curve while disruptors initiate new ones.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: On the S-curve diagram from the lectures, where does the 'valley of death' occur?
options: Between market breakout and market saturation
Between the weak signal stage and the go-to-market stage
Between trend growth and market saturation
Between the established trend and the strengthening trend
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: The detailed S-curve diagram shows the progression: weak signal → strong signal → valley of death → go to market → market breakout → established trend → trend growth → strengthening trend → market saturation → weakening/ending trend. The valley of death sits between early signal detection and market entry.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: What happened at Kodak in 1976 that illustrates the incumbent's dilemma with the S-curve?
options: Kodak launched the world's first digital camera product line
Kodak invented the digital CMOS sensor but did not commercialize it
Kodak acquired a digital photography startup that it later shut down
Kodak filed a patent on smartphone cameras and licensed it to Apple
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: In 1976, Kodak invented the digital CMOS sensor internally but did not commercialize it. Sony and Apple commercialized digital cameras in the 1980s-1990s, Canon and Nikon dominated digital in the 1990s-2000s, and smartphones replaced most digital cameras in the 2010s. This is a textbook example of incumbents staying on the current S-curve.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: According to the lecture slides, approximately what share of the top 500 US public firms do the top 5 tech companies represent?
options: About 10%
About 25%
About 40%
About 50%
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: Top 5 US Tech = approximately 25% of the top 500 public firms. This concentration raises concerns about whether Big Tech is stifling innovation through acquisitions and market dominance.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: Over the period 2000-2018, how frequently did Google acquire other companies on average?
options: 1 acquisition per quarter
1 acquisition per month
1 acquisition per week
1 acquisition per year
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: Google averaged 1 acquisition per month over nearly two decades (2000-2018). This is part of the 'siloing' phenomenon where large firms buy startups — often to eliminate competition before they grow (OECD finding).

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: The concept of 'Jimmy Got a Job at Google' from the lectures illustrates that:
options: Acquired firms typically double their innovation output under Google's resources
Purchased firms often get shut down or stagnate in corporate silos
Google's acquisitions primarily target firms with patented technologies
Employees from acquired firms typically leave within the first year
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: 'Jimmy Got a Job at Google' refers to the phenomenon where acquired firms are shut down or stagnate in corporate silos. Post-acquisition, innovation often declines and products are discontinued. The OECD found firms buy startups to eliminate competition before they grow.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: What is the 'modern productivity paradox' discussed in the Lecture 2 review?
options: Productivity is increasing faster than wages can keep up with
More R&D spending leads to fewer patents per dollar invested
Declining productivity despite increasing technological sophistication
Developing countries have higher productivity growth than developed countries
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: The modern productivity paradox is the observation of declining productivity despite increasing technological sophistication. This was listed as a key 'check your understanding' topic from Lecture 2.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: When analyzing Eli Lilly's 50-year insulin purity quest through Niosi's framework, the key concern is that Lilly's trajectory led to:
options: Radical innovation through scientific breakthroughs
Path dependency and overshooting of market needs
Successful disruption of the pharmaceutical industry
Cost reduction through economies of scale
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: Eli Lilly's 50-year pursuit of insulin purity is analyzed through Niosi's concepts of 'standard engineering' and 'technological trajectories.' The key finding is path dependency — Lilly's trajectory created organizational inertia that led to overshooting customer needs (purity was already 'good enough'), missing the shift to convenience-based competition.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: Henry Ford's quote 'If I had asked people what they wanted, they would have said faster horses' is used in the lectures to illustrate:
options: The importance of market research in innovation
Why customer surveys are the best source of innovation ideas
That radical innovation often comes from visionary entrepreneurs, not customer demand
That incremental innovation is more reliable than radical innovation
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: The Ford quote illustrates that customers often cannot envision radical innovations — they extrapolate from existing solutions ('faster horses'). Radical innovation requires visionary entrepreneurs who break economic equilibrium, a core Schumpeterian concept.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: In the Schumpeterian cycle, the 'angel' represents radical/revolutionary change while the 'devil' represents incremental/evolutionary change.
options: True
False
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: False. It is the opposite. The 'angel' represents evolution (incremental change) and the 'devil' represents revolution (radical change). Entrepreneurs are on the 'devil' side — they break economic equilibrium with radical innovations.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: The 1st innovation wave (~1785, lasting ~60 years) was characterized by water power, textiles, and iron.
options: True
False
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: True. The 1st innovation wave began around 1785, lasted approximately 60 years, and was driven by water power, textiles, and iron. This was the longest wave in the Edelson Institute framework.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: The 5th innovation wave (~1990) was defined by AI & IoT, robots & drones, and clean tech.
options: True
False
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: False. The 5th wave (~1990, ~30yr) was defined by digital networks, software, and new media. AI & IoT, robots & drones, and clean tech define the 6th wave (~2020, ~25yr). The lecture concludes we are leaving the 'IT Age' and entering the 'AI Age.'

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: The shift from individual inventors to institutionalized R&D labs was driven by increasing technical complexity and the declining productivity of individual inventors.
options: True
False
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: True. As technology became more complex, the 'lonely inventor' model became insufficient. The cost of complexity and the decline of the individual inventor drove the shift to collective/institutional R&D, beginning with Edison (1876) and spreading to Europe by 1880-1890.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: As innovation became institutionalized within firms in the late 19th century, patenting per capita increased because more resources were available for R&D.
options: True
False
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: False. Patenting per capita actually DROPPED as innovation consolidated within firms. The democratized innovation era (80% of inventors with no scientific training) gave way to corporate R&D labs, concentrating innovation in fewer hands.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: According to the S-curve framework from the lectures, incumbents tend to initiate new S-curves while disruptors prefer to stay on the existing curve.
options: True
False
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: False. It is the opposite. Incumbents like to stay on the CURRENT S-curve (exploiting existing technology), while disruptors/entrepreneurs/innovators initiate NEW S-curves. This is central to understanding why Kodak failed — they stayed on the film S-curve.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: Kodak invented the digital CMOS sensor in 1976, but Sony and Apple were the first companies to commercialize digital cameras in the 1980s-1990s.
options: True
False
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: True. Kodak invented the digital CMOS sensor in 1976 internally but did not commercialize it. Sony and Apple commercialized the first digital cameras in the 1980s-1990s. Canon and Nikon later dominated the digital camera market in the 1990s-2000s.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: Meta bought the AI startup Manus for over $2 billion in December 2025, illustrating the ongoing trend of Big Tech acquiring innovative startups.
options: True
False
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: True. Meta bought Manus AI startup for >$2 billion in December 2025. This is consistent with the market concentration / 'siloing' trend discussed in the lectures, where large firms buy startups — sometimes to eliminate competition.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: Innovation waves have been getting longer over time because each new technology requires more development time before it can reach the market.
options: True
False
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: False. Innovation waves have been getting SHORTER, not longer: from ~60 years (1st wave) to ~25 years (6th wave). Despite increasing complexity, faster communication, global R&D networks, and digital tools have accelerated the cycle.

Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).q: Henry Ford's 'faster horses' quote supports the Schumpeterian view that radical innovation comes from entrepreneurs breaking equilibrium, not from responding to existing customer demands.
options: True
False
Lecture 2: Tech Evolution (Slides).explanation: True. Ford's quote ('If I had asked people what they wanted, they would have said faster horses') perfectly illustrates the Schumpeterian view: entrepreneurs introduce radical innovations that customers cannot envision. They break economic equilibrium rather than responding to expressed demand.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: According to Schumpeter (1945, p82), creative destruction is best described as:
options: A government policy that removes outdated regulations to promote innovation
A process of industrial mutation that revolutionizes economic structure from within, destroying the old and creating the new
The deliberate decision by firms to shut down unprofitable product lines
An evolutionary process where the strongest firms survive through natural selection
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: Schumpeter's exact quote: 'A process of industrial mutation...that incessantly revolutionizes the economic structure from within, incessantly destroying the old one, incessantly creating a new one. This process of creative destruction is the essential fact about capitalism.' (1945, p82)

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: The lecture's humorous rule for spotting creative destruction is:
options: If a product needs a manual, it was likely a victim of creative destruction
If hipsters like it, it was at some point likely the victim of creative destruction
If you can find it in a museum, it survived creative destruction
If your grandparents used it, it was probably a victim of creative destruction
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: 'If hipsters like it, it was at some point likely the victim of creative destruction' — examples include vinyl records, typewriters, mechanical watches, cargo bikes, and brick buildings. These products were displaced by newer alternatives but retained niche/nostalgic appeal.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: Schumpeterian rents are best defined as:
options: Government subsidies given to innovative firms to offset R&D costs
Profits earned by innovators between the introduction of an innovation and its diffusion
The cost savings achieved through economies of scale in production
Revenue generated from licensing intellectual property to competitors
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: Schumpeterian rents are the profits earned by innovators in the window between introducing an innovation and its widespread diffusion. Also called 'entrepreneurial rent.' Once imitators catch up, these rents erode.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: In the determinants of profitability framework, which pairing of rent type and its source is CORRECT?
options: Schumpeterian rents — firm-specific resources and capabilities
Monopoly rents — technological change and innovation
Ricardian rents — firm-specific resources, capabilities, and barriers to imitation
Ricardian rents — industry position and barriers to entry
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: Ricardian rents come from firm-specific determinants: resources, capabilities, barriers to imitation, structures, systems, and people. Schumpeterian rents come from change (technology paradigms, supply/demand shifts). Monopoly rents come from environmental determinants (industry position, institutional factors, barriers to entry).

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: Which type of rent is associated with environmental determinants such as industry position, institutional factors, and barriers to entry?
options: Schumpeterian rents
Monopoly rents
Ricardian rents
Entrepreneurial rents
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: Monopoly rents are associated with environmental determinants: industry position, institutional factors, and barriers to entry. Schumpeterian rents come from change/innovation. Ricardian rents come from firm-specific resources. Note: 'Entrepreneurial rents' is just another name for Schumpeterian rents.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: The determinants of profitability diagram from the lecture shows three interacting boxes leading to firm performance. These three boxes are:
options: Innovation, Competition, Regulation → Performance
Change (Schumpeterian) ↔ Environmental (Monopoly) ↔ Firm-Specific (Ricardian) → Performance
R&D Spending, Market Share, Customer Satisfaction → Performance
Technology, Strategy, Organization → Performance
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: The three boxes are: Change (Schumpeterian) ↔ Environmental (Monopoly) ↔ Firm-Specific (Ricardian) → Performance. All three interact with each other and together determine firm performance.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: Competitive life cycles describe how firms compete within each paradigm. Which set of dimensions is used to measure this?
options: Revenue, market share, customer acquisition cost, brand value
Revenues, number of firms/concentration, profits, technological change
R&D spending, patent count, employee count, stock price
Innovation rate, market penetration, profit margin, regulatory compliance
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: Competitive life cycles are measured by four dimensions: revenues, number of firms/concentration, profits, and technological change. These capture how industries evolve from emergence through growth to maturity.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: According to the lecture on the nature of markets, which statement is most accurate?
options: Most markets are static, with dynamic markets being the exception
Dynamic markets are the norm, not the exception; few markets are actually static
All markets cycle between static and dynamic phases at predictable intervals
Markets in developed economies are static while emerging economy markets are dynamic
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: The lecture emphasizes that dynamic markets are the norm, not the exception. Few markets are actually static. This is a fundamental insight — firms must assume their competitive environment will change.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: Which of the following is an example of a 'slow' market as categorized in the lectures?
options: Information technology
Digital services
Insurance
Software development
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: Insurance is categorized as a 'slow' market alongside transportation and hairdressers. 'Fast' markets include IT, technology, and digital services. Holding competitive advantage in fast-changing markets is particularly difficult.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: In industries with rapid technological shifts, the primary source of radical innovation is typically:
options: Government-funded research laboratories
Incumbent firms investing in sustaining technologies
New entrants (startups) that challenge the status quo
University-industry collaborations and technology transfer
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: In industries with rapid tech shifts, NEW ENTRANTS (startups) are the primary source of innovation. Unlike incumbents who focus on incremental improvements, these entrepreneurial firms introduce RADICAL innovations and are engines of long-term growth.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: Schumpeterian rents are also known by another name. What is it?
options: Innovation premiums
Entrepreneurial rents
First-mover rents
Monopolistic rents
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: Schumpeterian rents are also called 'entrepreneurial rents.' They represent the profit earned between the introduction of an innovation and its diffusion. The name reflects that entrepreneurs are the ones who create and initially capture these rents.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: The lecture includes the observation that 'by the time the lawsuits are over, the market may have moved on.' This illustrates:
options: Why slow markets are more profitable than fast markets
The difficulty of maintaining competitive advantage in fast-changing markets
Why firms should avoid patenting their innovations
The effectiveness of legal protection in technology industries
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: This quote illustrates the difficulty of holding competitive advantage in fast-changing markets. Legal processes move slowly relative to market dynamics in 'fast' markets (IT, digital services), meaning competitive advantages can be fleeting.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: Schumpeterian rents lead to changes in which of the following?
options: Only technology paradigms
Only regulation and government policy
Technology paradigms, demand/supply, and regulation
Only demand and supply dynamics
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: Schumpeterian rents lead to changes in three areas: technology paradigms, demand/supply dynamics, and regulation. These rents represent the profit from innovation that drives broader systemic changes across the economy.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: According to Schumpeter (1945), what is 'the essential fact about capitalism'?
options: The accumulation of capital through compound interest
The process of creative destruction
The invisible hand of market competition
The division of labor and specialization
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: Schumpeter explicitly called creative destruction 'the essential fact about capitalism' (1945, p82). It is the process of industrial mutation that incessantly revolutionizes economic structure from within.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: The lecture characterizes entrepreneurial firms as:
options: Temporary disruptors that rarely survive beyond the first market cycle
Engines of long-term growth that constantly challenge the status quo and prevent economic stagnation
Incremental innovators that gradually improve existing products
Risk-averse firms that wait for market signals before innovating
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: Entrepreneurial firms are characterized as engines of long-term growth — not just temporary disruptors. They constantly challenge the status quo and prevent economic stagnation. In industries with rapid tech shifts, they are the primary source of radical innovation.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: Schumpeter's creative destruction quote from 1945 describes a process that 'incessantly revolutionizes the economic structure from within.'
options: True
False
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: True. The exact quote: 'A process of industrial mutation...that incessantly revolutionizes the economic structure from within, incessantly destroying the old one, incessantly creating a new one.' (Schumpeter, 1945, p82)

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: According to the 'hipster test' for creative destruction, vinyl records, typewriters, and mechanical watches are all examples of products that were victims of creative destruction.
options: True
False
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: True. The lecture lists vinyl, typewriters, mechanical watches, cargo bikes, and brick buildings as examples. 'If hipsters like it, it was at some point likely the victim of creative destruction' — these were displaced by newer technologies but retained niche/nostalgic appeal.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: Ricardian rents are derived from environmental determinants such as industry position and barriers to entry.
options: True
False
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: False. Ricardian rents come from FIRM-SPECIFIC determinants: resources, capabilities, barriers to imitation, structures, systems, and people. It is MONOPOLY rents that come from environmental determinants (industry position, institutional factors, barriers to entry).

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: Schumpeterian rents are the profits earned after an innovation has been fully diffused throughout the market and all competitors have adopted it.
options: True
False
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: False. Schumpeterian rents are earned BETWEEN the introduction of an innovation and its diffusion — not after diffusion. Once imitators catch up and the innovation is widely adopted, these rents erode. The window of profit is temporary.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: According to the lecture, dynamic markets are the norm rather than the exception, and few markets are actually static.
options: True
False
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: True. The lecture explicitly states that dynamic markets are the norm, not the exception, and few markets are actually static. Even 'slow' markets like insurance and transportation undergo change over time.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: In industries with rapid technological shifts, incumbent firms are typically the primary source of radical innovation because they have the most R&D resources.
options: True
False
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: False. In industries with rapid tech shifts, NEW ENTRANTS (startups) — not incumbents — are the primary source of innovation. Incumbents tend to focus on incremental improvements. Having more R&D resources does not make incumbents the source of radical innovation.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: Competitive life cycles measure how firms compete within each paradigm using four dimensions: revenues, number of firms/concentration, profits, and technological change.
options: True
False
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: True. These are the four dimensions explicitly listed in the lecture slides for measuring competitive life cycles within a given technology paradigm.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: In the determinants of profitability diagram, the three boxes (Change, Environmental, Firm-Specific) operate independently of each other and do not interact.
options: True
False
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: False. The three boxes (Change/Schumpeterian ↔ Environmental/Monopoly ↔ Firm-Specific/Ricardian) all INTERACT with each other and together determine firm performance. They are connected by bidirectional arrows.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: IT and digital services are examples of 'slow' markets where holding competitive advantage is relatively straightforward.
options: True
False
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: False. IT and digital services are examples of 'FAST' markets, not slow. Slow markets include transportation, insurance, and hairdressers. Holding competitive advantage in fast-changing markets is particularly DIFFICULT, not straightforward.

Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).q: 'Entrepreneurial rent' is another term for Schumpeterian rent — the profit earned between the introduction of an innovation and its diffusion.
options: True
False
Lecture 3: Industry Evolution (Slides).explanation: True. 'Entrepreneurial rent' and 'Schumpeterian rent' are synonymous. Both refer to the temporary profit innovators earn in the window between introducing an innovation and its widespread diffusion by competitors.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: What shifted the basis of competition in the insulin market during the early 1980s?
options: Price competition from generic manufacturers
Competition shifted from purity to convenience/delivery method
Government regulation forced standardized pricing
Patients demanded organic, non-synthetic insulin
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: Eli Lilly invested ~$1B in Humulin (rDNA human insulin, 0 ppm impurities), but the market was already satisfied with pork insulin purity. Novo Nordisk's insulin pen shifted competition to convenience — it commanded a 30% price premium because injection took <10 seconds vs 1-2 minutes.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: Eli Lilly's failure to respond to the insulin pen is best explained by:
options: Lack of R&D funding
Path dependency — 5+ decades locked into the purity trajectory
The insulin pen was patented by a competitor and couldn't be copied
Regulatory barriers prevented Lilly from making medical devices
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: Lilly's entire corporate identity was built around pharmaceutical chemistry and purity improvement. 50+ years on the purity trajectory created path dependency — they were a drug company, not a device company. Their marketers also surveyed specialists (lead users) rather than mainstream patients.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: Eli Lilly's Humulin case illustrates which concept from Shane's technology evolution framework?
options: Creative destruction
First-mover advantage
Overshooting — exceeding customer needs on the current trajectory
Dominant design emergence
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: Overshooting occurs when a technological trajectory exceeds customer needs. Lilly pushed insulin purity beyond what patients needed, opening the door for Novo Nordisk to compete on a different dimension (convenience) where Lilly had no advantage.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: According to Lerner & Nanda, which is NOT a limitation of venture capital as a funding mechanism for innovation?
options: Short-term focus due to fund lifecycle pressure
Herd behavior — VCs cluster in trendy sectors
Geographic concentration in a few regions
VC firms charge excessive management fees of 10-15%
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: VC management fees are typically 2% (not 10-15%). The real limitations are: short-term focus (must return capital within fund life), herd behavior (overinvesting in trendy areas), geographic bias (concentrated in Silicon Valley/Boston/NYC), and inability to fund all types of innovation.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: In the VC model, the extreme skewness of returns means that:
options: Most VC-funded startups achieve moderate success
0.4% of investments generate approximately 97% of total returns
Returns are evenly distributed across portfolio companies
Only biotech investments generate outsized returns
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: VC returns follow a power law: 0.4% of investments generate ~97% of returns. This means most VC-funded companies fail or return little, while a tiny fraction produce massive returns that carry the entire portfolio.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: A VC fund typically expects returns of 5-10x over approximately how many years?
options: 2-3 years
5-7 years
7 years
15-20 years
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: VCs target 5-10x returns over approximately 7 years. The fund lifecycle creates pressure to invest in companies with relatively quick paths to exit (IPO or acquisition), which is one limitation of VC funding.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: When Steve Jobs returned to Apple in 1997, what organizational change did he make on his first day?
options: He created autonomous business units for each product line
He fired all general managers and put the company under one P&L
He outsourced manufacturing to reduce costs
He established a matrix organization with dual reporting
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: Jobs fired ALL general managers of business units in a single day and reorganized Apple into a functional structure under one P&L. This was radical — most companies Apple's size use multi-divisional structures. Apple kept this functional structure even as it grew from 8,000 to 137,000 employees.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: Apple's functional structure is based on the principle of aligning:
options: Accountability and control
Expertise and decision rights
Revenue targets and bonuses
Product lines and market segments
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: Apple's functional structure aligns EXPERTISE with DECISION RIGHTS — those who know the most make the decisions. Multi-divisional structures align accountability and control instead. Apple believes it's easier to train an expert to manage than to train a manager to become an expert.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: In Apple's discretionary leadership model, a VP who spends time deeply involved in a NEW area where they lack expertise is operating in the:
options: Owning box
Learning box
Teaching box
Delegating box
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: The Learning box = low expertise + high detail involvement. Leaders adopt a beginner's mindset for new areas. The four boxes: Owning (high expertise, high involvement, ~40% time), Learning (low expertise, high involvement, ~30%), Teaching (high expertise, low involvement, ~15%), Delegating (low expertise, low involvement, ~15%).

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: How did Apple manage organizational scaling from 1998 to 2019?
options: They added general managers for each new product category
They limited VP growth (8x employees, 2x VPs), split functions, and added AI/ML
They adopted a multi-divisional structure once revenue exceeded $100B
They outsourced management to consulting firms
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: While employees grew 8x (8K→137K) and VPs only ~2x (50→96), Apple maintained its functional structure by: splitting hardware into Hardware Engineering and Hardware Technologies, adding AI/ML as a new function, and merging Human Interface with Industrial Design. Leaders moved mature activities from 'owning' to 'teaching.'

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: According to Podolny & Hansen (2020), at most multi-divisional companies, general managers spend most of their time in the _____ box, while Apple VPs spend most time in the _____ boxes.
options: Delegating; owning and learning
Owning; teaching and delegating
Learning; owning and teaching
Teaching; learning and delegating
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: General managers at divisional companies primarily delegate because they oversee broad portfolios. Apple VPs primarily own and learn — they stay deeply involved in details, either in areas of expertise (owning) or actively building new competence (learning).

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: Apple's iPhone 7 Plus Portrait Mode required collaboration from approximately how many specialist teams?
options: 5 teams
12 teams
40+ teams
Over 100 teams
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: Portrait Mode required 40+ specialist teams (silicon design, camera software, reliability engineering, motion sensors, video engineering, etc.). Paul Hubel conceived the idea in 2009, and it launched with iPhone 7 Plus. The feature was deferred one year to address edge cases. This illustrates Apple's 'accountability without control' model.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: The Metaverse case primarily illustrates which strategic concept?
options: First-mover advantage guarantees market dominance
Technical feasibility does not equal commercial viability
All disruptive innovations eventually succeed
Platform markets always converge to a single standard
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: The Metaverse case illustrates that technical feasibility ≠ commercial viability. The Concorde is used as a parallel example — supersonic flight was technically feasible but commercially unviable. The same question applies to VR/metaverse technology, flying cars, and other technologies.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: The term 'metaverse' originates from:
options: A 2021 Facebook press release
Neal Stephenson's novel Snow Crash (1992)
Ernest Cline's novel Ready Player One (2011)
A Stanford research paper on virtual worlds
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: The term 'metaverse' comes from Neal Stephenson's 1992 science fiction novel Snow Crash. Ready Player One (2011) by Ernest Cline also popularized the concept. Facebook renamed itself Meta in October 2021, but the concept predates it by decades.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: Should Moderna spin off its COVID vaccine business into a separate unit?
options: Yes — a dedicated unit would be faster and more agile
Yes — it would prevent COVID from overshadowing the other 22 pipeline programs
No — Moderna's competitive advantage comes from shared data and integrated platform across all programs
No — spinning off is illegal under BARDA grant terms
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: Moderna should NOT spin off. Their competitive advantage comes from shared data, integrated digital infrastructure, and cross-learning between programs. Spinning off would create the silos that Moderna specifically designed its organization to avoid. As the case states: 'Moderna's competitive advantage came exactly from a shared understanding and shared data across all departments and teams.'

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: Moderna's most important priorities going forward include:
options: Focusing exclusively on COVID boosters and pandemic preparedness
Expanding the mRNA platform to other diseases while maintaining the born-digital model
Transitioning to a traditional pharmaceutical organizational structure
Outsourcing manufacturing to reduce costs
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: Moderna's priorities are: expanding the mRNA platform across the 23-program pipeline (CMV, RSV, Zika, cancer vaccines), maintaining platform economics, and sustaining their born-digital/AI factory model. COVID was their 10th vaccine — the platform's value is in treating many diseases, not just one.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: Moderna CEO Stephane Bancel describes the company as:
options: A pharmaceutical company with strong digital tools
A technology company that happens to do biology
A biotech startup focused on vaccine development
A digital health platform for telemedicine
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: Bancel explicitly states: 'We're a technology company that happens to do biology.' This reflects Moderna's born-digital identity — drugs designed on computer, paperless factory, 20+ AI algorithms in production, all data shared and integrated across departments.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: Moderna's mRNA platform analogy is:
options: mRNA is the factory and LNPs are the products
mRNA is the software and LNPs are the hardware
mRNA is the hardware and diseases are the software
mRNA is the medicine and AI is the delivery system
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: CSO Melissa Moore: 'mRNA is the software and LNPs (lipid nanoparticles) are the hardware.' Another analogy: 'mRNA is a platform like the iPhone; individual drugs are like apps.' This platform logic means if one drug works, others can work on the same underlying technology.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: According to Christensen (2015), Uber is:
options: A classic example of disruptive innovation
A sustaining innovation relative to taxis, not disruptive
A new-market disruptive innovation
A low-end disruptive innovation
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: Christensen argues Uber is NOT disruptive. It launched in San Francisco (a well-served market), targeted existing taxi users (not nonconsumers), and offered BETTER service (not inferior). It built position in the mainstream first, then expanded — the OPPOSITE direction of disruption. Uber is a sustaining innovation.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: According to Christensen (2015), Tesla is not considered disruptive because:
options: Electric vehicles are not innovative
Tesla's foothold is in the high end of the market, which IS interesting to incumbents
Tesla was acquired by an incumbent before it could disrupt
Tesla uses the same business model as traditional automakers
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: Tesla entered at the HIGH END ($70,000+ cars), a segment very much of interest to incumbents. True disruption starts in the low end or new-market foothold — segments incumbents ignore. Tesla attracted significant attention and investment from established automakers, the opposite of what happens with disruptive entrants.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: According to Christensen (2015), disruption is best understood as:
options: A product that replaces an existing one
A process whereby smaller firms successfully challenge incumbents by targeting overlooked segments
Any innovation that significantly changes a market
A sudden technological breakthrough that destroys an industry
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: Christensen defines disruption as a PROCESS, not a product. Smaller firms target overlooked segments (low-end or new-market), gain a foothold, then move upmarket until mainstream customers adopt their offering. It can take decades — discount department stores took 50+ years to fully challenge traditional ones.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: The iPhone's launch in 2007 was a sustaining innovation in the smartphone market, but its subsequent growth was disruptive to:
options: Feature phones like Nokia
The laptop as primary internet access device
Digital cameras
MP3 players like the iPod
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: The iPhone launched as a sustaining innovation in smartphones (targeting the same customers with a better product). But it became disruptive to LAPTOPS as the primary internet access device, enabled by a new business model (the App Store ecosystem). This shows disruption is relative — the same product can be sustaining in one market and disruptive in another.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: The AutoStore vs Ocado case in the FT article illustrates the same patent strategy dynamic as:
options: Eli Lilly vs Novo Nordisk
Apple vs Samsung
Selden vs Ford
Moderna vs Pfizer
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: AutoStore vs Ocado mirrors Selden vs Ford — using broad patents to restrict competitors' market entry. Selden held a broad automobile patent (1879-1911) and sued manufacturers. Ford won on appeal by using a different engine type. The same strategic dynamics play out across centuries: patents can be circumvented through alternative technical approaches.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: According to Christensen (2015), how should incumbents respond to disruptive threats?
options: Immediately pivot the entire company toward the disruptive technology
Continue investing in sustaining innovation for core customers AND create a separate division for the disruptive opportunity
Acquire the disruptive entrant as quickly as possible
Reduce R&D spending and focus on cost cutting
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: Christensen recommends a dual approach: continue investing in sustaining innovations for core customers (don't dismantle a profitable business), while creating a SEPARATE division for the disruptive opportunity. The new division must be kept separate from the core business. The mantra 'disrupt or be disrupted' can misguide if it leads to overreaction.

Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.q: In Christensen's disk drive study, what percentage of sustaining entrants succeeded?
Gap Drill: Holes & Weak Spots.explanation: Only 6% of sustaining entrants succeeded in the disk drive industry. This statistic highlights how difficult it is for new entrants to compete head-on with incumbents using sustaining innovations. Disruption, by contrast, avoids direct competition initially by targeting overlooked segments.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: Which of the following is NOT a reason Eli Lilly failed to respond to Novo Nordisk's insulin pen?
options: Path dependency from 50+ years on the purity trajectory
Corporate identity built around pharmaceutical chemistry
Surveyed specialists (lead users) instead of mainstream patients
Lilly lacked the financial resources to develop a competing pen
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Lilly invested nearly $1B in Humulin — money was not the problem. The real barriers were path dependency, a drug-company identity (not device), and marketing research biased toward high-end specialists who valued purity over convenience.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: In the Abernathy-Utterback model, the rate of PROCESS innovation peaks:
options: During the fluid phase, before a dominant design emerges
At the same time as product innovation
During the specific phase, after a dominant design emerges
During the transitional phase, around the time of dominant design emergence
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: In the A-U model, product innovation peaks during the fluid phase. After dominant design emerges (transitional phase), process innovation increases and peaks during the specific phase — firms shift focus from product variation to efficient production.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: A firm discovers that its technology now exceeds what mainstream customers need, while a competitor offers a simpler alternative. This scenario describes:
options: Creative destruction
Overshooting on the technology S-curve
A competence-destroying discontinuity
The chasm between early adopters and early majority
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Overshooting occurs when a firm's technology trajectory exceeds mainstream customer needs, opening the door for simpler alternatives. This is distinct from creative destruction (replacement of industries), competence destruction (skills become obsolete), and the chasm (gap between adopter categories).

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: According to Christensen (2015), the iPhone's launch in 2007 was disruptive to the smartphone market.
options: True
False
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: False. The iPhone launched as a SUSTAINING innovation in the smartphone market — it targeted the same customers as incumbents and competed on product superiority. Its subsequent growth was disruptive to LAPTOPS as primary internet access devices, not to smartphones.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: Nokia's market capitalization fell from $250B to approximately:
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Nokia's market cap fell from $250B at its peak to ~$43B. This dramatic decline illustrates how even dominant firms can fail when technological discontinuities shift the basis of competition — in Nokia's case, from hardware to software/ecosystems.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: Which is NOT one of Apple's three leadership characteristics according to Podolny & Hansen (2020)?
options: Deep expertise — experts lead experts
Immersion in details three levels down
Willingness to collaboratively debate across functions
Strong P&L accountability for individual product lines
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Apple explicitly does NOT use individual product P&L accountability — the entire company runs under ONE P&L. The three characteristics are: deep expertise, immersion in details, and willingness to collaboratively debate. R&D bonuses are based on companywide performance.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: Moderna shipped its COVID vaccine candidate to the NIH within 42 days of the genome sequence being posted. The previous fastest vaccine development timeline was:
options: 6 months for H1N1
20 months for SARS
3 years for Ebola
5 years for HPV
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: The previous fastest was 20 MONTHS for SARS. Moderna achieved a 90% reduction in time (42 days vs 20 months). Their team finalized the vaccine design just 2 DAYS after the genome sequence was posted, first clinical batch manufactured by day 25.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: In the VC model, the typical management fee and carry structure is:
options: 5% fee, 50% carry
2% fee, 20-30% carry
1% fee, 10% carry
10% fee, 15% carry
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Standard VC economics: 2% annual management fee on committed capital + 20-30% carried interest (share of profits). VCs target 5-10x returns over ~7 years. The 0.4%→97% statistic shows extreme return skewness.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: Selden's automobile patent case (1879-1911) is historically significant because Ford ultimately won by:
options: Proving that Selden never built a working automobile
Showing that his car used a four-cycle engine, not Selden's patented Brayton two-stroke
Demonstrating that Selden's patent had expired
Lobbying Congress to change patent law
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Ford lost the initial case but won on appeal by showing his cars used a different engine type (four-cycle) than what Selden's patent covered (Brayton two-stroke). Key lesson: broad patents can be circumvented through alternative technical approaches. AutoStore vs Ocado mirrors this same dynamic.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: According to Christensen (2015), which statement about disruption is correct?
options: Disruption is a product characteristic that can be identified at launch
All successful startups are disruptive by definition
Disruption is a process that can take decades to unfold
Disruptive innovations always succeed in the marketplace
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Disruption is a PROCESS, not a product attribute at a fixed point. Discount department stores took 50+ years to fully challenge traditional ones. Some disruptive innovations succeed, some don't — success is not built into the definition. Not every successful newcomer follows a disruptive path.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: The 'shakeout' phase of industry evolution typically eliminates approximately what percentage of firms?
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: The shakeout typically eliminates ~52% of firms in an industry. This occurs after a dominant design emerges and the basis of competition shifts from product innovation to process efficiency and cost reduction.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: An organic organizational structure is MOST appropriate when:
options: The firm operates in a stable environment with routine tasks
The firm needs to explore new technologies in an uncertain environment
The firm is primarily focused on cost reduction and efficiency
The firm has achieved dominant market share in a mature industry
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Organic structures (flat, decentralized, informal) suit uncertain environments where exploration and flexibility are needed. Mechanistic structures (hierarchical, centralized, formal) suit stable environments with routine tasks. Burns & Stalker's classic distinction.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: Copyright protection is automatic upon creation and lasts for:
options: 20 years from filing date
Life of the creator plus 50 years
Life of the creator plus 70 years
10 years, renewable indefinitely
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Copyright = automatic (no registration needed), lasts life + 70 years. Compare: patents = 20 years from filing (requires application), trademarks = 10 years (renewable). Copyright's automatic nature and long duration make it unique among IP protections.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: Moderna's CSO Melissa Moore describes the company's platform using which analogy?
options: mRNA is the factory; diseases are the products
mRNA is the software and LNPs are the hardware
mRNA is the fuel; the body is the engine
mRNA is the architect; proteins are the buildings
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Moore: 'mRNA is the software and LNPs (lipid nanoparticles) are the hardware.' Also: 'mRNA is a platform like the iPhone; individual drugs are like apps.' This platform logic means if one drug works on mRNA, others can too — COVID was Moderna's 10th vaccine, not their first.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: Which concept from Teece's appropriability framework determines whether an innovator can capture value from their innovation?
options: The strength of the dominant design
The tightness of the appropriability regime and control of complementary assets
The number of patents filed
The size of the R&D budget relative to competitors
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Teece argues that value capture depends on: (1) the tightness of the appropriability regime (how easy it is for others to imitate), and (2) control of complementary assets (manufacturing, distribution, brand). Having a great innovation means nothing if you can't appropriate the returns.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: P&G's Connect and Develop strategy aimed to source innovation externally. Their R&D as a percentage of sales went from:
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: P&G reduced R&D spending from 4.8% to 3.4% of sales while increasing innovation output through Connect and Develop. By sourcing ideas externally (from inventors, suppliers, competitors), they got more innovation for less internal R&D spending.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: According to the course, approximately what percentage of commercially successful products originate from academic/university research?
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Only about 11% of commercially successful products trace back to academic research. This highlights that most innovation comes from firms, individual inventors, and other sources rather than universities — though universities remain important for breakthrough discoveries.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: The Metaverse case uses which historical example to illustrate that technical feasibility does not guarantee commercial viability?
options: Google Glass
The Segway
Concorde supersonic jet
3D television
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: The Concorde is used as the key parallel: supersonic travel was technically feasible (it worked!) but commercially unviable. The metaverse faces the same question — just because VR technology works doesn't mean it will be commercially successful at scale.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: In a decision tree analysis, the key limitation compared to other project selection tools is that:
options: It cannot handle more than two outcomes
It restricts decisions to information available at the current stage
It requires perfect information about all future outcomes
It only works for incremental innovations
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Decision trees limit you to using information available at the current decision point — you make sequential decisions as uncertainty resolves. This contrasts with tools like NPV that try to estimate all future cash flows upfront, or real options that value the flexibility to abandon.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: Adverse selection in innovation financing refers to:
options: Investors choosing projects with the highest risk
Investors being unable to distinguish good projects from bad ones before investing
Entrepreneurs deliberately choosing investors with the least oversight
Banks selecting only large firms for innovation loans
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Adverse selection = PRE-investment information asymmetry. Investors can't tell good projects from bad, so they may underfund good innovations or overfund bad ones. Moral hazard is POST-investment — the entrepreneur may change behavior after receiving funding. Both are key problems in financing innovation.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: According to Shane, which of the following is NOT one of the four policies for linking research to development?
options: Financial incentives for researchers who contribute to development
Having researchers use the same equipment as developers
Exposing researchers to the development process
Hiring external consultants to bridge the research-development gap
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Shane's four R&D linking policies (p.88): (1) financial incentives, (2) same equipment, (3) expose to development, (4) fit with strategy. External consultants are NOT one of the four. These policies help prevent the common disconnect between research discoveries and commercial development.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: Moderna's IPO in December 2018 was notable because:
options: It was the first biotech IPO on NASDAQ
The company was already profitable
It was the biggest biotech IPO in history at $7.5B valuation
The stock price doubled on the first day
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Moderna's IPO at $7.5B was the biggest biotech IPO in history at that time — remarkable for a company with $60.2M revenue and $514M net loss in 2019. They had raised $5.1B total, including $750M from strategic partners. The valuation reflected belief in the mRNA platform potential.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: Creative accumulation differs from creative destruction in that:
options: Creative accumulation occurs only in service industries
Incumbents build on existing knowledge rather than being replaced by newcomers
Creative accumulation is slower and always produces inferior innovations
Creative accumulation requires government subsidies
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Creative destruction (Schumpeter Mark I): new entrants destroy incumbents with radical innovations. Creative accumulation (Schumpeter Mark II): incumbents leverage existing knowledge, resources, and R&D to drive innovation — building on what exists rather than replacing it. Both patterns coexist in different industries.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: According to Christensen (2015), incumbents facing disruption should:
options: Immediately pivot the entire organization toward the disruptive technology
Ignore the disruption and focus solely on profitable core customers
Continue sustaining innovation in the core business AND create a separate division for the disruptive opportunity
Acquire the disruptive entrant to eliminate the threat
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Christensen recommends BOTH: keep investing in sustaining innovations for core customers (don't dismantle a profitable business) AND create a separate division for the disruptive opportunity. The key is keeping the new division separate. 'Disrupt or be disrupted' can misguide if it leads to abandoning a still-profitable core.

Hard Mock Exam 3.q: The adopter category that represents 13.5% of the market and is characterized by being opinion leaders who are respected by peers but more deliberate than innovators is:
options: Innovators
Early adopters
Early majority
Late majority
Hard Mock Exam 3.explanation: Early adopters = 13.5%. They are opinion leaders respected by peers, more integrated in the social system than innovators (2.5%), but more willing to take risks than the early majority (34%).
