The full distribution: innovators 2.5%, early adopters 13.5%, early majority 34%, late majority 34%, laggards 16%.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: P&G's Connect and Develop strategy found that for every 100 external ideas evaluated, approximately how many reached the market?
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Only 1 out of every 100 external ideas made it to market. This shows that open innovation requires massive screening effort — external ideas are not 'free' or 'easy.' Strong internal capabilities are needed to evaluate, develop, and commercialize outside ideas.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: P&G's innovation success rate after implementing Connect and Develop:
options: Stayed at 35% but cost less
More than doubled from the previous 35% rate
Tripled to over 100 products per year
Decreased because external ideas were lower quality
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: P&G's innovation success rate more than doubled after C&D. Additionally, R&D productivity increased ~60%, and R&D as % of sales fell from 4.8% to 3.4%. The stock price also doubled within 5 years.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: The Pringle Prints innovation (printing images on chips) originated from:
options: P&G's internal R&D lab in Cincinnati
A university professor running a bakery in Bologna, Italy
A competing snack manufacturer in Japan
MIT's food science department
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: A university professor in Bologna, Italy had developed inkjet printing technology for edible products (cakes and cookies). P&G's technology entrepreneurs found this through their external network and adapted it for Pringles. Concept to launch took less than 1 year (vs. the old ~2 year timeline).

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: According to the Nature 2023 study (Park, Leahey & Funk), the absolute number of disruptive scientific papers has:
options: Declined sharply since 1970
Stayed broadly the same — but the SHARE dropped because total output exploded
Increased proportionally with total research output
Doubled every decade since 1945
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: The absolute number of disruptive papers stayed broadly the same, but the SHARE of disruptive papers dropped because the total number of papers, patents, and funding exploded. More research is being done, but most of it is consolidating (building on existing work) rather than disruptive (replacing old paradigms).

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: In the Nature 2023 study, a 'consolidating' paper is one where subsequent work citing it:
options: Is less likely to also cite the paper's predecessors
Is more likely to also cite the paper's predecessors
Receives fewer total citations over time
Is retracted or corrected within 5 years
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: A consolidating paper BUILDS ON existing work — papers that cite it also cite its predecessors. A disruptive paper REPLACES old ones — papers that cite it are LESS likely to cite predecessors. The CD (disruption) index measures this distinction.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: According to Smil (WSJ 2023), processor performance annual growth went from 52% (1986-2003) to what level by 2015-2018?
options: Less than 4%
0% — performance stopped improving
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Processor performance growth collapsed: 52% annually (1986-2003) → 23% (2003-2011) → less than 4% (2015-2018). This is Smil's core argument — even the fastest-improving technology (Moore's Law) is slowing down dramatically.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: According to the NBER patent study cited by Smil, breakthrough patents in computers and electronics peaked:
options: Before 1900
Before 1950
Post-1970
They have not peaked yet
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Only agriculture (GMOs), medical equipment, and computers/electronics had breakthrough patent peaks post-1970. Most other sectors peaked much earlier: furniture, textiles, transportation before 1900; mining, electrical equipment, rubber before 1950.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: Moderna's revenue growth from 2019 to 2021 went from $60M to approximately:
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Moderna's revenue exploded: $60M (2019) → $803M (2020) → $17,675M (2021). This nearly 300x growth in 2 years demonstrates the power of the mRNA platform when a massive market need (COVID) aligned with their technology.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: Which is NOT a requirement for an invention to be patentable?
options: Useful — produces a desirable result or solves a problem
Novel — not already described in public literature
Non-obvious — to a person experienced in the field
Profitable — must demonstrate commercial viability
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: The three requirements are: useful, novel, and non-obvious. Commercial viability/profitability is NOT required. Many patented inventions never generate revenue. You can patent something that has no proven market.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: According to Shane, the TOP motivation for firms to patent (96% of respondents) is:
options: Licensing revenue
Preventing copying
Enhancing company reputation
Blocking competitors from adjacent markets
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Preventing copying: 96% (top), blocking others: 82%, preventing lawsuits: 59%, enhancing reputation: 48%, negotiation: 47%, licensing: only 28% (lowest). Most firms patent defensively, not for licensing income.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: Patents are rated MOST effective as protection in which industry?
options: Computers (3.4/7)
Organic chemicals (6.1/7)
Drugs/pharmaceuticals (6.5/7)
Pulp and paper (3.3/7)
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Drugs are rated 6.5/7 for patent effectiveness — highest of all industries. This is because drugs have clear molecular structures that are easy to define in patent claims. Computers (3.4/7) and pulp/paper (3.3/7) are among the lowest. Patents work best in biological/chemical industries.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: Apple's Roger Rosner's function grew from 150 to approximately how many people over the past decade?
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Rosner's function grew from 150 to ~1,000 people. Despite this massive growth, Apple maintained its functional structure. Leaders managed scaling by moving mature activities from 'owning' to 'teaching' and taking on new areas in the 'learning' box.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: In Apple's functional structure, 'accountability without control' means:
options: Managers are not held responsible for outcomes
Teams have no budget oversight
You are accountable for project success even though you don't control all teams involved
Apple uses external contractors with no accountability
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Accountability without control means you're responsible for making a project succeed even though you don't control all the teams involved. A 'good mess' = teams work with shared purpose across boundaries. A 'bad mess' = teams push their own agendas. This is how 40+ teams collaborated on Portrait Mode.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: Moderna's Norwood manufacturing plant can produce a manufactured molecule from a scientist's sequence in:
options: 48 hours
7 days
30 days
90 days
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Scientist sends sequence to Norwood → manufactured molecule in 30 days, with all data available and shared. The plant can reference 25,000+ past iterations. This 'paperless factory' has all machines directly connected to digital manufacturing systems.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: Moderna's technology transfer to manufacturing partner Lonza was completed in less than 3 months. The industry norm for such transfers is:
options: 6 months
1 year
2-3 years
5 years
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Technology transfer to Lonza took <3 months vs. the industry norm of 2-3 YEARS. This speed was possible because of Moderna's digital-first approach — all processes were documented digitally, enabling rapid knowledge transfer.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: The term 'squircle' in the Apple HBR case refers to:
options: Apple's circular organizational chart
The shape of Apple Watch display
Continuous curves Apple uses instead of standard rounded rectangles
A type of chip architecture in Apple Silicon
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: A squircle is a continuous curve shape (between a square and circle) that Apple uses instead of standard rounded rectangles. It requires extremely precise manufacturing tolerances. This detail illustrates Apple's obsession with design precision — 'leaders should know details three levels down.'

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: Patent trolls (Non-Practicing Entities / NPEs) are characterized by:
options: Filing patents and actively developing products from them
Acquiring patents WITHOUT intention to use them, then suing cash-rich firms
Offering free patent licenses to startups
Working with universities to commercialize research
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: NPEs/patent trolls acquire patents without any intention of making products. Instead, they sue cash-rich companies for alleged infringement. This is a major problem in the US patent system and represents a strategic abuse of patent rights.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: Solar panel cost now represents only about what percentage of total residential PV system cost?
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Solar panels are now only ~15% of total residential PV system cost. The rest is structural/electrical components, labor, permitting, and taxes. This is why overall solar cost reduction has slowed: 55% drop (2010-2015) but only 20% (2015-2020). Panel prices can't drive further cost reductions alone.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: P&G's external innovation network included YourEncore, which consisted of:
options: Competing firms sharing R&D resources
800 retired scientists and engineers from 150 companies
University labs in 20 countries
Government research agencies
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: YourEncore connected P&G with 800 retired scientists and engineers from 150 companies. Combined with NineSigma (connecting problem-owners to solvers) and 70 'technology entrepreneurs' globally, P&G built a network to access external innovation systematically.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: Which of the following can NOT be patented?
options: A manufacturing process
A mathematical formula
A composition of matter
A machine
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Mathematical formulas cannot be patented, along with ideas, natural laws, and natural substances. Patentable items include: processes, machines, manufactured items, and compositions of matter ('recipes').

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: Apple's camera hardware team has approximately how many specialists working together in one functional group?
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Apple has 600+ camera hardware experts in one group, led by Graham Townsend. In a divisional structure, these experts would be scattered across iPhone, iPad, Mac divisions with much smaller teams. The functional structure creates a deep specialist bench that enables innovations like Portrait Mode.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: According to Smil, battery energy density has grown from 25 Wh/kg in 1900 to ~300 Wh/kg in 2022. This represents an annual growth rate of approximately:
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Battery energy density grew at only ~2% per year over 122 years (25 → 300 Wh/kg). This is far slower than Moore's Law (~35% per year for chips). Smil uses this to argue that progress in energy and materials is fundamentally slower than in computing.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: In Christensen's framework, Netflix initially appealed to which segment?
options: Blockbuster's most profitable mainstream customers
A niche of movie buffs and early DVD adopters
International markets underserved by US competitors
Corporate clients needing content libraries
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Netflix started as a niche service for movie buffs and early DVD adopters — NOT Blockbuster's core customers. Streaming later made it appeal to Blockbuster's mainstream audience. This follows the classic disruptive pattern: overlooked segment → foothold → move upmarket.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: Pfizer's Lipitor revenue dropped dramatically after patent expiry, from approximately $13B to:
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Lipitor revenue fell from ~$13B to ~$2B after patent expiry — illustrating why patents are rated 6.5/7 effectiveness in pharmaceuticals. Without patent protection, generic competitors entered and captured most of the market almost immediately.

Hard Mock Exam 4.q: According to Christensen (2015), for incumbents facing disruption, the strategy 'disrupt or be disrupted' is:
options: The best guiding principle for any established company
Only applicable to technology companies
Potentially misleading — it can cause firms to prematurely dismantle profitable businesses
Irrelevant because incumbents always lose to disruptors
Hard Mock Exam 4.explanation: Christensen warns that 'disrupt or be disrupted' can MISGUIDE. Incumbents should NOT dismantle still-profitable core businesses. Instead: continue investing in sustaining innovations for core customers AND create a separate division for the disruptive opportunity. Overreaction is as dangerous as inaction.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: The Barras reverse product cycle differs from the traditional A-U model because:
options: Product innovation always precedes process innovation
In services, process innovation comes FIRST, then product innovation follows
It only applies to manufacturing industries
Innovation rates remain constant throughout the cycle
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Barras (1986) showed that in SERVICE industries, the cycle is reversed: firms first adopt process innovations (e.g. IT systems to improve efficiency), which then enable entirely new product/service innovations. This is the opposite of A-U where product innovation leads.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: Henderson & Clark's architectural innovation is dangerous to incumbents because:
options: It requires entirely new components and knowledge
It reconfigures the linkages between EXISTING components in new ways
It is always more expensive than radical innovation
It can only occur in the fluid phase
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Architectural innovation keeps the same components but changes how they fit together. It's especially dangerous because incumbents don't realize their existing knowledge is being undermined — they see familiar components and assume they understand the product, but the architecture has shifted.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: Which type of innovation uses NEW components but the SAME architecture?
options: Incremental innovation
Architectural innovation
Modular innovation
Radical innovation
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Henderson & Clark's 2x2: Incremental = same components, same architecture. Modular = NEW components, same architecture. Architectural = same components, NEW architecture. Radical = new components, new architecture. Modular is the one students most often forget.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: The Bayh-Dole Act (1980) was significant because it:
options: Banned universities from conducting commercial research
Allowed universities to patent and license inventions from federally funded research
Required all patents to be filed within 1 year of invention
Created the US Patent and Trademark Office
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Before Bayh-Dole, inventions from federally funded research belonged to the government. The Act allowed universities to retain ownership, patent, and license these inventions. This dramatically increased university-industry technology transfer and spin-off creation.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: In the Abernathy-Utterback model, the fluid phase is characterized by:
options: Low product innovation and high process innovation
High product innovation, many competing designs, no dominant design yet
A single dominant design and focus on cost reduction
Equal rates of product and process innovation
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: The fluid phase: high product innovation, many firms experimenting with different designs, no dominant design. Transitional phase: dominant design emerges, product innovation declines, process innovation rises. Specific phase: process innovation peaks, focus on efficiency.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: Moral hazard in innovation financing refers to:
options: Investors being unable to distinguish good from bad projects before investing
Entrepreneurs changing their behavior AFTER receiving funding
Banks charging excessive interest rates on innovation loans
Government subsidies crowding out private investment
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Moral hazard = POST-investment problem. After getting funded, the entrepreneur may take excessive risks, shirk, or divert resources. Adverse selection = PRE-investment problem (can't tell good from bad). VCs mitigate moral hazard through staged funding, board seats, and covenants.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: Nokia's decline illustrates all of the following EXCEPT:
options: An atmosphere of fear that prevented bad news from reaching top management
Failure to transition from hardware to software/ecosystem competition
Lack of financial resources for R&D investment
The danger of incumbent blindness to new technology trajectories
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Nokia had plenty of money — at its peak it had $250B market cap. The problems were organizational: fear culture suppressed information flow, the company was locked into Symbian/hardware thinking, and management failed to recognize the shift to software ecosystems (iOS, Android). Resources were not the issue.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: Creative destruction (Schumpeter Mark I) differs from creative accumulation (Mark II) in that:
options: Mark I: new entrants destroy incumbents; Mark II: incumbents drive innovation through accumulated knowledge
Mark I: large firms dominate; Mark II: startups lead
Mark I: process innovation; Mark II: product innovation
Mark I and II are identical concepts with different names
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Mark I (creative destruction): entrepreneurs and new entrants drive innovation, destroying incumbents. Mark II (creative accumulation): large incumbents leverage existing R&D, knowledge, and resources to drive innovation, accumulating advantage over time. Both patterns coexist in different industries.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: The 'chasm' in Moore's technology adoption lifecycle exists between:
options: Innovators and early adopters
Early adopters and early majority
Early majority and late majority
Late majority and laggards
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: The chasm is between early adopters (visionaries, 13.5%) and early majority (pragmatists, 34%). Early adopters buy for competitive advantage and tolerate imperfection. The early majority wants proven, complete solutions. Many innovations die in this gap because the same marketing doesn't work for both groups.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: Real options thinking in project selection adds value over NPV because it:
options: Always produces a higher valuation
Eliminates uncertainty from the decision
Values the flexibility to abandon, expand, or delay a project as information is revealed
Requires fewer assumptions about future cash flows
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Real options values FLEXIBILITY — the right (not obligation) to abandon a failing project, expand a successful one, or delay until uncertainty resolves. NPV treats projects as 'now or never' decisions. Real options adds value to projects with high uncertainty and staged decision points.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: Willful patent infringement can result in penalties up to:
options: The original licensing fee
Double the lost profits
Triple damages
Criminal imprisonment
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Willful infringement can result in up to TRIPLE damages (treble damages) plus injunction and legal fees. This makes intentional infringement very risky. Regular infringement typically results in lost profits/reasonable royalties and injunction.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: P&G had 7,500 internal researchers, but their suppliers' combined R&D staff numbered:
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: P&G's suppliers had a combined R&D staff of 50,000 — nearly 7x P&G's own 7,500. This insight drove Connect and Develop: why limit yourself to your own researchers when there's a massive pool of external talent and ideas accessible through your supply chain?

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: In Apple's 2019 organizational chart, approximately how many functional areas did the company have?
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Apple grew from 8 functions (1998) to 17 functions (2019). New additions included Design, HW Technologies, Services, ML & AI, Marketing Comm., Retail, People, Corporate Comm., Environment/Policy/Social, and Corporate Dev. Yet all still under ONE P&L — no divisional structure.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: Kodak dominated photography for 100 years using all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Patent protection
Tacit knowledge
Time dependency advantages
Open-source technology sharing
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Kodak used patents, tacit knowledge (hard to imitate expertise), and time dependency (accumulated advantages over decades). They did NOT share technology openly — their strategy was proprietary protection. Despite all this, digital photography disrupted them because it was a completely different technology trajectory.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: Increasing returns to adoption means that:
options: Prices always increase as more people adopt a technology
The more a technology is adopted, the more valuable and likely to be further adopted it becomes
Returns on investment increase linearly with market share
Only the first adopters benefit from the technology
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Increasing returns: adoption → more value → more adoption (positive feedback loop). Sources include network externalities (more users = more value, e.g. phone network), learning effects, and complementary goods. This can lead to lock-in and winner-take-all dynamics.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: The Portrait Mode feature on iPhone 7 Plus was first conceived by Paul Hubel in:
options: 2007, alongside the original iPhone
2009, seven years before the feature launched
2014, two years before launch
2016, just months before the iPhone 7 Plus shipped
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Hubel conceived the idea for computational bokeh (portrait effect) in 2009 — seven years before it launched with iPhone 7 Plus in 2016. The feature was even deferred one year to address edge cases like chicken wire. This timeline shows how long breakthrough features can take even at Apple.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: The Moderna case describes a 2012 incident where CEO Bancel was horrified to find a scientist:
options: Using outdated laboratory equipment
Manually entering a nucleotide sequence into Excel
Publishing research results without peer review
Outsourcing drug design to a contractor
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Bancel found a scientist manually entering a nucleotide sequence in Excel and was horrified — 'if any cell was wrong, the entire drug design would be useless.' This incident drove Moderna to create a 'drug design studio' where drugs are designed on computers, leading to their 'born digital' identity.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: In a technology S-curve, the inflection point represents:
options: The point where the technology becomes obsolete
The point where improvement rate is highest — maximum acceleration
The exact moment a dominant design emerges
The point where all firms exit the market
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: The inflection point is where the S-curve is steepest — the rate of improvement is at its maximum. Before this point, progress is slow (investment phase). After it, diminishing returns set in. This is also where ATAP looks for opportunities — recognizing inflection points in science.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: A firm uses a functional organizational structure (like Apple). Which statement is TRUE?
options: Each product line has its own P&L and general manager
Decision rights are aligned with expertise rather than product accountability
It is only suitable for small companies under 1,000 employees
Communication is primarily vertical within product divisions
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Functional structures align EXPERTISE with DECISION RIGHTS — those who know most make the decisions. Multi-divisional structures align accountability with control (each GM owns a P&L). Apple proves functional structures can work at massive scale (137,000 employees, $260B revenue).

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: How many of the three competitors' imitation methods involve reading publicly available documents?
options: None — all require covert activity
One — reading patent applications
Two — reading patents and academic papers
All three methods use public information
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: The three imitation methods are: (1) reverse engineering, (2) hiring experts, (3) reading patent applications. Only reading patent applications uses publicly available documents. This is an irony of patents — you must disclose your invention publicly, which helps competitors understand and potentially work around it.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: Denmark, Ireland, and Germany have the highest share of wind and solar energy in the EU. According to Smil, they also have:
options: The lowest electricity prices
The highest electricity prices
Average electricity prices
The most stable electricity grids
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Smil points out the irony: countries with the highest renewable energy share (Denmark, Ireland, Germany) also have the highest electricity prices in the EU. He uses this to argue that the transition to renewables is more complex and costly than commonly presented.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: Moderna had developed how many vaccines BEFORE the COVID-19 vaccine?
options: None — COVID was their first
3 vaccines
9 vaccines
15 vaccines
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Moderna had 9 vaccines in development before COVID — including a 2-year MERS vaccine collaboration with Fauci/NIH. COVID was their TENTH vaccine. The platform was already proven; COVID just demonstrated its speed. This is why spinning off COVID would have been wrong — it was one 'app' on an existing platform.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process) as a decision tool is best described as:
options: A simulation that runs thousands of random scenarios
A method that ranks alternatives by comparing them against weighted criteria in pairs
An anonymous expert opinion process
A discounted cash flow analysis
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: AHP structures complex decisions by breaking them into criteria, weighting the criteria, then doing pairwise comparisons of alternatives against each criterion. It's semi-quantitative — uses numbers but based on subjective judgments. Different from Monte Carlo (simulation), Delphi (expert opinion), and NPV (cash flow).

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: In the adopter categories model, the late majority (34%) is characterized by:
options: Willingness to take risks on unproven technology
Adopting only after most of the market has already adopted, driven by peer pressure or necessity
Never adopting new technologies under any circumstances
Being opinion leaders who influence the early majority
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Late majority (34%) adopts out of economic necessity or peer pressure — NOT enthusiasm. They are skeptical and risk-averse, waiting until a technology is well-established. Innovators (2.5%) take risks. Early adopters (13.5%) are opinion leaders. Laggards (16%) resist until forced.

Hard Mock Exam 5.q: Eli Lilly's marketers surveyed endocrinologists to assess demand for Humulin. According to the case, this was a critical error because:
options: Endocrinologists had no knowledge of insulin products
Endocrinologists represented the most demanding lead users, not the mainstream market
The survey sample size was too small
Endocrinologists recommended Novo Nordisk's pen instead
Hard Mock Exam 5.explanation: Endocrinologists were the MOST DEMANDING, HIGH-END customers who emphasized the need for pure human insulin. But mainstream patients (and their GPs) were 'pretty happy with pork insulin' at 10 ppm. Lilly made the classic Christensen mistake: listening to lead users and overshooting mainstream needs.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: Which of the following is most likely to be a competence-destroying discontinuity for an incumbent?
options: An improvement in the manufacturing process of existing products
A shift to a fundamentally different knowledge base that renders existing expertise obsolete
A price reduction strategy by a new competitor
An incremental upgrade to the current product line
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: Competence-destroying discontinuities require entirely new knowledge/skills, making the incumbent's accumulated expertise obsolete. This is what happened to Kodak (chemical film → digital) and Nokia (hardware → software ecosystems).

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: As part of which strategic action should you use licensing and joint ventures to share risk with partners?
options: Maintaining flexibility
Minimizing investment
Seeking high returns
Reallocating uncertainty to others
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: Reallocating uncertainty to others means transferring risk to partners through licensing, joint ventures, outsourcing, or franchise models. You let others bear part of the financial risk.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: Which group of adopters is typically more than two standard deviations before the mean?
options: Early adopters
Innovators
Early majority
Late majority
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: Innovators (2.5%) are more than 2 standard deviations before the mean. Early adopters (13.5%) are 1-2 standard deviations before. Early majority (34%) and late majority (34%) are around the mean.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: The political environment influences the rules of the society in which technology advance takes place.
options: True
False
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: True. The POLITICAL environment influences the rules of society. Note: the economic environment influences the RESOURCES available for innovation, not the rules. This is a common trap — don't confuse economic and political.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: What quantitative method evaluates projects by calculating a percentage return that can be compared against a hurdle rate?
options: Net present value
Analytical hierarchy process
Internal rate of return
Real options
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: IRR calculates the discount rate that yields NPV = 0, expressed as a percentage. If IRR > hurdle rate, the project is worth pursuing. NPV gives a dollar amount, not a percentage.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: Which of the following is NOT a way competitors can learn about a patented invention?
options: Reverse engineering the product
Hiring the inventor's employees
Reading the patent application
Accessing the inventor's trade secrets database
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: The three ways competitors imitate patented inventions are: reverse engineering, hiring experts, and reading patent applications (which are public). Trade secrets databases are private and protected — accessing them would be illegal corporate espionage, not a standard method.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: In the Abernathy-Utterback model, when does the rate of product innovation peak?
options: During the specific phase
During the transitional phase
During the fluid phase
After the dominant design has been established
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: Product innovation peaks during the fluid phase — many competing designs, no dominant design yet. After dominant design emerges (transitional phase), product innovation declines and process innovation rises. Process innovation peaks in the specific phase.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: Nokia's engineers never developed touchscreen technology before Apple's iPhone launch.
options: True
False
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: False. Nokia engineers DID create touchscreen devices before the iPhone. The problem was organizational — an atmosphere of fear prevented information from flowing upward, and management failed to act on the technology they already had.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: Which segment of the market is most concerned with proven track records and established standards before adopting?
options: Innovators
Early adopters
Early majority
Late majority
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: The early majority (pragmatists, 34%) wants to see a whole product solution, value proposition, and proven track record before buying. They are practical — unlike early adopters who buy for competitive advantage and tolerate imperfection.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: As part of which strategic action should you demand compensation proportional to the level of risk you are taking?
options: Maintaining flexibility
Minimizing investment
Seeking high returns
Reallocating uncertainty to others
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: Seeking high returns means if you accept high risk, you demand high potential payoff to compensate. This is the VC logic: target 5-10x returns because most investments fail. Risk must be matched by potential reward.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: A patent grants the holder the right to exclude others from making, using, or selling the invention for how many years?
options: 10 years
15 years
20 years
Life of the inventor plus 70 years
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: Patents last 20 years from filing date. Copyright = life + 70 years (automatic). Trademarks = 10 years (renewable). Don't confuse patent and copyright duration — it's a common exam trap.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: Procter & Gamble's Connect and Develop strategy involved outsourcing the 'develop' part of R&D.
options: True
False
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: False. P&G outsourced the CONNECT part (finding ideas externally), not the DEVELOP part. Development still happened internally at P&G. They found ideas outside through networks of technology entrepreneurs, NineSigma, and YourEncore, then developed them in-house.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: A technology that has broad applicability across many different industries and use cases is called a:
options: Complementary technology
Radical technology
General purpose technology
Dominant design
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: General purpose technologies (GPTs) have broad applicability across many sectors — e.g., electricity, the internet, AI. They are particularly important for new firms because they permit strategic flexibility.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: Which of the following is a primary reason for good companies to 'go bad'?
options: Excessive R&D spending
Competency traps
Too much customer focus
Lack of patents
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: Competency traps occur when a firm becomes so good at what it currently does that it cannot adapt to new technologies or markets. Other reasons include narrow technological focus and lack of agility. Excessive R&D spending is NOT listed as a primary reason.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: What term describes a situation where the more a technology is adopted, the more valuable it becomes to each user?
options: Diminishing returns
Technology overshooting
Increasing returns to adoption
Creative destruction
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: Increasing returns to adoption: more users → more value → more adoption (positive feedback loop). Sources include network externalities, learning effects, and complementary goods. This can lead to lock-in and winner-take-all dynamics.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: The economic environment influences the resources available for innovative activity.
options: True
False
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: True. The economic environment determines RESOURCES for innovation (capital, labor, market demand). The POLITICAL environment influences the RULES. The SOCIAL environment influences attitudes and demographics. Don't mix these up.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: Which decision-making tool is best suited when a firm needs to make sequential decisions as new information becomes available over time?
options: Net present value
Checklist
Analytical hierarchy process
Real options
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: Real options limits decision making to information known at the current stage. You make sequential decisions as uncertainty resolves — you can abandon, expand, or delay. NPV and IRR require estimating all future cash flows upfront.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: Nokia fell behind Apple primarily because it spent less on R&D.
options: True
False
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: False. Nokia actually spent MORE on R&D than Apple. The problem was organizational: fear culture, inability to transition from hardware to software/ecosystem thinking, and management blindness to the smartphone revolution. Money was not the issue.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: Which of the following forces is most directly related to changes in demographics and lifestyle trends?
options: Economic
Competitive
Political
Social
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: Social forces include demographics, lifestyle trends, attitudes, fears, and perceptions of risk. The creation of body odor products is the classic example of social change driving innovation.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: Any innovation opportunity that generates a positive NPV is always worth pursuing.
options: True
False
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: False. A positive NPV doesn't automatically mean you should invest. You must also consider: opportunity cost (is there a BETTER project?), strategic fit, risk tolerance, and whether the project aligns with company capabilities. Resources are limited.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: A beachhead strategy is most appropriate at which stage of market development?
options: After successfully crossing the chasm
During the decline stage
Before crossing the chasm — to gain initial foothold
During mass market adoption
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: A beachhead strategy is used BEFORE crossing the chasm — you focus on one narrow segment (the beachhead) to gain a strong foothold, then expand from there. It is NOT appropriate after the chasm has been crossed.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: What percentage of all commercially successful new products would not have been developed without academic research?
options: 5 percent
11 percent
25 percent
45 percent
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: 11% of commercially successful products trace back to academic research. This shows universities are important but most innovation comes from companies and individuals, not academia.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between fixed costs and production level?
options: Fixed costs increase proportionally with production
Fixed costs decrease as production increases
Fixed costs remain constant regardless of the level of production
Fixed costs are only incurred during peak production
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: Fixed costs (rent, loan payments, insurance) remain constant regardless of production level. Variable costs change with production. Total costs = fixed + variable. This is basic but Veisdal tests it.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: Development is best defined as:
options: The discovery of new scientific principles
The effort to use technical knowledge to produce something of commercial use
Basic research conducted at universities
The marketing of a new product
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: Development = using technical knowledge to create commercial products/processes. Research = discovering new knowledge. Don't confuse research (discovery) with development (application). The R&D process goes: research → invention → development → innovation.

Veisdal-Style Mock 1.q: When crossing the chasm, you should focus on a vertical marketing strategy rather than a horizontal one.
options: True
False
Veisdal-Style Mock 1.explanation: True. Vertical = targeting one specific industry/segment deeply (the beachhead). Horizontal = targeting many segments broadly. Crossing the chasm requires deep focus on one segment to build a complete solution and references before expanding.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: Which entity accounts for the majority of total R&D spending?
options: Universities
Government agencies
Companies
Individual inventors
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Companies account for the majority of R&D spending (over 70%). Universities and government agencies contribute much less. Of the entities that undertake innovation, companies are also the most prolific source.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: As part of which strategic action should you begin operations on a small scale?
options: Seeking high returns
Minimizing investment
Maintaining flexibility
Reallocating uncertainty to others
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Starting small = minimizing investment. You keep costs low until you know more about whether the opportunity is viable. This reduces the total amount at risk if the project fails.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: What describes the knowledge that cannot be readily codified or transferred in written form?
options: Explicit knowledge
Patent claims
Tacit knowledge
Trade secrets
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Tacit knowledge is knowledge that is difficult to write down, codify, or transfer — it's learned through experience and practice. Unlike trade secrets (which are deliberately kept hidden), tacit knowledge is inherently hard to communicate even if you wanted to share it.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: The S-curve shows that early investment in a technology typically produces:
options: Rapid performance improvement
Constant performance improvement
Very little performance improvement for each unit of effort
Decreasing performance improvement
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: At the bottom of the S-curve, performance improvement is slow despite significant effort — you're building foundational knowledge. The rapid improvement phase comes in the middle (steep part). At the top, diminishing returns set in.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: Which of the following is NOT a requirement for an invention to receive patent protection?
options: It must be useful
It must be novel
It must be non-obvious to a person skilled in the field
It must be commercially profitable
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: The three requirements are: useful, novel, and non-obvious. Commercial profitability is NOT required. You can patent something that never makes money. Many patented inventions are never commercialized.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: Diffusion refers to:
options: The process of inventing a new product
The rate at which a new technology becomes adopted by potential users
The decline in price of a product over time
The speed of R&D development
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Diffusion = the rate at which a technology spreads through potential users. It's different from adoption (an individual's decision to use) and innovation (creating something new). The Bass model is a key tool for predicting diffusion rates.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: In P&G's Connect and Develop strategy, external ideas were successful because P&G merely needed to find them and put them on the market.
options: True
False
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: False. C&D required strong INTERNAL capabilities to evaluate, develop, and commercialize external ideas. For every 100 ideas found, only 1 reached market. External ideas also had to have evidence of working (prototype or proven technology). It was NOT just about finding ideas.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: A firm in a highly uncertain market should primarily focus on which combination of strategic actions?
options: Seeking high returns and maximizing investment
Maintaining flexibility and minimizing investment
Reallocating uncertainty and seeking low returns
Maximizing investment and maintaining rigidity
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Under high uncertainty: maintain flexibility (keep options open, avoid lock-in) and minimize investment (reduce total risk). You don't commit heavily until uncertainty resolves. This is the real options logic applied to strategy.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: Which type of intellectual property protection does NOT require formal registration?
options: Patent
Trademark
Copyright
Design patent
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Copyright is automatic upon creation — no registration needed. It lasts life + 70 years. Patents and trademarks both require formal application/registration. This automatic nature makes copyright unique among IP protections.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: The 'chasm' exists between which two groups in the technology adoption lifecycle?
options: Innovators and early adopters
Early adopters and early majority
Early majority and late majority
Late majority and laggards
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: The chasm is between early adopters (visionaries, 13.5%) and early majority (pragmatists, 34%). Visionaries buy for competitive advantage; pragmatists want proven solutions. Different marketing strategies are needed for each — this is why many products die in this gap.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: In the DARPA model of innovation, which element is NOT part of the approach?
options: Ambitious goals
Temporary project teams
Independence from bureaucracy
General managers overseeing each project
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: DARPA uses NO general managers — projects are led by technical experts (program managers) on temporary assignments. The model includes ambitious goals, temporary teams, and independence. Google ATAP adopted the same approach.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: Qualitative decision-making tools compare projects on the basis of:
options: Numerical calculations only
Cash flow projections
Scales or words
Statistical simulations
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Qualitative tools use scales or words (e.g., high/medium/low, checklists). Quantitative tools use numerical calculations (NPV, IRR, Monte Carlo). AHP is semi-quantitative — it uses numbers but based on subjective pairwise comparisons.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: Which of the following best explains why established firms often fail to respond to disruptive technologies?
options: They lack the financial resources to invest
They focus on their most profitable customers and ignore less profitable segments
Their R&D teams are less talented than those at startups
Government regulations prevent them from entering new markets
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Christensen's core insight: incumbents focus on most demanding/profitable customers, overshooting mainstream needs and ignoring low-end or new-market segments. This opens the door for disruptors. Nokia and Eli Lilly both fell into this trap.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: What occurs during the 'shakeout' phase of industry evolution?
options: Many new firms enter the market
Product innovation reaches its peak
A large percentage of firms exit the market
The dominant design is first introduced
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: During shakeout, ~52% of firms exit. It occurs after the dominant design is established — competition shifts from product variation to efficiency and cost. Firms that can't compete on process efficiency are eliminated.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: Complementary technologies are technologies that:
options: Replace the focal technology entirely
Are used along with the focal technology
Compete directly with the focal technology
Are developed by government research labs
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Complementary technologies are used alongside the focal technology. Example: game software is complementary to game consoles. Complementary assets (manufacturing, distribution) are similarly important — Teece's framework shows how control of these determines who captures value.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: The creation of new products through changes in social attitudes is illustrated by which example?
options: The development of nuclear energy
The invention of the printing press
Products that mask body odor
The discovery of penicillin
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Body odor products emerged because of changing social attitudes about personal hygiene — a social/cultural change, not a technological one. Nuclear energy = political/technological. Printing press = technological. Penicillin = accidental technological innovation.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: A firm at the upper portion of its technology S-curve is most likely experiencing:
options: Very little performance improvement for each unit of effort
Rapid improvement for each unit of effort
Constant improvement for each unit of effort
Decreasing improvement for each unit of effort
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: At the top of the S-curve: decreasing/diminishing returns — each unit of effort produces less improvement. This is when a firm should invest in alternative technologies. Veisdal uses both 'very little' (bottom) and 'decreasing' (top) — the distinction is subtle.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: Adverse selection in innovation financing occurs:
options: After the investment has been made
Before the investment — when investors cannot distinguish good from bad projects
Only in government-funded research
When entrepreneurs have too much information
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Adverse selection = PRE-investment information asymmetry. Investors can't tell good from bad projects before investing. Moral hazard = POST-investment — entrepreneur may change behavior after receiving funds. Key distinction for the exam.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: A comparative decision-making tool is best described as one that:
options: Evaluates projects using numerical cash flow calculations
Compares projects against standard scales
Pits projects against each other
Uses expert panels to rank projects
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Comparative tools pit projects AGAINST EACH OTHER (e.g., AHP pairwise comparison). This is different from individual evaluation (NPV — each project evaluated alone) or standard scales (checklists — compared against criteria, not each other).

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: Nokia's failure was primarily caused by insufficient R&D investment.
options: True
False
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: False. Nokia spent MORE on R&D than Apple. The failure was organizational: an atmosphere of fear suppressed honest reporting, the company was locked into hardware/Symbian thinking, and leadership chose Windows Phone over Android. Resources were not the problem.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: What is the correct sequence in the process model of R&D investment?
options: Innovation → Invention → R&D → Product
Investment in R&D → Invention → Innovation → New products
Invention → R&D → Product → Innovation
R&D → Innovation → Invention → Product
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: The process model: Investment in R&D → technological invention → innovation (practical implementation) → new or improved products/processes. Investment in R&D must come BEFORE invention, not after.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: Which IP protection can potentially last forever?
options: Patent
Trade secret
Copyright
Design patent
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Trade secrets last indefinitely — as long as the information remains secret. Coca-Cola's formula has been protected for 130+ years. Patents expire after 20 years, copyright after life + 70 years, trademarks after 10 years (but are renewable).

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: Which adopter category is most price-sensitive and requires strong evidence of low cost and reliability?
options: Innovators
Early adopters
Late majority
Laggards
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Laggards (16%) are the most price-sensitive and skeptical. They adopt only when the technology is mature, cheap, and essentially unavoidable. Advertising to laggards should emphasize reliability and low cost. Late majority is also skeptical but adopts earlier than laggards.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: Most opportunities for innovation result from:
options: A single technological breakthrough
Government regulation alone
A combination of technological, political, social and demographic changes
Individual genius inventors
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Most innovation opportunities arise from a COMBINATION of factors — technological, political/regulatory, social, and demographic changes interact to create opportunities. Rarely does a single factor drive innovation alone.

Veisdal-Style Mock 2.q: The Selden patent case demonstrates that broad patents can be circumvented through:
options: Lobbying for patent law changes
Waiting for the patent to expire
Alternative technical approaches
Ignoring the patent entirely
Veisdal-Style Mock 2.explanation: Ford circumvented Selden's broad automobile patent by using a four-cycle engine instead of the patented Brayton two-stroke. Key lesson: broad patents can be worked around through alternative technical approaches. AutoStore vs Ocado mirrors this same dynamic.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: Which force determines the availability of capital and human resources for innovative activity?
options: Political
Social
Economic
Competitive
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: The economic environment determines RESOURCES (capital, labor, market size). Political = rules. Social = attitudes/demographics. Competitive = actions of other firms. This is a frequently tested distinction.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: As part of which strategic action should you use modular designs that can be adapted for different uses?
options: Maintaining flexibility
Minimizing investment
Seeking high returns
Reallocating uncertainty to others
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: Modular, adaptable designs = maintaining flexibility. You keep options open by avoiding commitment to a single configuration. If the market shifts, you can reconfigure without starting over. This is different from minimizing investment (spending less overall).

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: Which phase of the Abernathy-Utterback model is characterized by a focus on cost reduction and efficient production?
options: Fluid phase
Transitional phase
Specific phase
Innovation phase
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: The specific phase: dominant design is established, product innovation is low, process innovation peaks, competition is based on cost reduction and efficiency. Firms that survive the shakeout compete by producing the dominant design more efficiently.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: What is the primary reason for the 'gap' or 'chasm' between early adopters and the early majority?
options: Price differences between the groups
Early adopters want competitive advantage while the early majority wants proven, complete solutions
Geographic distance between the groups
Government regulations slow adoption
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: Early adopters (visionaries) buy for competitive advantage and tolerate incomplete products. The early majority (pragmatists) wants proven solutions with references and full product packages. The same sales pitch doesn't work for both — this is why products die in the chasm.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: Over 70% of total R&D spending comes from colleges and universities.
options: True
False
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: False. Over 70% of R&D spending comes from COMPANIES, not universities. Universities contribute to basic research but account for a much smaller share of total R&D expenditure.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: What does the term 'dominant design' refer to?
options: The product with the highest market share
A product architecture that becomes the accepted standard in the industry
The most innovative product on the market
The cheapest product available to consumers
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: A dominant design is the architecture/configuration that becomes the industry standard. After it emerges, competition shifts from product variation to process innovation. Examples: QWERTY keyboard, VHS over Betamax, the basic car layout.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: Which type of innovation keeps existing components but changes how they are linked together?
options: Incremental innovation
Radical innovation
Architectural innovation
Modular innovation
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: Henderson & Clark: Architectural = same components, NEW linkages/architecture. Incremental = same both. Modular = new components, same architecture. Radical = new both. Architectural innovation is especially dangerous to incumbents because they don't realize their knowledge is being undermined.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: For which adopter segment would a marketing message emphasizing cutting-edge technology and visionary potential be most effective?
options: Innovators
Early adopters
Early majority
Late majority
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: Early adopters (13.5%) are visionaries who want competitive advantage. They respond to messages about strategic potential and being ahead of competitors. Innovators are technology enthusiasts (different motivation). Early majority wants practical proof.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: The internal rate of return is the discount rate that yields a net present value of zero.
options: True
False
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: True. IRR is exactly the discount rate where NPV = 0. If IRR > hurdle rate (required return), the project is worth pursuing. If IRR < hurdle rate, reject it.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: Which of the following is NOT something that can be patented?
options: A process for manufacturing steel
A new machine for harvesting crops
A mathematical formula for predicting weather
A new chemical compound for treating disease
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: Mathematical formulas cannot be patented, along with ideas, natural laws, and natural substances. Processes, machines, manufactured items, and compositions of matter (chemical compounds) CAN be patented.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: Nokia failed because it did not create an operating system.
options: True
False
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: False. Nokia DID have an operating system — Symbian. The problem was that Symbian couldn't compete with iOS and Android's app ecosystems. Nokia's failure was about ecosystem and software strategy, not the absence of an OS.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: A technology that permits strategic flexibility for new firms and has broad applicability is called a:
options: Radical innovation
Sustaining technology
General purpose technology
Disruptive technology
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: General purpose technologies (GPTs) have broad applicability and enable strategic flexibility — new firms can apply them across many different use cases. Examples: electricity, internet, AI, steam engine.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: Which of the following is a characteristic of a mechanistic organizational structure?
options: Flat hierarchy with few management levels
Decentralized decision making
Informal communication channels
Centralized decision making with formal rules and procedures
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: Mechanistic = centralized decisions, formal rules, many hierarchy levels, specialized roles, vertical communication. Organic = the opposite on all dimensions. Mechanistic suits stable environments; organic suits uncertain/innovative environments.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: What happens when a technology trajectory exceeds what mainstream customers need?
options: Market share automatically increases
The basis of competition shifts to a dimension where the incumbent may not be dominant
Customers willingly pay premium prices for the extra performance
The technology enters a new S-curve
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: Overshooting: when technology exceeds mainstream needs, the basis of competition shifts. Eli Lilly overshot insulin purity (customers were 'pretty happy with pork insulin'), allowing Novo Nordisk to compete on convenience instead.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: Venture capital firms typically charge a management fee of:
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: Standard VC: 2% management fee + 20-30% carried interest (profit share). They target 5-10x returns over ~7 years. The extreme skewness means 0.4% of investments generate ~97% of total returns.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: The Barras reverse product cycle describes how in service industries:
options: Product innovation always comes before process innovation
Process innovation comes first, then enables new product/service innovations
Innovation follows the same pattern as manufacturing
Only radical innovations occur
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: Barras (1986) reversed the A-U model for services: process innovation first (e.g., IT systems for efficiency), then those process improvements enable entirely new services (product innovation). Opposite of manufacturing where product innovation leads.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: Which is NOT one of the four strategic actions for dealing with uncertainty in innovation?
options: Maintaining flexibility
Minimizing investment
Maximizing market share
Seeking high returns
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: The four strategic actions are: (1) maintaining flexibility, (2) minimizing investment, (3) seeking high returns, (4) reallocating uncertainty to others. Maximizing market share is a business goal, not one of Shane's four strategies for handling innovation uncertainty.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: In the VHS vs Betamax standards battle, VHS won primarily because of:
options: Superior video quality
Lower manufacturing cost
Network externalities and increasing returns to adoption
Government regulation favoring VHS
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: VHS won despite Betamax having arguably better quality. VHS had more content (longer recording time attracted movie studios), which attracted more users, which attracted more content — a positive feedback loop. Classic example of increasing returns and network externalities.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: Moral hazard in the context of financing innovation refers to:
options: The risk that investors cannot distinguish good projects from bad ones
The risk that entrepreneurs change behavior after receiving funding
The risk of market downturns affecting innovation
The risk of patent infringement
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: Moral hazard = POST-investment risk that the entrepreneur may shirk, take excessive risks, or divert resources after getting funded. Adverse selection = PRE-investment inability to distinguish good from bad projects. VCs mitigate moral hazard through staged funding, board seats, and covenants.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: What is the typical adoption percentage for the early majority segment?
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: Early majority = 34%. Full distribution: innovators 2.5%, early adopters 13.5%, early majority 34%, late majority 34%, laggards 16%. The early and late majority each represent the largest segments at 34%.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: Which appropriability mechanism allows Coca-Cola to protect its formula for over 130 years?
options: Patent
Copyright
Trade secret
Trademark
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: Coca-Cola uses trade secret — the formula has never been disclosed publicly. A patent would have expired after 20 years AND required public disclosure. Trade secrets last indefinitely as long as secrecy is maintained. The Nike swoosh is protected by trademark, Disney by copyright.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: In Apple's functional organizational structure, R&D executive bonuses are based on:
options: Individual product line P&L performance
Companywide performance
Department-level revenue targets
Number of patents filed
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: Apple runs under ONE P&L — executive bonuses are based on companywide performance, not individual product results. This encourages cross-functional collaboration rather than internal competition between product divisions.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: P&G's R&D spending as a percentage of sales changed from:
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: P&G reduced R&D from 4.8% to 3.4% of sales while MORE THAN DOUBLING their innovation success rate. This was achieved through Connect and Develop — sourcing ideas externally while developing them internally.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: Which of the following is the strongest motivation for firms to seek patent protection?
options: Licensing revenue
Enhancing company reputation
Preventing copying
Use in negotiation
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: Preventing copying: 96% (top motivation). Blocking others: 82%. Preventing lawsuits: 59%. Reputation: 48%. Negotiation: 47%. Licensing: only 28%. Most firms patent defensively, not to generate licensing income.

Veisdal-Style Mock 3.q: Moderna's competitive advantage came from its integrated, cross-functional platform approach. Spinning off the COVID business would have:
options: Increased speed of vaccine development
Created the silos Moderna specifically designed its organization to avoid
Reduced costs through focused operations
Improved regulatory approval timelines
Veisdal-Style Mock 3.explanation: Moderna's advantage was 'shared understanding and shared data across all departments and teams.' Spinning off COVID would create silos — the exact organizational structure Moderna had 'seen the detrimental effects of in big pharma.' The integrated platform enables cross-learning between all 23 programs.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: Which force influences the RULES of the society in which technology advance takes place?
options: Economic
Competitive
Political
Social
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: POLITICAL = rules/regulations. ECONOMIC = resources. SOCIAL = attitudes/demographics. COMPETITIVE = actions of other firms. You answered 'competitive' on the real quiz. The economic environment influences resources, NOT rules.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: The economic environment influences the rules of the society in which the technology advance takes place.
options: True
False
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: False. The POLITICAL environment influences the rules. The ECONOMIC environment influences the RESOURCES available for innovation. You got this wrong on Quiz 1 by selecting True.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: The Pentium computer processor is an example of a radical technology.
options: True
False
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: False. Pentium is incremental — same x86 architecture, same component types, just faster. Radical would be a fundamentally different computing paradigm (quantum computing, neuromorphic chips).

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: As a group, early adopters are:
options: Smaller than innovators
Larger than innovators
About the same size as innovators
Depends on the product
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: Early adopters (13.5%) are LARGER than innovators (2.5%). You selected 'about the same' on the real quiz. Remember: 2.5 / 13.5 / 34 / 34 / 16.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: For which segment is it most important to develop an advertising message emphasizing reliability and low cost?
options: Innovators
Early majority
Late majority
Laggards
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: LAGGARDS — not late majority. Laggards (16%) are the most price-sensitive and skeptical. They only adopt when it's cheap, proven, and unavoidable. You selected late majority on the real quiz. Late majority is skeptical too, but laggards are the extreme.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: Which of the following is NOT an attribute of technological change associated with subsequent innovation?
options: The magnitude of the change
The commercial viability of the change
The specificity of the change
All of the above are attributes
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: Commercial viability is NOT an attribute of the technological change itself — it's an outcome. The two attributes are: magnitude (how big?) and specificity (how broad/narrow?). You may have confused this — the correct answer to 'which is NOT' is commercial viability.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: In P&G's Connect and Develop, the innovation success rate doubled merely by implementing the external search strategy.
options: True
False
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: False. It wasn't 'merely' — C&D required massive internal capabilities to screen (100:1 ratio), evaluate, and develop external ideas. They also needed 70 technology entrepreneurs, networks like NineSigma and YourEncore, and strong internal R&D. You got this wrong by selecting True.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: Which of the following is NOT a primary reason for good companies to go bad?
options: Competency traps
Lack of innovativeness
Narrow technological focus
Lack of agility
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: Lack of innovativeness is NOT listed — good companies that go bad are often VERY innovative, just in the wrong direction. The real reasons: competency traps, narrow technological focus, and lack of agility. You selected this incorrectly on Quiz 3.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: As part of which strategic action should you minimize your investment in non-salvageable assets?
options: Seeking high returns
Minimizing investment
Maintaining flexibility
Reallocating uncertainty to others
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: MINIMIZING INVESTMENT — not maintaining flexibility. Non-salvageable = money you can't get back. Minimizing investment = spend less overall. Maintaining flexibility = keep options open with modular/adaptable approaches. You got this wrong on both the real quiz AND when I first explained it.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: Wholly proprietary systems differ from wholly open systems in that wholly proprietary systems:
options: Comprise technology based on available standards openly diffused to producers
Are typically compatible with products offered by other manufacturers
Provide their developers with the opportunity to appropriate rents from the technology
Comprise technologies that may be freely accessed by anyone
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: Proprietary = you control it = you can appropriate rents (capture value). The other options describe OPEN systems. You got this wrong on Quiz 5 by selecting the open-system description.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: A service mark distinguishes the provider of a service rather than a product.
options: True
False
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: True. Service mark = services (FedEx delivery). Trademark = products (Nike shoes). In practice they're treated similarly, but the distinction is tested. You got this right on Quiz 5 — keeping it here for reinforcement.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: To innovate successfully, companies need to be concerned with:
options: Centralization only
Efficiency only
Creating new companies only
All of the above
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: ALL of the above — centralization, efficiency, AND creating new companies. Innovation requires balancing multiple organizational concerns. You selected 'efficiency' alone on Quiz 6.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: What advantage of decentralization was Microsoft trying to exploit when it reorganized its centralized structure?
options: Improves access to information
Facilitates innovation in noncore areas
Facilitates idea generation
Improved coordination
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: Facilitates IDEA GENERATION. Microsoft's centralized structure was stifling creativity — decentralization gives more people autonomy to generate ideas. You selected 'improves access to information' on the real quiz. Note: 'facilitates innovation in noncore areas' is a DIFFERENT advantage of decentralization.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: What is a benefit of centralization?
options: Encourages companies to invest in radical innovation
Increases market orientation
Reduces financial risk to the overall organization
Makes it easier to get government aid
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: Centralization ENCOURAGES RADICAL INNOVATION — because top management can direct large investments toward risky, long-term bets without needing approval from multiple divisions. You selected 'reduces financial risk' on Quiz 6.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: 3M changed its structure so managers of all R&D units report to the same senior manager. This was an attempt to:
options: Increase the mechanistic nature
Increase the organic nature
Increase centralization
Increase decentralization
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: Having all R&D managers report to ONE senior manager = CENTRALIZATION (decisions flow to one point). You selected 'increase organic nature' — but organic vs centralized are different dimensions. Organic = flat/informal. Centralized = decisions at top. You can be organic AND centralized.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: General Motors resisted pressure to cut R&D during financial difficulties. This was primarily an attempt to:
options: Increase efficiency
Reduce costs
Focus on the long-term as compared to the short-term
Eliminate dead-end costs
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: LONG-TERM FOCUS. Cutting R&D saves money short-term but destroys future innovation. GM chose to protect R&D spending despite financial pressure = prioritizing long-term over short-term. You selected 'eliminate dead-end costs.'

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: What is more consistent with a large firm compared to a small one?
options: Large firms have an increased ability to colocate marketing and engineering
Large firms promote fewer talented researchers to management
Large firms produce innovation at lower R&D cost
Large firms improve the quality of human capital
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: Large firms IMPROVE QUALITY OF HUMAN CAPITAL — they can attract better talent with higher salaries, training programs, and career paths. You selected 'colocate marketing and engineering' — but colocation is actually HARDER in large firms due to size and bureaucracy.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: Small firms tend to be more innovative in which type of industry?
options: Less advertising intensive
Older industries
More concentrated
Less reliant on skilled labor
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: LESS ADVERTISING INTENSIVE. In advertising-heavy industries, big firms have the advantage (economies of scale in marketing). Small firms thrive where advertising matters less and technical innovation matters more. You selected 'more concentrated' — but concentrated industries favor incumbents.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: Options have positive value if the expected cash flow from making the investment is more than the value of maintaining the option.
options: True
False
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: False (per Shane p. 106). The value of an option comes from the FLEXIBILITY to wait — not from the cash flow exceeding the option value. When cash flows exceed option value, you EXERCISE the option (invest), but that's about when to act, not about whether the option has value.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: Felix designed foldable sunglasses by adding a hinge. Can he receive a patent?
options: No — the invention is only eligible for copyright protection
No — he is merely making the sunglasses more portable
No — the invention is not useful
Yes — the invention is a tangible product
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: He CANNOT patent it because adding a hinge is merely making sunglasses more portable — it fails the NON-OBVIOUS requirement. A person skilled in eyewear design would consider this an obvious modification, not an invention.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: In the United States, an inventor can publish an invention up to how long before applying for a patent?
options: 30 days
6 months
1 year
There is no grace period
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: In the US, there is a 1-year grace period — you can publish your invention and still file a patent within one year. This is unusual — most countries require filing BEFORE any public disclosure.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: Patent trolling refers to:
options: Filing for a patent but not receiving approval
Owning patents primarily to extort money from other firms
A dense web of overlapping patents making competition difficult
Patenting inventions in countries where you won't produce
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: Patent trolling (NPEs) = acquiring patents WITHOUT intent to make products, then suing cash-rich firms for infringement to extract settlements. The 'dense web of overlapping patents' describes a PATENT THICKET — a different concept.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: If you register a trademark in a Madrid Union member country, it is protected throughout the world.
options: True
False
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: False. Madrid Union simplifies FILING in multiple countries, but you still need to register in each country individually through the system. One registration does NOT automatically give worldwide protection.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: An advantage of corporate venturing is that it reduces risks on companies.
options: True
False
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: False. Corporate venturing can actually INCREASE risk — it involves investing in uncertain new ventures. A disadvantage is it can interfere with ongoing operations. The advantages are about exploring new opportunities and accessing innovation outside the core business.

Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.q: Functional organizations only work best for companies selling to innovators and early adopters.
options: True
False
Revenge Round: Every Mistake Drilled.explanation: False. Functional organizations can work across ALL adopter segments. Apple proves this — functional structure at $260B revenue, selling to mainstream consumers. The type of organizational structure is not determined by which adopter category you target.

Curveball Exam 1.q: First movers in a market have a failure rate of approximately:
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: First movers fail 47% of the time and achieve only ~10% market share on average. Late movers can free-ride on first mover's R&D, correct mistakes, and leapfrog with newer technology. Examples: Google beat Lycos, Microsoft beat VisiCalc and Netscape.

Curveball Exam 1.q: According to R&D managers, which mechanism is MOST effective at deterring imitation?
options: Patents (33% effectiveness)
Complementary assets like manufacturing/marketing (43%)
Lead time advantage (~50%)
Trade secrets (25%)
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Lead time (~50%) is rated most effective, followed by complementary assets (43%), then patents (33%). This is counterintuitive — most people assume patents are the best protection. In practice, being first and having the right assets matters more than legal protection.

Curveball Exam 1.q: The Bass model of technology diffusion is best suited for predicting adoption of:
options: Software-as-a-service products
Consumer durables whose value is not exhausted in one year
Competing technologies in the same market
Government-funded research programs
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: The Bass model works best for consumer durables (appliances, cars, TVs). It is LESS accurate for: competing technologies, economic shocks, and supply-constrained markets. It also cannot estimate at the very beginning (needs prior period data).

Curveball Exam 1.q: Xerox invented the mouse, GUI, and Ethernet but lost billions because it failed to:
options: Patent these inventions
Connect its research inventions to its operating units
Hire enough engineers
Market the products internationally
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Xerox PARC invented groundbreaking technology but failed to LINK research to development — the classic R&D disconnect. Apple and Microsoft commercialized the GUI instead. Shane uses this as the key example of why firms need policies to connect research labs to business units.

Curveball Exam 1.q: Which of the following is NOT one of Shane's four solutions for linking research to development?
options: Financial incentives aligned with development success
Shared equipment and materials between R&D units
Hiring external consultants to bridge the gap
Temporary development rotations for new researchers
