Curveball Exam 1.explanation: The four solutions are: (1) financial incentives, (2) shared equipment/materials, (3) temporary development rotations, (4) strategic fit criteria for funding decisions. Hiring external consultants is NOT one of the four.

Curveball Exam 1.q: Absorptive capacity is the ability to:
options: Maximize production output per employee
Recognize the value of external knowledge and apply it
Absorb financial losses during product development
Process large amounts of customer data
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Absorptive capacity = ability to recognize, value, and apply external knowledge. IBM researched X-ray lithography not to use it, but to SPOT outside breakthroughs. This is critical for open innovation — firms without absorptive capacity cannot benefit from external ideas.

Curveball Exam 1.q: When complementary assets are SPECIALIZED rather than generic, a firm should:
options: Contract for them from external suppliers
License the technology to a partner
Own them rather than contract for them
Share them through a joint venture
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Specialized assets cannot be redeployed cheaply. Contracting fails because of the 'opportunistic renegotiation problem' — the supplier can exploit your dependence to strike a better deal. Generic assets CAN be contracted. Example: cell phone companies could contract for switching networks (generic) but not for specialized chip designs.

Curveball Exam 1.q: A noncompete agreement in the United States must be:
options: Unlimited in duration to be valid
Limited in length and geographic breadth, or it may be declared invalid
Approved by a federal court before it takes effect
Signed only by employees earning above minimum wage
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Noncompete agreements must be limited in both TIME and GEOGRAPHY — otherwise courts declare them invalid. They also cannot preclude someone from earning a living in their chosen field. California cannot enforce noncompete agreements at all.

Curveball Exam 1.q: Under the work-for-hire doctrine, copyright belongs to:
options: The individual creator in all cases
The commissioning entity if the work was done within scope of employment
The patent office that registered the work
Whoever publishes the work first
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Work-for-hire: copyright goes to the commissioning entity (employer), NOT the creator, if: (1) work was done within scope of employment, OR (2) there's a written agreement requesting work on the contractor's behalf.

Curveball Exam 1.q: The Patent Cooperation Treaty preserves the right to apply for patent protection in member countries for:
options: 6 months
12 months
30 months
5 years
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: PCT preserves patent application rights for 30 months across ~100 countries. Other key treaties: Paris Convention (160 countries, prohibits differential rights), European Patent Convention (single application), Madrid Protocol (70 countries, trademarks), TRIPS (uniform 20-year patent length).

Curveball Exam 1.q: Technology substitution can take very different amounts of time. The fastest substitution studied was:
options: Margarine replacing butter (56 years)
Plastics replacing metals in cars (16 years)
Animal/detergent soap (9 years)
Synthetic rubber replacing natural (58 years)
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Animal/detergent soap substitution took only 9 years — the fastest. Synthetic rubber, synthetic fibers, and plastic leather all took 56-58 years. The speed matters enormously for strategy: if you bet on 20 years but it happens in 5, competitors enter before you're ready.

Curveball Exam 1.q: The 'cost spiral' in technology substitution occurs when:
options: New technology gets cheaper through economies of scale
Substitution reduces incumbent's production volume, raising costs, causing more substitution
Government subsidies artificially reduce costs of new technology
Patent expiration allows cheaper generic alternatives
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Cost spiral: substitution → old tech production volume drops → costs rise (loss of scale) → more customers switch → more volume loss → bankruptcy. This was observed with steel minimills disrupting integrated steel producers.

Curveball Exam 1.q: Before the mid-19th century, approximately what percentage of patenting inventors had NO formal scientific training?
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: 80% of patenting inventors had no formal scientific training before the mid-1800s. Innovation had low barriers to entry and 'technical simplicity.' The shift to institutional R&D began with Edison's Menlo Park lab (1876), and patenting per capita actually DROPPED as innovation consolidated within firms.

Curveball Exam 1.q: The TRIPS Agreement makes patent length uniform across all signatory countries at:
options: 10 years
15 years
20 years
25 years
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: TRIPS standardizes patents at 20 years across all signatories. It also requires patents on chemical/pharmaceutical products and restricts mandatory licensing. Other IP treaties focus on different aspects: PCT (application rights), Paris Convention (non-discrimination), Madrid Protocol (trademarks).

Curveball Exam 1.q: Lead users are valuable for innovation because they:
options: Represent the average customer's preferences
Are so dissatisfied with existing products that they create their own solutions
Always adopt technology last and provide final validation
Have the largest purchasing budgets
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Lead users are customers so unhappy with current products they're already building their own solutions. They're likely the first users of truly new products and eager beta testers. 3M uses a 4-phase lead user method involving 10-15 users in workshops. Examples: mountain bikes, Gatorade, sports bras.

Curveball Exam 1.q: The innovation waves identified by Edelson Institute have been getting:
options: Longer over time (60 → 80 years)
Shorter over time (60 → 25 years)
Staying constant at about 50 years
More unpredictable with no clear pattern
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Innovation waves are getting shorter: 1st wave ~60 years (water/textiles/iron, 1785), 2nd ~55 years (steam/rail, 1845), 3rd ~50 years (electricity, 1900), 4th ~40 years (electronics, 1950), 5th ~30 years (digital, 1990), 6th ~25 years (AI/IoT/clean tech, 2020).

Curveball Exam 1.q: Google has averaged approximately one acquisition per month over which period?
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Google averaged ~1 acquisition/month from 2000 to 2018 (nearly 2 decades). OECD found that firms often buy startups to eliminate competition before growth, and post-acquisition innovation often declines. Top 5 US tech firms = ~25% of top 500 public firms.

Curveball Exam 1.q: Conditions that favor trade secrecy OVER patents include all EXCEPT:
options: Few sources of information about the technology
Product complexity makes reverse engineering difficult
Knowledge is tacit and lives in people's heads
The product is easily observable by customers
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Observable products FAVOR patents over secrecy — because customers can reverse engineer them, making secrecy useless. Secrecy works when: few info sources, complex products, poorly understood processes, tacit knowledge, and knowledge not observable in use.

Curveball Exam 1.q: Kodak invented the digital CMOS sensor in what year?
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Kodak invented the digital CMOS sensor in 1976 — yet Sony and Apple commercialized the first digital cameras in the 1980s-90s. Classic case: inventing ≠ commercializing. Kodak's incumbent inertia and path dependency in chemical film prevented them from pursuing their own invention.

Curveball Exam 1.q: In the VC industry, what percentage of portfolio cost typically goes to investments returning less than 1x (failures)?
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: 63.2% of portfolio cost goes to <1x returns (failures). Only 4.3% of cost goes to >10x investments, but those generate 61.7% of total value. Of 4,000 firms seeking VC, only 200 raise money, and only 15 reach 1B NOK revenue.

Curveball Exam 1.q: A nondisclosure agreement must specify:
options: All employee knowledge as confidential
Exactly what information is secret and identify who can receive it
Only the employee's salary and title
The company's entire patent portfolio
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: NDAs must specify EXACTLY what info is secret (cannot be 'all employee knowledge'), provide consideration (salary counts), specify legitimate uses, identify people who can receive info, and state what happens to documents during/after employment.

Curveball Exam 1.q: In the aircraft industry, the innovation ratio of large to small firms is approximately:
options: 0.067 (small firms dominate)
1.0 (equal)
9.231 (large firms dominate)
31.000 (large firms strongly dominate)
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Aircraft: 31.000 (large firms dominate massively). Pharmaceutical: 9.231 (large dominate). Measuring/controlling devices: 0.067 (small firms dominate). Small firms are more innovative in younger, less R&D-intensive, less concentrated industries.

Curveball Exam 1.q: Moderna's 'virtuous cycle' in their AI factory model works by:
options: More patents → more licensing revenue → more R&D funding
More data → better algorithms → better mRNA medicines → more trials → more data
More employees → more ideas → more products → more employees
Lower prices → more customers → more revenue → lower prices
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Moderna's virtuous cycle: More data → Better algorithms → Better mRNA medicines → More trials → More data. By spring 2020 they had ~20 AI algorithms in production and ~20 in development. This data flywheel is core to their 'born digital' competitive advantage.

Curveball Exam 1.q: DNA sequencing performance has increased at a rate that:
options: Exactly matches Moore's Law
Is slower than Moore's Law
Is faster than Moore's Law
Has remained constant since 1977
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: DNA sequencing performance increased FASTER than Moore's Law — from Sanger's chain-termination (1977) through Illumina's bridge PCR (2004) to modern techniques. This is one of the few technology areas that outpaced semiconductor improvement, driven by biotech innovation cascades.

Curveball Exam 1.q: Organizational slack is important for innovation because it:
options: Reduces the total number of employees needed
Provides employees time and resources for non-core exploration
Increases short-term profitability
Eliminates the need for formal R&D departments
Curveball Exam 1.explanation: Slack = extra time/resources for exploration beyond core duties. Google's 20% time produced Gmail and Orkut. Companies facing financial pressure often cut slack for efficiency — GM resisted this by maintaining R&D in electric/fuel cell vehicles despite financial difficulties.

Curveball Exam 2.q: First mover advantage is MORE effective in industries where products are:
options: Cheap, frequently purchased, and commoditized
Expensive, durable, and infrequently purchased
Sold only to government agencies
Digital and easily copied
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: First movers benefit most when products are expensive, durable, and infrequently purchased — because customers won't repurchase soon, giving the first mover a longer window. Also more effective in advertising-intensive industries and where distributors must hold large stocks.

Curveball Exam 2.q: The 'opportunistic renegotiation problem' in contracting for complementary assets occurs when:
options: Both parties have equal bargaining power
The asset is generic and easily replaceable
One party exploits the other's dependence on a specialized investment
Government regulations change the terms of the contract
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: When assets are specialized (can't be redeployed cheaply), the supplier can exploit the innovator's dependence — 'hold-up problem.' The innovator is locked in and the supplier demands better terms. This is why Teece says firms must OWN specialized complementary assets, not contract for them.

Curveball Exam 2.q: The Bass model's key limitation is that it assumes:
options: All customers adopt simultaneously
Prices remain constant over time
Demand-side factors only — ignoring supply constraints
Technology never improves after launch
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: The Bass model is demand-side only — it ignores supply constraints like raw material availability, production capacity, and manufacturing bottlenecks. Other limitations: can't estimate at the very beginning, accuracy depends on correct market size (m), and declines with competing technologies or economic shocks.

Curveball Exam 2.q: The 4-phase lead user method involves a workshop with approximately how many lead users?
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: The lead user workshop (phase 4) includes 10-15 lead users to fill gaps in product concepts. Phase 1: identify markets/trends. Phase 2: select specific trend, plan interviews. Phase 3: interview lead users, gather needs. Phase 4: workshop. 3M used this with MASH unit surgeons and veterinary surgeons.

Curveball Exam 2.q: California is unique in US employment law because it:
options: Requires all employees to sign noncompete agreements
Cannot enforce noncompete agreements at all
Has the longest noncompete duration in the US
Requires federal court approval for all NDAs
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: California CANNOT enforce noncompete agreements — this is one reason Silicon Valley has such high employee mobility and rapid knowledge diffusion between firms. Other states allow noncompetes but they must be limited in time and geography.

Curveball Exam 2.q: Schumpeter's exact definition of creative destruction includes the phrase:
options: 'survival of the fittest in the marketplace'
'a process of industrial mutation that incessantly revolutionizes the economic structure from within'
'the invisible hand of market competition'
'technological progress through planned obsolescence'
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: Schumpeter: 'A process of industrial mutation...that incessantly revolutionizes the economic structure from within, incessantly destroying the old one, incessantly creating a new one.' (1945, p82). Key word: FROM WITHIN — not external destruction but internal transformation.

Curveball Exam 2.q: The 6th innovation wave (~2020) is characterized by:
options: Digital networks, software, new media
Petrochemicals, electronics, aviation
AI & IoT, robots & drones, clean tech
Electricity, chemicals, internal combustion
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: 6th wave (~2020, ~25 years): AI & IoT, robots & drones, clean tech. Previous waves: 5th (digital/software, 1990), 4th (petrochemicals/electronics, 1950), 3rd (electricity/chemicals, 1900), 2nd (steam/rail, 1845), 1st (water/textiles, 1785).

Curveball Exam 2.q: Firm performance is determined by the interaction of which three types of rents?
options: Patent rents, licensing rents, trademark rents
Schumpeterian rents, monopoly rents, Ricardian rents
Short-term rents, medium-term rents, long-term rents
Production rents, marketing rents, distribution rents
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: Three rent types: Schumpeterian (change-based: profit between innovation and diffusion), Monopoly (environment-based: industry position, barriers to entry), Ricardian (firm-specific: resources, capabilities, barriers to imitation). All three interact to determine firm performance.

Curveball Exam 2.q: Moderna CEO Bancel's principle for digitization, articulated by CDO Damiani, is:
options: 'Move fast and break things'
'Digital-first, analog-never'
'You cannot blindly translate manual processes into digital — processes must be redesigned'
'Automate everything, question nothing'
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: Damiani's principle: processes must be REDESIGNED for digital, not just digitized. The 2012 anecdote of a scientist entering nucleotide sequences in Excel horrified Bancel — one wrong cell could ruin an entire drug design. This led to the 'drug design studio' approach.

Curveball Exam 2.q: Which of the following is NOT a limitation of the Bass model?
options: Cannot estimate at the very beginning of a product launch
Accuracy depends on correctly estimating total market size
It overestimates adoption in supply-constrained markets
It accounts for competition from substitute technologies
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: The Bass model does NOT account for competing technologies — that's a limitation, not a feature. It assumes no competition. Other limitations: can't estimate at start (needs prior data), depends on correct market size (m), demand-side only (ignores supply), accuracy declines over time.

Curveball Exam 2.q: An advantage of being a late mover into a market is the ability to:
options: Set industry standards before competitors
Build brand recognition as the pioneer
Free ride on the first mover's R&D investments and correct their mistakes
Secure exclusive supplier contracts
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: Late movers can: free-ride on first mover's R&D, correct first mover's design mistakes, leapfrog with newer technology (first movers locked into older designs), benefit from knowledge spillovers (cost of innovation > cost of imitation), and time complementary technology better.

Curveball Exam 2.q: In Teece's appropriability framework, the decision to license vs. integrate depends on three factors. Which is NOT one of them?
options: Are patents/trade secrets effective?
Has the dominant design converged?
Are complementary assets specialized or generic?
Is the company publicly traded?
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: Teece's three decision axes: (1) Are patents/trade secrets effective? (2) Has dominant design converged? (3) Are complementary assets specialized or generic? Whether the company is publicly traded is irrelevant to the appropriability framework.

Curveball Exam 2.q: The first industrial research laboratories were established in:
options: 1776 by Adam Smith
1836 at MIT
1876 by Edison (Menlo Park) and Alexander Graham Bell
1920 at Bell Labs
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: The first industrial labs appeared in 1876: Edison's Menlo Park and Bell's laboratory. The concept spread to European pharma/dye labs in Germany (1880-1890). This marked the shift from individual inventors (80% had no formal training) to collective, institutional R&D.

Curveball Exam 2.q: OECD research found that after large firms acquire small startups, the typical result is:
options: Innovation accelerates dramatically
The startup's products are distributed more widely
Innovation often declines and products are discontinued
The acquiring firm becomes more entrepreneurial
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: OECD found firms often buy startups to eliminate competition before growth. Post-acquisition: innovation frequently declines and products are discontinued. Google averaged 1 acquisition/month (2000-2018), described culturally as 'Jimmy Got a Job at Google' — purchased firms stagnate in silos.

Curveball Exam 2.q: The science fiction novel that predicted electronic currencies, avatars, and VR headsets was:
options: Ready Player One (2011)
Fahrenheit 451 (1955)
Snow Crash (1992)
2001: A Space Odyssey (1968)
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: Snow Crash (1992) by Neal Stephenson predicted electronic currencies, avatars, online subcultures, and VR/AR headsets — and coined the term 'metaverse.' Other sci-fi predictions: wireless earbuds (Fahrenheit 451, 1955), folding phone (Star Trek, 1966), videoconferencing (2001, 1968).

Curveball Exam 2.q: In the VC pipeline, approximately how many firms seeking venture capital actually raise money?
options: 50 out of 4,000
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: Of 4,000 firms seeking VC: only 200 raise money (5%), and only 15 reach 1B NOK revenue. Combined with the skewness stat (0.4% of investments → 97% of returns), this shows how extremely selective and winner-take-all the VC industry is.

Curveball Exam 2.q: Angel investors differ from VCs in that angels have:
options: Defined fund lifecycles of 7-10 years
Minimum investments of $1M+
Infinite fund life and smaller check sizes (~$25,000)
Higher expected returns than VCs
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: Angels: infinite fund life (no forced exit timeline), small checks (~$25k minimum), earlier stage (pre-seed/seed), moderate returns (<9x). Funded ventures often have 15+ angels. VCs: defined fund life (7-10 years), larger checks, higher return targets (5-10x), 2% fee + 20-30% carry.

Curveball Exam 2.q: A VC term sheet typically includes all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Cap table showing ownership percentages
Employee stock pool allocation
Board structure (company vs. investor vs. neutral seats)
The company's 5-year marketing budget
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: Term sheet components: cap table (who owns what %), employee pool (stock for employees), board structure (company vs. investor vs. neutral seats), preferred payout (order of investment payouts). A 5-year marketing budget is an operational document, not a term sheet item.

Curveball Exam 2.q: The era of VC 'democratization' (micro VC boom, founder-friendly terms) began approximately in:
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: VC history: 1960 (cottage industry), 1980 (capital boom/ERISA Act), 2000 (dot com bust), 2004 (capital + service era), 2012 (democratization: micro VC, founder-friendly), 2018+ (adapting: bigger funds, diversity, data-driven).

Curveball Exam 2.q: Requiring R&D laboratories to use the SAME facilities is an example of a procedure that encourages links between research and development.
options: True
False
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: True. Shared equipment/materials is one of the four R&D linking policies. Note the trap: the mock exam asked about DIFFERENT facilities, which is False. SAME facilities = True (encourages linking). DIFFERENT facilities = False (hinders linking).

Curveball Exam 2.q: Knowledge spillovers are particularly problematic for innovators because:
options: The cost of innovation exceeds the cost of imitation
Government regulations require sharing research results
Customers always prefer the original inventor's product
Spillovers only affect small firms
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: The cost of innovating is higher than the cost of imitating — so competitors can hire your people and copy your knowledge at lower cost than it took you to create it. This is the fundamental knowledge spillover problem and one reason why late movers can succeed.

Curveball Exam 2.q: In Moderna's digital pyramid, the layers from bottom to top are:
options: AI → Analytics → Automation → Integration → Cloud
Cloud → Integration → Automation & Robotics → Analytics → AI
Data → Processing → Output → Feedback → Learning
Hardware → Software → Network → Applications → Users
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: Moderna's digital stack bottom to top: Cloud (AWS since 2013) → Integration (data harmonized, IoT) → Automation & Robotics (islands connected) → Analytics → AI. By spring 2020: ~20 AI algorithms in production, ~20 in development, MVP AI model built in avg 6 months.

Curveball Exam 2.q: Codified knowledge differs from tacit knowledge in that codified knowledge:
options: Can only be transferred through personal demonstration
Spreads slowly through apprenticeship
Can be written down and transferred instantly across distance
Lives only in people's heads and is learned by doing
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: Codified = written in blueprints/formulas, transfers instantly, spreads fast across distance. Tacit = lives in people's heads, learned by doing, spreads slowly (e.g., plant manager's assembly line intuition, salesperson's pitch timing). This distinction matters for choosing patents vs. secrecy.

Curveball Exam 2.q: R&D intensity is much higher for small firms than for large ones.
options: True
False
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: True. Small firms spend a HIGHER percentage of revenue on R&D (higher intensity) and produce innovations at lower R&D cost. However, large firms spend more in absolute terms and can hire more talented/diverse employees. Small firms also have higher innovation rates per employee.

Curveball Exam 2.q: The 'hipster indicator' of creative destruction suggests that:
options: Hipsters are the most innovative consumer segment
Products hipsters like were once mainstream and disrupted by newer alternatives
Hipster businesses have the highest failure rate
Creative destruction only affects artisanal products
Curveball Exam 2.explanation: From lectures: 'If hipsters like it, it was likely at some point the victim of creative destruction.' Examples: vinyl records, typewriters, mechanical watches, cargo bikes, brick buildings. These products were displaced by newer alternatives but maintained niche appeal.
